item.title: Short Talk: Latent Self-View
item.description:
AudioDharma talk by Matthew Brensilver.
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25498
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary: AudioDharma talk by Matthew Brensilver. Source: AudioDharma.

item.title: Tranquility
item.description:
What if tranquility is not flatness, but the condition that lets the mind actually see? The talk begins with a memorable line about the Dharma being “parasympathetically biased” and follows that thread into a case for steadiness, patience, and bodily calm as supports for truth rather than signs of emotional narrowing. It then turns practical, naming the ways hyperarousal, clinging, and compulsivity build stories, and offering low-friction ways to settle: music, smell, nature, touch, self-distanced speech, and simple labeling.
Chapters: 00:09 A Wilder Friend 01:23 Parasympathetic Dharma 03:34 Why Tranquility Matters 05:03 Feeling Into Story 07:16 Tension and Selfing 10:24 Practicing Refuge 11:37 Low-Effort Regulation 13:52 Small Soothing Supports
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25456

item.title: Daily Life Practice, Love, and the World (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if daily practice is less about becoming more controlled and more about learning to melt? The talk moves from a story about blessing others in meditation into a sustained reflection on love, kindness, and the subtle ways practice reshapes a life. Through images of ice cubes, spiritual friendship, and a sutta about light that does not need to land, it keeps asking what it means to care without clinging to views, outcomes, or the need to know.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Blessing 01:45 Mindfulness and Love 05:46 The Frozen Self 09:18 Views and Letting Go 14:25 Moral Force 18:37 Warm Hand to Warm Hand 23:37 Love and Sila 30:34 World, Loss, and Solidarity 36:47 Light That Does Not Land
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/97003/?access_key=RMLI47PRFc

item.title: Dharma Talk (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when the mind stops treating silence as absence and starts hearing how much of life is driven by noise, craving, and fear? The talk moves from a playful social-science example about conversations ending to a sustained inquiry into silence as a living practice: the noise of samsara, the attraction and danger of stillness, and the way attention shapes what becomes our world. Along the way, the speaker uses Ajahn Chah, William James, the Buddha, and a reading on Jorie Graham to show how quiet can become both more intimate and more revealing, until insight begins to emerge from it.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening with conversation 02:47 Noise and samsara 05:33 Silence as refuge 11:24 Not bothering the sound 13:45 What samadhi means 20:26 Attention shapes world 26:43 Aiming and sustaining 31:42 Fear and meaning-making 39:35 Hearing silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/player/96948.html?access_key=RMLI47PRFc

item.title: The Why of Practice: Three Possible Answers (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Why practice at all when the mind keeps turning toward pleasure, fear, and control? The talk begins with the reality of difficult meditation experiences, then moves into a gentler question: why come here in the first place, and what makes the path worth staying with when retreat amplifies whatever is already in motion. From there, the speaker unfolds three possible answers—appreciating self and world, improving self and world, and transcending self and world—showing how each one changes the way suffering, gratitude, and letting go are understood.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Research and retreat discomfort 02:06 Why we practice 06:55 Dukkha and no escape 12:06 Three possible whys 20:04 Acceptance and growth 26:02 Care in the world 31:34 Letting the world go 39:22 Closing reflections
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/96938/?access_key=RMLI47PRFc

item.title: Dharmette: Insight & Love
item.description:
What if insight and love are not separate aims, but two sides of the same seeing? The talk follows that question through impermanence, suffering, and not-self, arguing that when these are felt vividly rather than merely understood, craving loosens and care grows. It moves from a classic description of insight practice into a quieter claim: letting go can soften the heart, make ownership less convincing, and leave understanding and love reinforcing one another.
Chapters: 00:17 Insight and love 02:01 Wisdom from ordinariness 03:27 Gunaratana on insight 04:55 Heartbreak before freedom 06:23 Dukkha softens the heart 08:00 Facing impermanence 10:38 Letting self relax 13:34 Empty center, open heart
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25381

item.title: Dharmette: Very brief overview of Shinzen Young’s approach
item.description:
How does a mindfulness practice become systematic without becoming rigid? The talk gives a very brief sketch of Shinzen Young’s approach: mindfulness as three working skills — concentration, sensory clarity, and equanimity — and then follows that map into three reasons to practice, three sensory domains, and three basic methods. It moves from definition to application, showing how attention can be trained to notice more, let experience come and go, and use practice either by noting, by doing nothing, or by nurturing what is positive.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the sketch 01:24 What mindfulness means 02:13 Three skills together 03:50 The three skills unpacked 07:36 Why practice 10:22 Where to practice 12:23 Three practice modes 15:20 Closing invitation
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25294

item.title: Dharmette: Ajahn Chah's Advice to Woman on Deathbed
item.description:
What can actually help when the mind is stuck between helplessness, anger, and the wish for consolation? The talk turns to a remembered teaching from Ajahn Chah, spoken to a dying student’s mother, and uses it to explore letting go, seeing body and mind as not-self, and taking awareness and the breath as refuge. After reading the passage, the speaker reflects on its limits too: the words do not answer everything, but they can still steady the heart and point toward a kind of love that no longer tries to control outcomes.
Chapters: 00:11 Searching for a talk 02:53 Turning to a master 03:57 Letting body and world go 06:06 Awareness as refuge 07:57 No self, no attachment 09:52 A gift of Dhamma 11:41 What words cannot do
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25235

item.title: Working with Self-Hatred
item.description:
What if self-hatred is doing something for you, even while it hurts? The talk begins by asking what self-compassion looks like when familiar methods don’t land, then turns to a harder investigation: how self-harshness can soothe, protect, or feel less painful than facing limitation and dukkha directly. From there it moves toward lingering with suffering, trusting that suffering can support faith, and noticing that deep conditioning usually shifts through gentleness, inner communication, and a change in direction rather than a fight.
Chapters: 00:12 The question of self-hate 01:23 Finding what fits 02:23 What self-hatred protects 04:01 Ordinariness and suffering 05:53 Faith through suffering 08:02 No spiritual battle 10:12 Beyond the self game 12:15 Retreat, mortality, and love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25131

item.title: A Meaningful Dharma Life
item.description:
What if the point of practice is not only less suffering, but a life that feels more meaningful? The talk moves from the mind’s constant habit of making meaning out of emotion, gossip, and cultural status to a quieter claim: Dharma practice asks us to let go of compulsive significance, then build a deeper meaning through wise action, love, and non-regret. Along the way, the speaker draws on a recent research paper on meaning, a brief story about overheard office gossip, and reflections on nihilism, vow, and lineage.
Chapters: 00:22 The promise of practice 01:18 Meaning and its making 03:31 What meaning links 05:04 A personal turn 05:59 Drama, culture, and emptiness 08:58 Meaning from within 10:29 Practice and purpose 12:23 Vow and non-regret 15:04 Lineage and consolation
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25073

item.title: Dharmette: Meditation is so many things
item.description:
Meditation is approached here as more than attention training: the talk keeps widening the frame to include rest, steadiness, gratitude, suffering, goodness, and letting go. It moves from the problem of reductionism to a practical claim that there are “many ways to win,” and that practice can meet whatever is arising on a given day. Along the way, it briefly turns to Samantha Harvey’s Orbital for a view of gratitude from finitude, and later to Yuval Harari and Agnes Callard to deepen the questions of suffering, mortality, and what it would mean to feel life is complete.
Chapters: 00:12 Meditation has many facets 01:57 Deep rest and wakefulness 03:18 Peace, pain, gratitude 05:31 Earth from space 06:50 Knowing ourselves clearly 08:21 Suffering and goodness 10:26 Samadhi and letting go 12:23 Past, mortality, completion
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25029

item.title: Dharmette: Understanding, Forgiveness & Unconsciousness
item.description:
How do you forgive a past action you still cannot understand? The talk begins with a listener’s anguish about having caused harm and not being able to make a coherent story of why it happened, then moves into the limits of self-knowledge, remorse, and the difference between understanding and simply bearing witness. From there it develops into a quiet argument that shame can soften when the self is seen as divided, opaque, and often surprised by its own unconscious motives, with help from Freud and Adam Phillips. It closes by asking whether we can grieve the lives we didn’t live, renounce without disowning, and still dedicate even heartbreak to love.
Chapters: 00:12 The Question of Remorse 02:09 Witnessing Without Explanation 04:04 Seeking Forgiveness 05:55 Reasons and Causes 08:34 The Unconscious at Work 09:44 Divided Selves 12:04 Pleasure and Renunciation 13:34 Love and Heartbreak
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24994

item.title: Self, Self-Love, Selflessness
item.description:
What if the way into not-self begins with a stable enough sense of self? The talk starts from the claim that insight should be simple, then moves into the tension that real practice is not one teaching but a dialectic: stability and adaptability, self and selflessness, acceptance and change. Along the way, it uses retreat routine, neuroscience, and reflections on self-love to show why a mind needs some predictability before it can safely loosen identification and let the self become less fragile.
Chapters: 00:09 Simplicity and complexity 01:16 The dharma as dialectic 04:00 Stability and disorientation 08:18 What self means 13:21 Learning readiness 20:47 Brain balance and retreat 25:21 A self that can hold 30:13 Self-love and ownership 37:28 Unbinding and release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25024

item.title: Dharmette: Question on Social Anxiety
item.description:
What if social anxiety is less a problem to fix than a place to meet with complete non-resistance? The talk begins with the wish to be more confident and self-loving, then moves through acceptance, undefended suffering, and the fear of being seen or objectified by others. From there, it turns practical: stay with the most painful story, let its charge drain, and notice how shame, self-judgment, and the feeling of being watched shape the body and mind.
Chapters: 00:12 Acceptance First 01:57 Feeling the Pain 03:43 Why Others Matter 05:52 Judgment And Objectification 09:13 Bathe In The Thought 10:53 Jump Before Ready 12:30 Shame And Not-Self
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24944

item.title: Dharmette: How We Incarnate in Different Contexts
item.description:
What changes when the same person moves from family, work, sangha, or meditation into a different role? The talk starts from an Ajahn Chah aspiration verse, especially the line “in every kind of birth,” and uses it to ask how we take on different selves from moment to moment. From there it turns toward sankharas as subtle formative energies, showing how identity, emotion, and responsiveness can tighten or soften depending on context, and ending with a simple homework-style inquiry into what each setting reveals and conceals.
Chapters: 00:12 Ajahn Chah's Line 01:18 Identity and Variability 02:52 Roles of Daily Life 04:42 Foucault and Selfhood 05:30 Formative Energies 07:04 When Arousal Changes 08:51 Interrogating the Implicit 11:01 What Dharma Recalls 13:22 Homework in Awareness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24839

item.title: Dharmette: Tranquility, View, Truth
item.description:
If truth is tied to freedom, what state helps us actually see? The talk follows that question into the body: how mood, arousal, and balance shape perception, and why a settled mind may reveal more than a charged one. It then turns to tranquility and samadhi as a kind of quiet enoughness that narrows overwhelm, slows the world down, and makes clearer seeing possible without pretending everything is fine. From there, the speaker asks how to test whether that calmer view is really wise, and suggests trusting experience while refining the view.
Chapters: 00:35 Truth and freedom 01:35 The body of knowing 02:37 Heart-mind investigation 03:44 Mood shapes view 06:02 Subtle pleasantness 07:47 The flood of life 09:30 Samadhi and enoughness 11:49 What tranquility sees 13:56 Testing the view
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24761

item.title: Dharmette: Orienting to Goodness
item.description:
How do you stay oriented to goodness when suffering, hatred, and political breakdown seem to make goodness feel naïve or out of reach? The talk begins with that tension and argues that goodness is not a luxury but something the mind and world need for stability, protection, and moral clarity. A brief reflection on a peace walk and a reading about epistemic trust in democracy widen the frame, showing how love can soften hardened identity, make room for solidarity, and help people step out of defensive self-story.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 00:30 Goodness and Forgetting 01:58 Why Hatred Matters 03:47 Protecting the Mind 06:31 Compassion as Necessity 07:38 Democracy and Trust 10:33 Love Softens Identity 13:33 Return to Goodness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24706

item.title: Orienting and ruling out the alternatives to love
item.description:
What if the main alternatives to love are not villains to defeat, but habits to understand: ill will that feels charged with purpose, and apathy that comes from being too full? The talk opens by contrasting online distance with the fuller engagement of sitting together, then moves into a practical inquiry about how loving-kindness can be sustained without tipping into strain, detachment, or burnout. From there it becomes a quiet guided practice, asking what the heart needs right now and what kind of love is possible in this moment—sometimes tenderness, sometimes equanimity, sometimes simple rest.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 00:56 Engagement and presence 04:45 Why the day matters 08:06 Entering the Dharma field 12:31 Love and its alternatives 15:00 Understanding hate and apathy 20:50 Rest without apathy 25:01 Guided settling practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24614

item.title: Love, Grief and Inconsolability
item.description:
What if fear is what keeps love from being free? The talk begins with mortality and a deathbed teaching from Ajahn Chah, then moves into a harder question: whether Dharma can fully console grief, or whether some grief remains as an irreducible part of love itself. From there it opens into reflections on secular suffering, the limits of medicine and practice, and a final image of love as light that may not need to land anywhere to still illuminate.
Chapters: 00:00 Fear and death 01:33 Ajahn Chah's teaching 07:04 Freedom and theology 09:07 The Dharma as medicine 13:25 What grief meets 17:04 Emotion and history 20:24 Love, fear, mortality 24:26 Limits of care 27:07 Light that does not land
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24619

item.title: Functions of heart practice: cultivation, concentration, purification
item.description:
How do heart practices work when love does not show up on command? The talk moves through metta as something improvisational rather than performative, arguing that the practice may show up as loving-kindness, compassion, joy, equanimity, gratitude, or even simple words that help the mind gather and settle. It also describes three functions of the practice—cultivation, concentration, and purification—showing how heart practice can lower resistance, bring grief to the surface, and gradually loosen the grip of self. The guided portion then asks listeners to settle into the body, sense what is needed, and let time be an ally while the heart finds its own native form of care.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 02:57 Three functions 06:21 Cultivation and focus 11:06 Open space of self 12:00 Settling into practice 24:16 Words and reverberation 31:35 Purification and patience 33:46 Goodwill and Sangha
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24615

item.title: Anatta as a cause and effect of freedom
item.description:
What if insight into not-self is not a single cause of freedom, but one part of a wider, more gradual unfolding? The talk begins by asking about causality itself—what leads to suffering and what leads to peace—then turns to the question of whether anatta produces freedom, or whether freedom and not-self arise together through the path. From there it moves through selfing, shame, compassion, ethics, and humility, showing how practice can slowly loosen the grip of the self-story without turning the path into an all-or-nothing test of enlightenment.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the question 01:17 Causality and conditions 03:55 Anatta and freedom 07:09 De-centering self 09:50 Ethics and love 11:20 Humility on the path 13:31 Healing and fluidity 15:24 Holistic conclusion
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24555

item.title: Spiritual vs Psychological Health
item.description:
What is the relationship between psychological health and spiritual health? The talk moves through four possible answers: that they are separate, continuous, sequential, or deeply threaded together, and keeps returning to the practical question of what actually changes in ordinary life when practice deepens. Along the way, the speaker brings in psychotherapy, self-love, disidentification, and the limits of language to show why the issue is not just theoretical but bound up with suffering, healing, and freedom. The result is a careful argument for staying honest with both the psychological knots and the larger movement of Dharma.
Chapters: 00:11 Framing the question 01:18 Four possible views 04:46 Language and the absolute 07:01 Dharma as healing 11:13 Where psychology ends 14:59 Preconditions for practice 18:36 Threaded together 25:11 Self-love and not-self 30:20 Working with deep knots
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24512

item.title: Wise Intention
item.description:
What does wise intention look like when it is not another project of the self? The talk begins with meditation instruction—balancing relaxation and alertness, gathering scattered attention around the breath—and then opens into a longer inquiry about sincerity, habit, and the quiet difference between efforting and willingness. From there, the speaker turns to the Buddha’s three wise intentions—renunciation, loving-kindness, and harmlessness—and asks how practice can become less transactional, less compulsive, and more receptive to being changed. A dialogue at the end brings the questions down to earth, especially around intention, outcome, recovery, and what it means to keep choosing the path over time.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture 07:53 Attending with gentleness 15:21 Breath as the anchor 23:06 Sincerity over striving 38:29 Habits and values 52:36 Intention and behavior 01:05:51 Three wise intentions 01:15:26 Questions on consequences 01:21:29 Willingness and metta
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/94445/

item.title: TS Eliot and the Mystical Dimension of Buddhism
item.description:
What if the way into awakening runs not through more certainty, but through not knowing, dispossession, and the loosening of egoic control? The talk opens with William James on alternate forms of consciousness, then distinguishes real mystical experience from the temptation to fetishize it, insisting that an experience is not yet a path. From there it turns to T.S. Eliot’s lines about arriving “where you are” by way of ignorance and no ecstasy, using them to trace a Dharma movement through renunciation, non-ownership, and a more equanimous relation to views.
Chapters: 00:11 William James and consciousness 01:56 Mysticism without fetish 03:21 Renunciation and ego 05:39 Eliot’s way of ignorance 07:22 The dry terrain 09:05 Keeping balance in not-knowing 12:20 The practice of don't know 14:09 Non-attachment and views 15:53 Refrain and close
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24449

item.title: Dharmette: Very Condensed Overview of the Path
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple problem definition: we think we want the world, but what we actually want is peace. From there, it traces a compact map of the path, linking diagnosis and medicine through dukkha, anicca, and anatta, then showing how renunciation, metta, and ethical care support a quieter life. It also makes room for the way practice works in cycles: first forming stable foundations and then loosening the grip on the solid stories and sensations that make suffering feel fixed. The result is a very brief overview that keeps returning to the same question: what kind of training actually helps us let go?
Chapters: 00:11 Why a map matters 01:32 Naming the real problem 03:04 Diagnosis as medicine 04:33 Craving and suffering 06:03 The raft of practice 08:11 Wise view and memory 10:18 Holding forms lightly 11:37 De-reifying experience 13:09 Love at the end
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24400

item.title: Spiritual Friendship (Berkeley Satellite Daylong)
item.description:
How does friendship shape the mind all the way down to practice, honesty, and a sense of reality? The talk begins with the claim that we are socially permeable, then moves through belonging, dependency, and the need for reminders before turning to what spiritual friendship actually feels like: truthful, forgiving, and able to make room for play. Near the end, the focus settles on a quieter question—how being known, and helping others feel real, can soften self-consciousness and bring a more ordinary, alive sense of goodness into view.
Chapters: 00:09 The mind is permeable 01:52 Friendship as support 03:58 Belonging and dependency 07:05 Isolation and remoteness 10:39 What spiritual friendship is 13:55 Truthfulness and sanity 16:02 Being known clearly 40:13 Play and creativity 43:59 Feeling real
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24379

item.title: Dharmette: Rivers are just rivers
item.description:
What changes when the world stops feeling solid, but ordinary life is still here? The talk uses the Zen line about mountains and rivers—first as mountains and rivers, then not, then again as mountains and rivers—to trace a path from unquestioned certainty, through destabilizing insight, toward a more mature ordinariness. Along the way it draws on meditation practice, a bit of neuroscience, and references to Dhammapada, Suzuki Roshi, and Shinzen Young to show how intimacy, not escape, becomes the endpoint.
Chapters: 00:52 Opening the Theme 02:05 Ordinary Certainty 03:56 Looking Inward 05:32 Meditation Reverses Habit 07:36 Fluid Perception 09:28 The Weird Phase 12:07 New Baselines 14:35 Love Remains
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24242

item.title: The What and How of Investigation
item.description:
The talk begins with a familiar practice dilemma: when should investigation turn toward something, and when should it be left alone in the background? From there, it argues that the deepest move is not solving the mind’s questions but loosening the need to know, since sincerity matters more than a perfect algorithm for practice. The speaker then reframes investigation as an attentional practice—one that asks for patience, non-collection, and the willingness to let insight arrive without forcing a story too soon.
Chapters: 00:16 The Question of Practice 01:25 Letting the Question Dissolve 02:25 Answers and Letting Go 04:56 Sticky Thought 06:50 Attention, Not Answers 08:41 Relaxing the Need to Know 10:57 Dhammavichaya 13:27 Path and Goal
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24176

item.title: Dharmette: Empathic Resonance
item.description:
What does it mean to feel another person’s experience without losing the line between self and other? This talk moves from mindfulness as a kind of self-directed empathy into a careful account of empathic resonance, mentalizing, and the humility required to stay open without projecting our own stories onto someone else. Along the way, the speaker leans on Carl Rogers, Tanya Singer, and Krishnamurti to show why empathy needs both attunement and restraint. It ends by returning to the practical problem of when closeness becomes intrusive, and how compassion can remain steady there.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and context 00:40 Knowing our own mind 02:34 Three parts of empathy 04:53 Knowing and not knowing 06:40 Reverence for difference 09:17 Calibration and boundaries 12:18 The as-if condition 14:23 Love and closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24141

item.title: Dharmette: attention, awareness, mindfulness
item.description:
What is the difference between attention, awareness, and mindfulness—and how do we tell when practice has become tightening rather than letting go? The talk moves from a practical distinction between pointing attention at an object and holding a broader receptive awareness, then turns to how craving and control can hide inside even sincere concentration. Along the way, it uses the image of a spotlight, notes a scientific three-part model of attention, and keeps returning to the question of whether the mind is relaxed enough to see clearly.
Chapters: 00:09 Opening on mindfulness 00:32 Attention and receptivity 01:50 What attention does 03:40 Three kinds of attention 05:26 Object focus and its limits 07:01 Craving in the background 09:11 Awareness makes mindfulness 12:04 Seeing craving clearly 13:32 Freedom without strategy
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24107

item.title: Living Alongside Questions (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if practice means learning to live without freezing into final answers? The talk moves from impermanence and the mind’s hunger for certainty into a closer look at how views harden into greed, aversion, and fear, then opens into two recurring koans: did I love well, and why do I keep doing this. From there it becomes an extended inquiry into love, understanding, equanimity, and the stubborn patterns that keep reappearing even in long-term practice.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Settling into the room 01:34 The illusion of permanence 05:15 Impermanence and uncertainty 07:30 Views and certainty 10:11 A life with questions 12:29 Two koans of practice 16:45 Love as understanding 22:45 The circle of concern 27:22 Compassion and equanimity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93338/

item.title: Reflections on Time in Dharma: Surrendering to Time, Healing Time, Not-Making Time (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if the pressure to fill time is itself the problem? The talk begins with the sense that there is either too much time or not enough, and moves through boredom, grief, retreat structure, and the craving for “what’s next” toward a quieter question: how do we stop being pushed around by time and learn a wiser relationship to it? Along the way, the speaker uses references to Zadie Smith, Catherine Schultz, Shinzen Young, Bhikkhu Bodhi, and others to show how practice can slow experience down, reveal impermanence more directly, and soften the way memory and self get bound to the future.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening: time as the problem 02:51 Busyness and boredom 08:36 Grief widens the view 10:49 Retreat and the schedule 15:17 Let time work 22:38 Impermanence in real time 26:14 Presence and the past 33:16 Ghosts into ancestors 38:21 Beyond self and time
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93302/

item.title: Dharmette: PR Campaign for Non-Hatred
item.description:
How can the Dharma matter publicly without pretending to solve everything? The talk starts from that political and personal uncertainty, then tests a deliberately modest answer: a campaign for non-hatred, not as a demand for love but as a practical way to interrupt the corrosiveness of anger. From there it moves through examples, a Buddhist teaching on hatred ending through non-hatred, and a final turn to William Stafford’s poem on mutual recognition and clear signals in dark times.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Context 00:59 Dharma And Public Reach 03:49 Choosing Non-Hatred 05:19 The Cost Of Hatred 09:03 Non-Hatred In Practice 10:55 Guard The Senses 12:44 Stafford's Warning
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24005

item.title: Dharmette: 'Protect Empathy at All Costs and Live Groovy Lives'
item.description:
What does it mean to protect empathy in a world that keeps pulling the self to the center? The talk begins with an interview about art and politics, then moves into a story about Joe Strummer’s charge to “protect empathy at all costs and to live groovy lives,” which becomes the thread running through the rest of the talk. From there, the speaker folds Dharma, sangha, and self-understanding into a practice of widening concern—starting with one’s own heart and expanding toward others.
Chapters: 00:12 Art Opens the Heart 01:46 Strummer's Charge 03:08 Dharma as Art 04:17 Beauty and Sangha 05:40 Abiding in What We Love 07:31 Expanding the Circle 09:40 Seeing Others Clearly 11:44 A Lineage of Care
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23938

item.title: Dharma Practice and the Cultivation of Power
item.description:
How can a path that begins in vulnerability, helplessness, and the limits of love also be a cultivation of power? The talk moves from the recognition that we cannot engineer awakening or reach all suffering with our love, into a different kind of strength: the courage to face truth, pain, causality, and death without distortion. Along the way, it uses Nietzsche and a brief echo of the famous courtroom line from A Few Good Men to ask how much truth we can actually bear, then argues that Dharma makes us less reactive, more independent, and less dependent on conditions for happiness.
Chapters: 00:12 Softening and helplessness 02:55 A different kind of power 05:00 Truth requires strength 06:56 Causality and relinquishment 09:01 Independent in the Dharma 10:27 Refusing cultural confusion 12:23 Power and trust
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23888

item.title: The Circuitous Journey to Presence through Past and Future
item.description:
How do you get to presence when the past still feels unfinished and the future still feels threatening? The talk works through that question by arguing that “be present” is not a command to force the mind into place, but the result of gradual training: memory losing some of its thorns, fear loosening its grip, and time itself becoming less something to fill than something to release. Along the way, the speaker moves from humorous self-doubt and retreat practice to grief, shame, self-view, and the craving to become, using each turn to show why presence is often circuitous rather than direct.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 03:43 Presence and the Past 09:59 Change and Training 13:38 Past as Refuge and Burden 20:51 Listening to Sorrow 26:30 Making Memory Legible 32:19 The Urge to Fill Time 39:22 Futurelessness and Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93051/

item.title: Active and Receptive Dimensions of Practice
item.description:
How do we practice without turning effort into another form of grasping? The talk moves through the tension between activity and receptivity, showing how attention, patience, and renunciation can be active cultivations while still making room for letting go. Along the way, it draws on the Middle Path, William James, Thich Nhat Hanh, and Ajahn Sucitto to argue that healing is not something the will can engineer, but something we learn to receive.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:49 Middle Path Framing 04:01 Activity and Receptivity 07:39 Letting Go Gradually 09:55 Training Attention 15:34 Patience With the Path 21:45 Renunciation and Enough 27:14 What We Cannot Do 30:30 Limits of Willfulness 39:54 Receiving the Blessings
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93049/

item.title: Attention Models the Object, Mindfulness Models the Attention
item.description:
What happens when practice stops being about improving the moment and becomes a study of how attention itself shapes experience? The talk begins with the plain struggle of retreat—hindrances, fatigue, restlessness, doubt—and gradually turns toward a sharper distinction between attention and mindfulness, drawing on the Satipatthana Sutta and the Dhammapada to show that perception is constructed through mind. From there it moves into a close look at mind-wandering, self-models, and the hidden agendas of control, ending with a quiet insistence on allowing craving, aversion, pleasantness, and unpleasantness without trying to force them away.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and retreat frame 01:51 The first-day hindrances 05:59 Mindfulness in the sutta 08:03 Mind-made experience 12:28 Mind-wandering and self-models 24:08 What attention does 29:40 Control versus connection 34:37 Allowing instead of improving
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93047/

item.title: Dharmette: Opportunities and Complexities of Speaking about Awakening
item.description:
What happens when a Dharma talk names awakening directly, and what gets lost when it doesn’t? The talk moves from a light story about a nephew’s brutal pep talk into a careful argument for speaking openly about liberation as the heart of practice, while also checking the risks: complacency, self-view, and turning awakening into another self-improvement project. Along the way, it weighs group teaching against one-on-one conversation, brings in a Majjhima Nikaya passage, and ends by insisting that urgency and patience can belong together.
Chapters: 00:18 A Pep Talk Gone Wrong 01:13 Freedom Beyond Conditions 02:49 The Buddha's Straight Talk 04:05 Group Context Matters 05:20 Psychological Shifts 07:01 Urgency and Patience 08:44 Many Fruits of Practice 10:39 The Self's Difficulties 13:12 Complicated Cause and Effect
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23819

item.title: Dharmette: Uprooting vs Allowing
item.description:
When should the mind be left alone, and when should it be actively redirected? The talk answers by refusing a fixed hierarchy: it moves from not fetishizing technique, to the role of prevention and abandonment in training, to cases where a direct turn toward a wholesome object really is needed. It then distinguishes equanimity from subtle aversion, and finally shows how listening closely can reveal the clinging underneath jealousy, anger, or fear so that the real work can be done.
Chapters: 00:12 The question of technique 01:43 Don't fetishize technique 02:30 Training before crisis 04:23 Equanimity and aversion 05:58 Marinating versus being with 07:49 Active redirection 10:12 Listen for the signal 12:36 Self, fear, and peace
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23722

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (5of5); The Parami of Energy
item.description:
What if the energy needed for practice is not willpower, but a kind of willingness that gradually loosens the grip of self? The talk moves from the path of least resistance, through the difficulty of seeing conditioned life clearly, into a practical look at how energy rises and falls in retreat and ordinary practice. It then turns to the Tibetan four thoughts—precious human life, impermanence, karma, and the defects of samsara—as ways to rally the mind without glorifying striving.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening on effort 02:02 Hard to see the Dhamma 04:17 Against default programming 05:52 When energy rises and falls 07:39 Four reflections 10:30 Karma and samsara 11:57 Effort without self 13:11 Closing blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23688

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (4of5); The Parami of Metta
item.description:
What if metta is not a soft feeling, but the strength that lets us face things as they are? The talk pushes back against sentimental ideas of love and instead treats lovingkindness as something woven through wisdom, courage, and the ability to stay open when life is difficult. Along the way, it uses memorable images for the brahmaviharas—love as willingness, compassion as protection, sympathetic joy as swimming, equanimity as a bird hovering—before ending with the claim that love is not an optional ideal but a necessity of our nervous system.
Chapters: 00:11 Opening the Theme 00:48 Why Love Needs Defending 01:39 Not Hallmark Love 03:57 Love and Wisdom Together 05:30 What Love Sustains 07:04 Training Against Hatred 09:39 The Brahmavihāras 11:25 Metta As Necessity 14:05 Closing Blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23674

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (3of5); Parami of Wisdom
item.description:
What if Dharma practice begins not with wisdom, but with raw data: the felt texture of breathing, clinging, change, and the mind’s surprising habits? The talk moves from Toni Morrison’s progression of data, information, knowledge, and wisdom to a close look at how experience becomes pattern, story, and finally a more useful understanding of suffering and its causes. Along the way, it brings in Thomas Nagel, Ajahn Sumedho, Jennifer Egan, the Four Noble Truths, and research on how people mispredict happiness, all in service of one question: can we learn to predict what actually leads to flourishing?
Chapters: 00:12 Data to wisdom 01:38 Direct experience 03:35 Seeing the self clearly 05:00 Shocking mind-data 06:10 Patterns and story 08:03 Fluid dharma stories 09:49 Wisdom and causality 12:22 Predicting happiness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23663

item.title: Dharmette: NIsargadatta, Nothing, Everything, Wisdom, Love
item.description:
What happens when practice keeps moving between “I am nothing” and “I am everything”? The talk opens with a Nisargadatta quote and uses it to explore a precise, experiential path through emptiness, selfing, and the softening of feeling, control, and urgency. From there it turns toward a different kind of love: not sentimental or sought after, but what becomes possible when the sense of ownership loosens and others’ pain is felt as one’s own. It closes with a Shinzen Young passage that gathers the talk into a dialectic of dissolving into nothingness and noticing subtler forms of solidity.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening quote 01:26 Two poles of practice 02:38 Precise experience 04:11 Phenomena dissolving 06:21 Self becomes space 08:03 Desert of the real 09:59 Love after emptiness 12:38 Seeking settles 13:38 Shinzen's summary
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23668

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (2of5); The Parami of Patience
item.description:
Patience begins not as calm but as the willingness to stay with what is hard without immediately sorting it into a story. After a sweat lodge anecdote and a memory of watching Thich Nhat Hanh walk, the talk turns to how patience can steady the heart, make suffering more bearable, and reveal why practice is gradual rather than instant. It then widens into the difference between passing states and lasting traits, and into the slow work of coming to love meditation, letting go, and new ways of living.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and framing 00:38 The hard path 01:22 Thich Nhat Hanh's example 03:23 Patience and suffering 05:52 Gradual cultivation 07:03 Holding the heart still 08:07 States become traits 11:58 Learning new love 15:29 Closing blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23657

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (1 of 5): The Parami of Truthfulness
item.description:
What does it mean to honor truth when so much of life—personal, political, and spiritual—pulls toward self-deception? The talk moves from the Parami as a set of virtues to a searching reflection on honesty as a foundation of the path, drawing on childhood anger at hypocrisy, the pain of hidden reality, and the ethical cost of lying. It then turns practical: noticing our own bad faith, listening to others, and finding a Sangha where truth does not have to be pretended away.
Chapters: 00:24 The parami of truth 01:24 Truth and rigor 02:02 Childhood aversion to lies 03:37 Helping reality make sense 05:17 Why lies harm 06:57 Bullshit and indifference 08:14 Political rage and self-righteousness 10:00 Practicing veracity 12:25 Sangha and strength
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23705

item.title: Reflections on Freedom
item.description:
What if freedom is less about becoming someone better and more about letting the pressure to become drop away? The talk begins with a guided sit and slowly opens into a sustained reflection on relaxation, equanimity, and the way practice softens the compulsion to control experience. From there it moves toward a practical question: how freedom shows up in ordinary life as less friction, more love, and a more complete sense of being at home here.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling Into Practice 18:41 Relaxation and Permission 21:07 Release in Experience 28:13 Equanimity and Love 57:34 No Progress To Force 01:00:50 Freedom Feels Personal 01:15:42 Satisfaction And Happiness 01:24:27 Coming Home
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/92201/

item.title: The Circuitous Journey to Presence through Past and Future
item.description:
If the present feels hard to enter, the talk starts there: the speaker questions the familiar instruction to “be present” and argues that presence is less a command than the result of training, patience, and letting go. From there, the talk moves through how past pain lingers in memory, shame, and self-view, and how mindfulness can slowly make those patterns more transparent. It closes by turning toward the future, fear, and mortality, suggesting that peace comes not by filling time but by learning to rest amid change.
Chapters: 00:00 What presence means 01:50 Thich Nhat Hanh's influence 04:03 Presence as fruit 07:10 Dharma and conditionality 10:06 Backlog of the past 13:34 Self and opacity 18:04 Grief loosens shame 25:22 The urge to fill time 31:49 Futurelessness and refuge
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23629

item.title: Impressionistic v. Precise Meditation Instructions
item.description:
How much precision does meditation instruction need, and when does it start to narrow the mind? The talk begins with mindfulness as noticing the “light” rather than getting lost in the movie, then moves through a story about Ajahn Chah that undercuts judging a talk as good or bad. From there it works into the speaker’s own tension between impressionistic and exact guidance, before laying out a more structured practice map: concentration, clarity, and equanimity, along with the “see, hear, feel” way of tracking images, inner speech, and bodily feeling.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and setting 01:00 Watching the light 03:36 Beyond judging talks 06:45 Impressionistic or precise 13:57 Benefits and limits 18:09 Change of direction 20:27 Threefold mindfulness 23:54 Core subjective triad 33:54 Noting and closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23624

item.title: Dharmette: Fear, Love and Political Chaos
item.description:
What does love ask of us when the political moment feels chaotic, fear is present, and there may be no clean response? The talk stays with that question rather than trying to solve it, moving through balance and burnout, wholesome and inefficient suffering, and the possibility that compassion becomes clearer when it is less entangled with control. It then turns toward helplessness, mortality, and anicca, asking what kind of love is possible when there may be nothing left to lose. Ajahn Chah’s image of the already broken cup appears as a quiet way to contemplate impermanence and the freedom it can bring.
Chapters: 00:21 Living the Question 01:37 Balance and Moral Fire 03:20 Not Squandering Pain 04:56 Compassion with Equanimity 06:44 Seeing Dukkha Clearly 08:16 Helplessness and Mortality 09:45 The Broken Cup 11:06 Fear, Courage, and Care 13:24 Good Questions
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23615

item.title: Dharmette: Curiosity and Goodness
item.description:
What if the goodness of the path is not something you can force, but something that appears when curiosity is allowed to lead? The talk moves from the limits of idealizing Dharma or turning it into a set of assignments, toward a quieter claim: sincere, non-transactional interest is what lets mindfulness, energy, and understanding unfold. From there it turns to stubborn karmic knots and the patience of staying in relationship with pain without trying to get rid of it, with a brief reference to Ajahn Chah used to contrast control with open presence.
Chapters: 00:11 Opening into goodness 01:19 Goodness takes time 02:35 Beyond instructions 04:02 Curiosity as practice 06:17 Control or openness 07:28 Working with karmic knots 09:35 Patience and Sangha 11:31 Love deepens interest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23491

item.title: Dharmette: Death at the Center of Life
item.description:
What if death is not the end of life but its center? The talk moves from a wry opening about golf into a sustained reflection on mortality, finitude, and the way uncertainty shapes every present moment. Along the way, it weighs philosophical claims about eternity against Buddhist practices like the five remembrances, then turns to self-view as a possible bulwark against fear and asks what freedom might look like if we were less afraid.
Chapters: 00:18 From golf to mortality 00:54 Death at the core 02:23 Living with finitude 03:59 Eternity and meaning 05:58 Inter-being and loss 06:56 The deathless and refuge 08:33 Five remembrances 09:42 Uncertainty at the center 11:19 Selfing and freedom
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23460

item.title: Dharmette: Unquenchability and Questions of Meaning
item.description:
What if the most successful life in the room still leaves someone asking, “What’s the point?” The talk begins with golf, sports fandom, and a headline about a dominant player who says winning is only briefly satisfying, then turns that unease toward dukkha as the sense that experience itself does not fully quench the heart. From there it asks what is actually worth wanting, and suggests that this kind of question is not solved by an idea but lived into through training, practice, and time, with Rilke’s counsel to love the questions serving as a quiet guide.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening with golf 02:08 Scheffler asks why 05:40 More than suffering 07:27 What is worth wanting 09:04 Rilke and the questions 09:39 Answered through training 11:27 Doubt dissolves
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23422

item.title: Dharmette: Unconscious Acting-Out
item.description:
When a small, unskillful action seems to come from nowhere, what does that reveal about the mind? The talk begins with the familiar feeling of a self that seems close, coherent, and available to inspection, then turns to Freud, Copernicus, Darwin, and Yogacara to unsettle that picture and make room for unconscious forces, habitual patterns, and karmic seeds. From there it moves into shame, repair, and the practical question of how meditation can loosen these pressures without forcing everything into words. The closing emphasis is on investigation, self-knowledge, and the need for other people in the work of waking up.
Chapters: 00:12 The felt self 00:55 Three humblings 03:22 Storehouse consciousness 04:25 An unskillful moment 06:49 Leakage and shame 08:59 Non-shame investigation 11:36 Remorse and repair 13:42 Wisdom together
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23371

item.title: Morning Instruction: Reviewing the Factors of Enlightenment
item.description:
Starting from the feeling that we have to become someone else before peace is possible, the talk opens a different experiment: enoughness, basic okay-ness, and the sense that you do not need to change yourself to be worthy of peace. It then moves through several factors of awakening as lived experience—mindful noticing, investigation, willingness, interest, tranquility, and equanimity—showing how the nervous system settles when it stops fighting itself. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to the contrast between agitated future-thinking and a quieter way of resting in this moment, even while acknowledging that life and the world are still complex. By the end, the teaching turns toward a simple, intimate claim: the face of emptiness is love.
Chapters: 02:20 Enoughness 04:55 Mindful Seeing 06:30 Willingness and Interest 08:12 Awareness and Tranquility 09:43 Delight in the Path 13:24 Future-Lessness 20:58 Equanimity 25:58 Half-Dead, Half-Alive 40:50 Love and Emptiness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23254

item.title: Samadhi
item.description:
Can concentration be willed into place, or does it arrive when the mind stops bargaining with experience? The talk follows samadhi from attention and alertness into a deeper question of what conditions let the mind settle: letting go, soft readiness, and the shift from strategizing to simple presence. Along the way, it contrasts fragmented, problem-solving attention with a steadier state that is more porous, more receptive, and less driven by craving for what comes next.
Chapters: 00:30 Opening the Theme 05:56 Attention and Will 10:56 How Attention Works 15:08 Mindfulness and Samadhi 17:39 One Path, Not Two 24:58 Craving and Relief 34:15 Objects and Conditions 40:20 Letting Go of Meaning 45:14 Silence, Faith, and Enough
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23252

item.title: Energetic Vigor, Self-View & Rapturous Interest
item.description:
What if the energy behind practice is not just more effort, but a clearer why? The talk moves from asking why we want to live, through the fact that awakening runs against the stream of habit, into a careful look at how willful striving can become another form of self-making. It then turns toward a different mode of practice: surrender, willingness, and a quieter, more rapturous interest that makes effort feel less like strain and more like discovery.
Chapters: 00:30 Opening the inquiry 02:09 Why practice matters 04:44 Against the stream 08:34 Conditioning and freedom 11:24 Energy, depletion, and grace 17:51 The four recollections 28:03 Effort without selfing 40:08 Willingness and pīti 46:18 Closing reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23236

item.title: Morning Instruction: Equanimity precedes Mindfulness
item.description:
The talk begins with the problem of a mind that keeps trying to improve the moment, adjust the conditions, and fight with what is here. From that starting point, it turns toward befriending experience, forgiving imperfection, and seeing how equanimity can come before mindfulness: when control is set down, awareness becomes available in a new way. Much of the instruction stays close to the breath, noticing its beginning and ending, while also working with helplessness, self-judgment, and the effort to keep the heart open.
Chapters: 00:27 Befriending the Moment 01:24 The Urge to Optimize 03:57 Equanimity Before Mindfulness 07:27 Effortless Knowing 11:02 Forgiving Imperfection 15:14 The Innocence of Mind 21:23 Drifting and Returning 27:37 Beginning and Ending 33:00 Helplessness and Open Heart
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23229

item.title: Sati
item.description:
What does mindfulness actually do when it is no longer treated as a simple attentional trick? The talk moves from the common habit of asking “what do I get?” to a Dharma frame centered on what gets let go, then opens mindfulness as something woven through equanimity, concentration, non-reactivity, and the loosening of self-story. Along the way, the speaker uses images of the sick Buddha, a mind-wandering driver and shopper, and an elephant with a bamboo shaft to show how practice steadies attention without shutting life down. The talk closes by asking whether we can rest, think, and feel without losing contact with awareness.
Chapters: 00:00 Why the Dharma Lets Go 03:18 Awakening as Cultivation 05:38 The Seven Factors Balanced 09:32 What Sati Includes 14:06 Mindlessness and Restoration 20:45 Thought, Narrative, and Identification 27:22 Mindfulness as Master Key 33:57 Holding the Mind Steady 40:29 Reactivity, Self, and Less Grip
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23216

item.title: Dharmette: Moral Vandalism, Anger and Love
item.description:
What happens when anger is not denied, but listened to for the movement it wants to make? The talk begins with a small somatic story about a meditation injury and a leg that wants to kick, then widens into a question about whether rage can be channeled without harm. From there it moves through the risks of anger, a Buddhist teaching on the “poisoned root and honeyed tip” of anger, and finally to Martin Luther King Jr.’s account of agape as active, redemptive goodwill. The result is a quiet but charged inquiry into how moral outrage might become courage and care rather than self-righteousness or collapse.
Chapters: 00:09 Somatic starting point 02:43 The taboo of anger 04:55 Danger and restraint 06:04 Wisdom inside anger 09:18 Moral outrage needs a path 10:51 King as model 13:25 Agape, not sentiment 15:28 Closing encouragement
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23190

item.title: Dialogue on What's Beyond Judging, Comparing, Fixing
item.description:
What happens when judging, comparing, and fixing start to loosen their grip? The talk moves from retreat reflections and quotes from Ajahn Sumedho, Ajahn Chah, Philip Moffitt, and Dogen into a back-and-forth exploration of how practice can mature without turning into self-perfection. It keeps circling the question of psychological healing and awakening, asking how much the personality needs to be integrated, what freedom actually looks like, and why trust and non-grasping seem to matter more than control.
Chapters: 00:00 Judging, Comparing, Fixing 00:53 Ajahn Sumedho's Doorway 03:10 Not Perfecting the Self 07:47 Sincere Practice 11:44 Radical Inner Shift 14:11 Trust Beyond Thought 18:31 Psychology and Awakening 26:11 Safety, Depth, and Breadth 34:06 Ordinary Freedom
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://irc.audiodharma.org/talks/23114

item.title: Beyond Judging, Comparing and Fixing: Fixing
item.description:
What if the constant urge to improve, optimize, and repair is itself part of the problem? The talk moves from the fatigue of always wanting the moment to be better, into a close look at the hidden habits that make practice into self-fixing: judging, comparing, worrying, narrating, and trying to secure the future. From there, it gradually opens toward a different orientation—receptivity, equanimity, and the possibility of letting experience teach without immediately turning it into a project.
Chapters: 00:00 One Fidget Away 02:55 The Fixing Impulse 08:14 Pain and Pressure 13:33 Secret Motives 19:40 Non-Gaining Practice 25:35 Curiosity Over Outcome 31:25 Blessing the Past 37:03 Enough, Just This
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://irc.audiodharma.org/talks/23104

item.title: Beyond Judging Comparing and Fixing: Judging
item.description:
What if the mind’s habit of judging is less a sign of clarity than a way of bracing against pain? The talk moves from the early mindfulness language of being non-judgmental into a careful distinction between judgment and discernment, then into the First Noble Truth, the two arrows, and the ways anger, aversion, and self-blame add layers to suffering. By the end, the emphasis has shifted toward forgiveness, equanimity with unpleasantness, and a quieter self-love that lets judgment fall away without losing discernment.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 01:57 Mindfulness and Judgment 04:05 Judgment Versus Discernment 09:07 Learning Peace 15:44 The Two Arrows 18:59 Pain as Practice 24:15 Anger and Accountability 27:16 Forgiveness and Equanimity 31:47 Self-Love and Freedom
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://irc.audiodharma.org/talks/23094

item.title: Morning Reflection and Walking Instructions
item.description:
What does it mean to keep practice continuous when the mind is tired, restless, or ready to judge, fix, and compare? The talk begins with a plain-eyed morning reflection: the day may be beautiful, or not, and the work is to meet whatever shows up without collapsing into self-story. From there it turns to walking practice as a way to balance sitting, sustain awareness, and make the mind simple enough to stay awake, even when nothing obvious seems to be happening. It closes with a small experiment in varying the attention—seeing, hearing, feet, whole body, then loving-kindness—to keep the practice fresh.
Chapters: 00:00 Beginning Without Certainty 01:10 What Contact Reveals 02:38 Continuity Through the Day 03:46 Not Acquiescing to Delusion 05:14 Bell, Inventory, and Mountain 06:45 How to Walk 08:13 Planting Seeds Quietly 10:16 A Fresh Walking Sequence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://irc.audiodharma.org/talks/23184

item.title: Beyond Judging, Comparing and Fixing: Opening Session
item.description:
What happens if a retreat begins by dropping the need to know how it will go? The talk frames the opening as an experiment in tolerating uncertainty, then turns toward three movements that support practice: attention that receives experience without force, renunciation of the endless pull toward “more,” and love that can stay steady without naivete. The second half shifts into the retreat container itself, with an explanation of the refuges and the five precepts, so the talk moves from orientation and reassurance into a shared ethical and ritual beginning.
Chapters: 00:01 Entering Not Knowing 02:28 Reassurance and Faith 05:03 Attention as Prayer 10:03 What Is Enough 15:33 Love in Hard Times 19:03 Group Sharing and Themes 26:34 Judging and Comparing 38:31 Refuge and Precepts
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://irc.audiodharma.org/talks/23112

item.title: Dharmette: When Inspiritation to Practice is Lost
item.description:
When inspiration to practice goes flat, the talk asks what is actually happening: is the path being asked to do something it cannot do, or has an old intention simply outlived itself? The speaker moves through tolerance for waning energy, the limits of technique, and the possibility that practice may need a new motive, a life move, or support from friendship, service, or another kind of wisdom. Near the end, a Zen warning about not wasting life and the Tibetan four thoughts turn the talk toward urgency, impermanence, and the defects of ordinary craving, before closing with the image of resting in confusion until a direction emerges.
Chapters: 00:30 Making Room for Low Inspiration 02:43 Revisiting the Original Why 04:38 What Dharma Can’t Do 06:59 When Action Is Missing 08:44 Intrinsic Interest 10:27 Love for Others 12:22 Urgency and the Four Thoughts 13:56 Resting in Confusion
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23056

item.title: Dharmette: Sila Reflections
item.description:
What does ethical conduct look like when it is not just rule-following, but a way of becoming a refuge for others? The talk moves from a short reflection on sila into a wider meditation on humility, grief, and the willingness to see where our actions ripple outward and where we have been wrong. It then opens into questions of love, moral imagination, and the loosening of ego, ending with the suggestion that practice is a way of completing a life before death.
Chapters: 01:07 Becoming a refuge 02:20 Love, grief, and goodness 05:03 Ethics and moral growth 07:20 Pain becoming love 08:51 Skillful means and belonging 11:38 Dreamlike awakening 13:15 Moral imagination 14:45 Completing this life
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23005

item.title: Fear, Thinking, Safety and Samadhi (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if fear is not a sign that something is wrong, but the force that keeps making a self, planning, and narration feel necessary? The talk moves from early development and the body’s need for regulation into a close look at how thinking, language, and clinging try to create safety under pressure. From there it turns toward samadhi as a quieter, more unified state: not a narrow focus alone, but a widening equanimity in which effort softens into willingness, and the question becomes what is seen when nothing is being wanted.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 How self forms 03:57 Fear and regulation 09:10 Feeling as alarm 14:09 The safety of thinking 18:32 Letting practice work 24:15 Healing and mortality 30:43 Courage and faith 36:56 Samadhi and clarity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/90262/

item.title: Dharmette: 'A thing that masquerades as freedom of attention is on the rampage'
item.description:
What if the thing that looks like freedom of attention is actually feeding craving, hate, and a tighter loop of satisfaction and heartbreak? The talk moves from a sharp critique of technological frenzy to a slower argument for patient attention as a form of love: staying with experience, risking boredom, and letting attention open into care rather than compulsive self-expression. Along the way, the speaker leans on Rilke, Naomi Shihab Nye, and Simone Weil to show how attention can become prayer, and how hate can soften when it is really looked at.
Chapters: 00:19 Attention and love 01:31 The manifesto's warning 02:56 True freedom of attention 04:29 Craving tightened 06:08 Rilke and slowing down 07:58 Depth over surface 10:28 Attention as prayer 12:11 Giving up hate
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22905

The talk moves from a sharp critique of technological frenzy to a slower argument for patient attention as a form of love: staying with experience, risking boredom, and letting attention open into care rather than compulsive self-expression. Along the way, the speaker leans on Rilke, Naomi Shihab Nye, and Simone Weil to show how attention can become prayer, and how hate can soften when it is really looked at.
Chapters: 00:19 Attention and love 01:31 The manifesto's warning 02:56 True freedom of attention 04:29 Craving tightened 06:08 Rilke and slowing down 07:58 Depth over surface 10:28 Attention as prayer 12:11 Giving up hate
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22905

item.title: Somebody, Nobody, Self-Love, Anatta
item.description:
What is the difference between a healthy sense of self and the insight of not-self? The talk moves from an old teaching line — that you have to be somebody before being nobody — into a careful reworking of what self-love means: not ego-affirmation, but forgiveness, non-clinging, and a first loosening of attachment to self-story. From there it turns toward anatta as a deeper kind of disidentification, where what once felt like the place from which we see becomes just another thing seen.
Chapters: 00:15 Looking Back 02:12 Somebody Before Nobody 04:43 Language and Self 05:11 Self-Love as Forgiveness 07:45 Not-Self Is Flexibility 09:59 The Self Model Opens 12:27 Return to Innocence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22861

item.title: Dharmette: Pleasant and Unpleasant Dissolution
item.description:
What makes dissolution feel peaceful in one case and terrifying in another? The talk starts with a lived memory of self and agency melting away, then moves into the uneasy overlap between contemplative emptiness and experiences like derealization and depersonalization. From there it brings in a recent study, Nagarjuna on emptiness, and the Buddha’s teaching on craving to become, arguing that death anxiety and our models of self can change the felt meaning of letting go. By the end, the talk turns toward impermanence, grief, and a harder kind of courage that practice can support without pretending death is simple.
Chapters: 00:17 A peaceful dissolution 01:17 When emptiness unsettles 02:34 Nagarjuna on emptiness 04:05 Why experiences diverge 06:08 Self as fear's effect 08:20 Craving to become 10:20 Impermanence at the center 12:14 Grief and courage
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22801

item.title: Know Thyself, Release Thyself, Merging
item.description:
How do self-knowledge and not-self become the conditions for real intimacy? The talk moves from sorting out our stable longings, habits, and shame to the claim that meditation loosens the stale stories we keep telling about ourselves, so we can meet experience with less defensiveness and more contact. Along the way, it turns to embodiment, empathy, and the possibility of seeing and being seen without reducing another person to a role or a concept. The final movement explores merging and separateness, asking what changes when the need to protect softens enough for closeness, grief, and freedom to be felt together.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Know Thyself 03:45 Habit Energies 07:26 Seeing and Being Seen 12:38 New Ways of Thinking 19:11 Disidentifying From Self 24:50 Embodiment and Aliveness 32:42 Love Without Possession 40:54 Merging and Letting Go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/91357/

item.title: Dukkha as Ambivalence in Relational Life
item.description:
How do we love without asking another person to end our craving? The talk moves from a comic image of a “better soulmate” into a careful examination of dukkha as ambivalence in relationship: the way love and disappointment, trust and suspicion, care and control are often mixed together. From there, the speaker works through psychoanalytic and Dharma framing, then turns toward equanimity as the capacity to stay present without collapsing into clinging, suppression, or the urge to fix what cannot be fixed.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 A Comic Opening 02:31 Love and Lack 05:54 Pain and Disconnection 09:29 What Ambivalence Means 14:30 Freud and Mixed Feeling 17:51 Equanimity Widens 24:57 Acceptance in Practice 29:01 Relationship and Dependence 39:44 Compassion Without Control
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/91355/

item.title: Dharma Supports Relationships, Relationships Help Us Get Freer
item.description:
Can Dharma be used to make relationships work better, or does relationship itself become a way to see where freedom is still lacking? The talk moves through that tension carefully, warning against turning practice into a transaction or a prize, then widening into a view of belonging, loneliness, and the ethical weight of dependence on others. It eventually turns toward a quieter freedom: loosening the grip of story, softening possessiveness, and letting love be lighter, clearer, and less driven by control.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:12 Opening cautions 01:36 Practice without bargaining 04:18 Conditions, not guarantees 06:27 Truth and relationship 09:09 Freedom and attachment 13:25 Belonging and isolation 18:52 Sangha and non-harming 24:53 Love, clarity, and fog 30:28 One foot out
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/91353/

item.title: Landing in the Breath
item.description:
What happens when the mind keeps turning every sensation into a question about the future? The talk begins with a simple instruction to land in the body and relax the face, shoulders, and belly, then moves into the habit of loading experience with hope, self-consciousness, and interpretation. From there it settles into breath practice, emphasizing the actuality of breathing, the loose ends of a human life, and the discipline of putting some trust in this moment rather than in the next chapter. Later, the talk turns practical again with walking meditation, boredom, changing anchors, and the sense that the quiet work of practice may be building below the level of obvious feeling.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving in the body 07:38 Possibility in experience 11:31 Landing in the breath 20:47 Loose ends and uncertainty 33:19 Hope in this moment 40:46 Not overreading retreat 43:06 Walking with ease 48:18 Boredom and fresh anchors
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/91352/

item.title: Dharmette: Power and Safety
item.description:
How do we work with the power that practice develops without letting it turn into self-importance or harm? The talk begins with a story about Ajahn Chah warning young monks not to think, “I’m a teacher,” and keeps returning to the reminder that one is still, at heart, a practitioner—mindful, empty, and accountable. From there it moves through sila as protection, the need for honest feedback from others, and the idea that real Dharma speech arises from need rather than performance. By the end, the question is practical and direct: how can power be used in a way that makes the heart a refuge and keeps a community safe?
Chapters: 00:36 Why Power Matters 01:35 Ajahn Chah's Warning 04:57 Ripples of Goodness 06:45 Dharma as Response 08:22 Unpossessable Dharma 10:52 Feedback and Safety 12:07 Power and Prestige 13:58 A Quiet Trace
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22693

item.title: Dharmette: Courage and Fear
item.description:
What does courage look like when fear is already shaping the mind’s sense of safety? The talk moves from a personal admission of being risk-averse into a close look at how fear narrows attention, intensifies clinging, and makes letting go feel implausible. From there it widens into the path itself: opening to dukkha, facing groundlessness, relinquishing control, and asking what love looks like when it is less afraid.
Chapters: 00:13 Opening to courage 01:40 Fear at the threshold 03:58 Faith and the path 06:08 How fear works 08:25 Groundless moments 10:03 Terror and waiting 11:34 Fear of suffering 13:56 Shared courage 15:30 Love less afraid
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22672

item.title: Dharmette: Surrender
item.description:
What does surrender mean when it is not resignation, not submission to an outside authority, but the softening of the need to control experience? The talk moves from Leonard Cohen’s remark that “the search itself dissolved” through Augustine’s language of a restless heart, then into a Dharma framing where mindfulness, insight, and samadhi all require laying down volitional effort and trusting the present moment. Along the way, the speaker holds a careful balance: practice asks for independent verification, yet also for the willingness to stop fidgeting with life long enough to be changed by it.
Chapters: 00:31 Cohen and the search 01:17 Restless heart 02:42 Surrender in Dharma 03:32 Discernment and trust 05:59 The will to control 08:16 Changed by experience 11:08 Not knowing and refuge 13:33 Gateway to absorption 15:24 True power
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22670

item.title: Dharmette: Self-Esteem Preoccupation as Symptom
item.description:
What if self-esteem is not a problem to solve, but a symptom of something more basic: need deprivation, fear, and pain? The talk moves from a brief reflection on Dharma’s conversation with science into a close reading of psychologists Richard Ryan and Kirk Warren Brown, then into a Dharma argument that selfing is an effect of suffering rather than its cause. From there it turns toward ordinariness, refuge, and the possibility of living ethically without constantly having to prove who we are.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the conversation 01:26 Science and Dharma 02:29 Self-esteem as symptom 04:37 Not-self without idealism 06:23 Ordinary worthiness 07:49 Selfing and pain 10:06 Thawing the wound 12:07 Ethics and refuge 14:16 Just being a person
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22667

item.title: Dharmette: Expansiveness of Awe
item.description:
How does awe loosen the grip of a life that has become too small? The talk moves from the ordinary discomfort of disorientation into the widening feeling of awe—through nature, virtuosity, moral beauty, and the surprise of the Dharma itself—showing how a little disorientation can open into gratitude, wonder, and a less claustrophobic sense of self. Along the way, the speaker brings in psychology, personal retreat stories, and the Buddhist fetter of attachment to convention to ask how we might live when the world feels far larger than our usual habits allow.
Chapters: 00:12 Disorientation and Awe 01:19 What Awe Does 02:56 Nature, Skill, Goodness 05:10 Beyond Claustrophobia 06:37 Dharma Across Time 08:33 Closeness of Peace 10:56 Freedom and Convention 13:24 Stars and Smallness 15:32 Living in Awe
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22664

item.title: Dharmette: Grief as a Facet of Dharma
item.description:
What if grief is not a problem Dharma removes, but one of the ways the path stays honest? The talk moves from the claim that no teaching is the last word to a sustained argument that grief belongs with wakefulness, love, ethical sensitivity, and the hard work of seeing suffering clearly. Using images of being “soaked to the skin,” the Buddha’s own encounters with loss, and the difference between crying and real change, the talk keeps asking how awakening opens the heart without pretending pain disappears.
Chapters: 00:12 Dharma and tension 01:32 Grief as irreducible 03:10 The Buddha and loss 05:51 Not a pathology 07:12 Practice and growing pains 10:18 Facing dukkha 12:46 Grieving to change 14:02 Grief into love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22660

item.title: Dharmette: Humor in the Dharma
item.description:
What if seriousness is not a sign of depth, but a habit of the ego? The talk begins with a playful “fake list” of Dharma qualities and moves through stories of being called “Captain Buzzkill,” then opens into a case for humor as a way truth arrives quickly, before widening to spiritual friendship and the shared ease of laughing together. It closes by showing how practice can loosen self-consciousness enough for crying, laughter, and peace to be present at once.
Chapters: 00:11 A playful fake list 01:28 Captain Buzzkill 03:54 Seriousness and lightness 06:08 Truth told quickly 07:37 Learning to play 09:02 Therapy and play 10:38 Playing with self 12:43 Crying and laughing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22653

item.title: Dharmette: Question about Etiquette Approaching Teachers
item.description:
How should a student approach a meditation teacher, and what does the relationship actually ask for? The talk moves from a humorous Michael Scott aside about needing to be liked into a careful unpacking of approval, projection, and the way old relational patterns can get stirred up around teachers. From there, it settles into a quieter answer: teaching as service, the need for flexibility, and the value of sincerity—less ego, more willingness to be understood and to meet in the open space of the Dharma.
Chapters: 00:00 Question and framing 02:22 Teaching as service 04:25 No fixed etiquette 06:36 Projection and transference 09:00 Relationship as healing 10:56 What teachers want 13:39 Loved or understood
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22608

item.title: Talk: Spiritual Friendship and Sila as Our Practice
item.description:
What if awakening is not a private project, but something that arrives through relationship, resemblance, and care? The talk begins with the Buddha’s correction to Ananda on spiritual friendship, then moves into the way Sangha, teachers, and even small gestures shape the mind more than we usually admit. From there it widens into a careful argument that sila is not niceness or rule-keeping, but a way of living that leaves less trace, deepens moral sensitivity, and supports a life of non-regret.
Chapters: 00:00 Spiritual friendship 02:06 Mind is permeable 06:11 Sangha as support 09:09 Warm hand to warm hand 16:48 Not escape from life 26:03 Training through habits 32:51 Seeing ourselves clearly 39:23 Sila and non-regret
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22630
