item.title: Evening Reflections
item.description:
Can you let a felt sense of your own goodness be present, even for a moment, without turning it into self-indulgence? The talk moves gently through the tension between deserving and undeserving, setting aside the usual arguments of pride and shame in order to point toward a worthiness that comes before those judgments. It ends by suggesting that this quiet goodness can be carried into the night, into sleep, and into dreams.
Chapters: 00:00 Appreciating Goodness 01:27 Loving Yourself 02:16 Beyond Debates 03:34 A Deeper Worthiness 04:24 Carry It Into Sleep 05:38 Closing Thanks
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22632

item.title: Talk: Knowing, Not-Knowing, Selfing
item.description:
Knowing and not knowing are treated here as a live practice, not as opposites. The talk moves from the need to understand our conditioning and the causes of suffering, into a gentler inquiry that can unsettle the stories we use to hold ourselves together. Along the way it draws on Agnes Callard, Yeats, the Dhammapada, Ajahn Chah, and other voices to show how deep listening, surrender, and dialogue can loosen the grip of selfing. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from getting a better story about oneself to becoming less afraid of what clear seeing might reveal.
Chapters: 00:00 Knowing and Not Knowing 02:30 Safety and Power 06:15 Dialogue and Inquiry 08:44 Narrative Fractures 11:43 Ignorance and Dukkha 17:39 Learning What Matters 24:14 Renunciation of Control 28:03 The Two Selves 36:36 Listening Through Identity
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22626

item.title: Evening Reflections - A Poem
item.description:
A retreat in the mountains becomes a quiet confrontation with impermanence: the beauty of the landscape opens into the fact that everything changes, and even stability doesn’t last. The talk turns through a poem about weakness, crumbling, and non-existence, then into a more personal reflection on fear, childhood dusk, and the way aliveness can widen when change is fully felt. It ends by naming what remains to do: remembering, letting go, and allowing in the warmth of grief.
Chapters: 00:00 Mountain Austerity 00:50 A Poem Appears 01:34 Stability Crumbles 02:20 Fear Wakes Up 02:59 Aliveness Opens 03:36 Remembering and Letting Go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22622

item.title: Talk: Whys and Hows of Effort - Energy & Bhava Tanha
item.description:
What kind of effort is helpful in practice, and when does striving quietly become another form of craving? The talk moves from trust and patience, through motivation shaped by benefit to others, into the subtle ways progress-seeking can tighten the mind around becoming and achievement. Along the way, it draws on Sharon Salzberg, Nietzsche, Suzuki Roshi, Ajahn Sumedho, and the Tibetan reflections on precious human life to show how energy can come from receptivity rather than force.
Chapters: 00:00 Listening and trust 05:44 Practice that simply continues 12:21 Why make the effort 17:00 Precious human life 19:00 Against the stream 32:07 Open palm of release 40:08 Non-gaining practice 45:55 Your own path
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22618

item.title: Dharmette: Fear at the Gateway of Stillness
item.description:
Fear is traced to the edge of stillness: just as the mind begins to settle, a habit of vigilance rushes in and insists that surrender is unsafe. The talk follows that tension from body-level fear to the deeper unease around uncertainty, not-self, and the thinning of the usual sense of control. Rather than pushing through by force, it points toward making fear an object of practice, habituating to the edge, and letting faith and trust carry the moment when silence feels risky. Along the way, the speaker grounds the discussion in ordinary life pressures, then widens it to mortality, grief, and the possibility that nothing hidden is waiting to ambush us.
Chapters: 00:17 The question of fear 01:46 Safety and vigilance 03:00 Why letting go feels unsafe 05:21 At the gateway 06:59 Spiritual vertigo 09:31 Working with fear 11:32 Training the nervous system 13:28 Mortality and faith 15:03 Surrender to silence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22489

item.title: Dharmette: Moral outrage and love
item.description:
How do love and moral outrage get tangled, and can anger ever be useful without hardening into hate? The talk begins with ordinary care and widens into a question about how our concern for suffering can become fused with disgust, punishment, fear, and self-view. Drawing on Ruth King and Hannah Chapman, it follows that tension toward a quieter claim: outrage may start the movement, but love has to have the last word, even if that means carrying more grief and less certainty.
Chapters: 00:12 Love before practice 01:20 Outrage and entanglement 02:49 When care turns fierce 04:18 Purifying moral emotion 05:56 Disgust and hate 07:38 Fear and self-view 09:26 Love without a self 11:25 Grief as the burden
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22406

item.title: Dharmette: On not understanding dharma talks
item.description:
When a Dharma talk feels out of reach, is that a sign you missed something—or part of the practice itself? The talk begins with an anonymous question about moments of recognition and moments of confusion, then moves into a gentle defense of not understanding, arguing that wisdom includes noticing the limits of one’s own view. Along the way, it draws on the Buddha’s teaching style, Agnes Callard on Socratic inquiry, and Socrates as someone who cared more about learning than being right. The close is plainspoken: there are no makeup classes, only patience, ripening, and the willingness to let understanding come in its own time.
Chapters: 00:12 The listener's question 01:37 Teaching is imperfect 03:00 Recognition and intimacy 05:21 Wisdom in not knowing 07:34 Learning to be wrong 10:06 Practice matures 12:09 Patience and belief
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22333

item.title: Equanimity 6
item.description:
What changes when the heart stops wrestling with pleasant and unpleasant, and letting go is no longer something to do? The talk moves from the hard work of cultivating equanimity, through the stage of “equanimity with formations,” into a deeper sense of clarity where nothing sticks and experience feels less dualistic. Along the way, the speaker weighs the wholesomeness of this freedom against the risks of dissociation, nihilism, and mistaking states for liberation, then returns to the steadier question of what actually supports a Dharma life.
Chapters: 00:12 Cultivating Equanimity 02:27 Beyond Forcing Letting Go 04:27 Mahasi Equanimity 07:04 The Three Characteristics 10:11 Safety In Groundlessness 13:14 Rumi And Letting Go 15:09 Compassion And Nihilism 17:43 Not Chasing States 20:31 A Dharma Life
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22312

item.title: Equanimity 5
item.description:
What does equanimity look like when life and practice refuse to fit a clean story? The talk begins by questioning neat spiritual autobiographies, then moves into a practical, almost clinical look at how equanimity is noticed, lost, and slowly restored in the midst of thought, clinging, and emotional overload. It lingers on the limits of what can be done in the moment, the way self-stories drain their charge, and how pain tolerance, care for others, and remorse can support a steadier heart.
Chapters: 00:30 Unreliable spiritual stories 01:58 Sincerity over certainty 02:51 Noticing lack of equanimity 04:33 Equanimity and not-self 05:54 Draining self-charges 07:52 Respecting limits 09:38 Expanding pain tolerance 11:05 Holding pain for others
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22311

item.title: Equanimity 4
item.description:
What happens when compassion reaches the end of its power? The talk follows a practice of alternating loving-kindness and equanimity, especially when caring becomes heavy, clingy, or ineffective, and when the heart needs to rest rather than strain to fix what it cannot control. A friend’s month-long heart practice, reflections on global suffering, and a brief reference to Carl Rogers all help shape the teaching into a practical rhythm: take in suffering, respond with love, then return to the recognition of limits and release.
Chapters: 00:13 Opening practice frame 02:30 Love at the limit 04:38 Holding suffering lightly 07:14 Two sides of love 09:12 Suffering wisely 11:28 Local and practical care 13:47 Closing practice instructions
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22310

item.title: Equanimity 3
item.description:
What happens when you stop trying to force experience into the right shape and instead learn to stay with pleasant, unpleasant, and the urge to react? The talk moves from definitions of equanimity into a practical map: balanced perception, less identification, and a widening of perspective that makes reactive stories less necessary. It then turns to how that balance ripens over time, how it can feel painful at first, and why even shame and jealousy may need to be met carefully rather than pushed away. The closing sections become more intimate and specific, with questions about difficult feelings, small acts of practice, mortality, and the support of another person’s calm gaze.
Chapters: 09:00 Beginning with affect 11:15 What equanimity means 14:13 Not self-absorption 17:00 A workable sequence 19:53 What it is not 23:56 Compassion with balance 27:42 Love and gratitude 32:40 How it ripens 41:14 Shame, mortality, and support
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22309

item.title: Equanimity 2
item.description:
When pleasant or unpleasant feelings start to feel self-relevant, the talk traces how quickly the mind turns them into a project: something to secure, fix, or defend. It then shifts toward a different response to dukkha and pain—not more control, but relaxing into sensory experience and seeing the push and pull as a flow of arising and passing. The emphasis stays practical and close to experience: notice the alarm, the urgency, the bracing, and see what loosens when those reactions are allowed to soften.
Chapters: 03:34 Self-Relevance 05:37 Pleasant and Unpleasant 07:02 Alarm and Strategy 09:24 What Equanimity Means 13:18 Desperation Around Pain 16:01 Relaxing the Bracing 17:26 Letting Go Practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22308

item.title: Equanimity 1
item.description:
Equanimity here is not presented as calm distance, but as a way of meeting the pressure of experience without trying to control it. The talk moves from a practical distinction between lecture and lived practice, into stories from retreat participants, and then into a sustained inquiry into how suffering, preference, helplessness, and love shape the heart. Near the end, the speaker turns to the Satipatthana formula and to feeling tone itself, arguing that equanimity is less about blankness than about softening our relationship to what arises.
Chapters: 01:20 What retreat teaches 05:18 First meanings of equanimity 11:40 Softening instead of freezing 19:35 Helplessness and wise action 29:25 Sitting with experience 40:32 Friction falls away 54:45 Love it, not escape it 58:42 Feeling at the center
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22307

item.title: Dharmette: The mirage of self evaporates as the fires cool
item.description:
What happens when the urge to get free is still tangled with the very self that wants freedom? The talk follows a quote from Ajahn Sucitto into a meditation on cooling rather than struggle: nibbana as space, dispassion, and the easing of compulsive heat. Along the way it takes up Mara, evolutionary conditioning, and the way practice can shift from self-improvement to a quieter, less territorial care for life. It closes by asking how the self may reappear more innocently once it is no longer being endlessly curated.
Chapters: 00:16 Ajahn Sucitto's frame 02:23 Practice turns inward 04:52 Mara and conditioning 06:19 Openness instead of denial 08:38 Space and cooling 10:45 The self cannot own freedom 12:50 What remains
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22252

item.title: Dharmette: Remembering Ann Buck
item.description:
A remembrance of a friend becomes a meditation on how practice actually shows itself in ordinary life: at a stoplight, in a hospital room, on retreat, and in the long arc of aging. The talk moves from Dylan’s “knocking on heaven’s door,” to a brief reading from Timothy Snyder, to stories of Ann Buck’s fierce warmth, her blunt encouragement to love yourself, and the way her Dharma confidence stayed intact through loss, illness, and time. It ends by widening grief into a question about what the dead would want from the living, with a poem by James Fenton about memory, generosity, and continuing well.
Chapters: 00:09 Morning, Dylan, mortality 01:14 Ann’s passing and example 03:10 Life in the ordinary 05:01 Cling, then let go 06:40 Her direct kindness 08:13 Dharma through conversation 10:30 Mind, aging, and refuge 12:50 What the dead might want
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22216

item.title: Dharmette: To Care and Not to Care Simultaneously
item.description:
How can one care deeply without getting trapped in strain, grief, or attachment? The talk begins with T.S. Eliot’s line “teach us to care and not to care,” then works through the difference between caring sequentially and the more difficult question of caring and not caring at once. From there it moves into emptiness as a thinning of perception, using a sutta image of village, wilderness, earth, and then even subtler absence, before closing with the picture of consciousness as light that no longer lands anywhere and so finds no sorrow.
Chapters: 00:09 Eliot and the question 02:02 Love and rest 04:57 Beyond sequence 06:59 Refining perception 08:47 Less and less 09:58 Where mind does not land 12:01 Quiet closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22138

item.title: Dharmette: Unsustainable Trajectories & Love
item.description:
The talk begins with a hard question: what do we do when our lives, and our society, seem to be on unsustainable trajectories? From there, the speaker moves through anxiety, anger, and a wary brush with nihilism, then turns back toward a practice response that is less about fixing everything than about not adding more harm. A passage from the Dhammapada and a brief story of rangers rescuing a goose on Mount Shasta bring the talk into a quieter register, where care, stewardship, and love are treated as the only durable way forward.
Chapters: 00:09 Unsustainable beginnings 02:00 A larger trajectory 03:21 Anger and confusion 05:06 Powerlessness and nihilism 07:38 Freedom and hope 09:14 Planting long seeds 10:52 Back to fundamentals 11:38 The goose on Shasta
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22101

item.title: Dharmette: Truth & Lies in the US in 2025
item.description:
What happens when the impulse to correct lies turns into rage, and that rage starts to feel like its own trap? The talk begins with childhood anger at hypocrisy, then moves through suffering, honesty, and the damage deception does to shared reality, drawing on a Theravada commentary, Harry Frankfurt’s distinction between lying and bullshit, and a remark from Thomas Metzinger on truthfulness. From there it turns inward: the struggle to stay sane in a deceptive political moment, the pull toward outrage, and a quieter practice of treasuring truth without making one’s peace depend on the world honoring it.
Chapters: 00:00 Hypocrisy and early anger 02:37 Honesty as moral care 04:24 Lies and bullshit 06:33 Truth in practice 07:25 Political outrage cycle 10:20 Treasure the truth 12:24 Seeing self-deception 13:17 Sangha and letting go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22046

item.title: Dharmette: more of our personality in freedom than in our suffering
item.description:
What if suffering is the thing that makes us feel most like a fixed character, while freedom is what reveals our actual particularity? The talk begins with Tolstoy’s famous line about unhappy families, then turns it inside out: suffering may come in many forms, but it is also strangely repetitive, while liberated life shows up as spontaneity, quirkiness, and distinctiveness. From there, the speaker moves through empathy, the cessation of suffering, Winnicott’s spontaneous gesture, and a closing reflection on how to meet others as already free, or at least not far from it.
Chapters: 00:17 Tolstoy Reversed 01:48 Pain Feels Unique 04:55 Meeting Others Carefully 07:28 What Freedom Looks Like 09:28 True Self and Gesture 11:53 Already Enlightened 13:27 Closing Questions
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22017

item.title: Attention and Anicca, Awareness and Anatta
item.description:
What happens when attention is treated as love, and then that same attention is turned back to expose impermanence and not-self? The talk moves from a humorous story about trying to “fix” a partner, through Simone Weil, William James, and retreat practice, into a careful distinction between attention and awareness. From there it asks whether practice is really about control at all, or about learning to rest in what cannot be governed, until the sense of a centered self begins to loosen.
Chapters: 00:01 From Fixing to Listening 02:22 Attention as Love 05:43 Unmixed Attention 09:51 Right Now, Like This 16:00 What Awareness Means 21:33 How Attention Shapes Experience 28:44 Attention and Impermanence 34:35 Awareness Without Control 40:21 Surrendering the Center
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21943
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
What happens when attention is treated as love, and then that same attention is turned back to expose impermanence and not-self? The talk moves from a humorous story about trying to “fix” a partner, through Simone Weil, William James, and retreat practice, into a careful distinction between attention and awareness. From there it asks whether practice is really about control at all, or about learning to rest in what cannot be governed, until the sense of a centered self begins to loosen.
Chapters: 00:01 From Fixing to Listening 02:22 Attention as Love 05:43 Unmixed Attention 09:51 Right Now, Like This 16:00 What Awareness Means 21:33 How Attention Shapes Experience 28:44 Attention and Impermanence 34:35 Awareness Without Control 40:21 Surrendering the Center
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21943

item.title: Dharmette: It's Complicated and It's Simple
item.description:
The talk begins by taking stock of how complicated life really is: the brain, the world, and even our own motives resist tidy explanation. From that wide view, it turns toward a much simpler frame—love and heartbreak—and asks what changes when we stop getting lost in the wrong categories and look instead at suffering, peace, and the limits of control. Along the way, the speaker uses a retreat student’s insight, a Buddha teaching, and a line from Leonard Cohen to show how practice can meet life’s unavoidable tenderness with more honesty and grace.
Chapters: 00:09 Life Is Complicated 01:02 Brains and Networks 02:39 Stories We Tell 04:12 The Reach of the Cosmos 06:15 Love and Heartbreak 06:59 Right Distinctions 09:26 No Cure for Love 11:57 Yielding to Time
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21935

item.title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (5 of 5) Equanimity
item.description:
How do we keep caring without being worn down by the very fact that suffering is so large and so hard to govern? The talk develops equanimity as a kind of deep rest for the heart: still but not rigid, able to hold compassion without collapsing into compulsion, exhaustion, or helplessness. Along the way, it moves through the cost of caring, the danger of nihilism, and the middle path between thinking nothing matters and thinking everything matters. The result is a quiet case for refining love so it becomes more sustainable and more potent.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening gratitude 01:57 Ease and release 03:38 Still, not rigid 05:05 The cost of caring 07:27 Heart at rest 09:40 Caring and not caring 11:31 Purifying compassion 14:03 Helplessness and goodwill
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21889

item.title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (4 of 5) Sympathetic Joy
item.description:
Mudita, or sympathetic joy, is approached here as something you can’t force and don’t need to fake. The talk moves from the difficulty of delighting in another’s good fortune to a practical account of what gets in the way: ownership, comparison, envy, and the ego’s habit of taking in every bit of goodness as its own. Along the way, the speaker uses a sutta from the Anguttara Nikaya and brief references to Joseph Epstein and Matthieu Ricard to sharpen the point that other people’s happiness does not deprive us of anything; it can actually free the heart.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving at Mudita 00:57 Joy Cannot Be Forced 03:24 Resentment and the Sutta 05:41 Joy Beyond Scarcity 08:00 Letting Down the Burden 10:26 What Blocks Joy 12:58 Jealousy and Envy 14:34 Sufficient and Unthreatened
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21874

item.title: Dharmette; The Divine Abodes (3 of 5): Compassion
item.description:
What does it mean to love suffering without trying to control it? The talk begins with dukkha as something obvious and yet still hard to see, then turns to compassion as love in the face of pain, shaped by wisdom, grief, and the complexity of dependent arising. Along the way, it questions easy fixes, emotional enmeshment, and advice-giving, and settles into a quieter sense that compassion becomes steadier as we become more free.
Chapters: 00:16 Suffering Is Everywhere 00:52 Deep and Hard to See 01:48 The Question of Love 03:57 What Compassion Is 06:26 Loss Reveals Impermanence 09:22 Not Enmeshment 11:22 Grief Held by Love 14:21 Wise and Steady Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21867

item.title: Dharmette: Mindfulness: Acceptance and Change
item.description:
Is mindfulness about accepting what is here, or changing what is here? The talk stays with that tension and argues that the real practice is both: full acceptance without bargaining, and change that is not driven by shame or self-improvement. Along the way, it uses a temple story, brief quotations from Bohr and Suzuki Roshi, and a close look at shame, craving, and the urge to become someone else. By the end, the emphasis is on resting in non-shame so that wisdom and love can unfold without self-deprecation.
Chapters: 00:12 The central question 00:55 A pointed teaching story 02:06 Two profound truths 03:08 Acceptance without bargaining 04:59 Shame and self-view 06:21 Sitting with reality 07:45 The craving to become 09:05 Freedom from shame 10:35 Change from acceptance
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21865

item.title: Dharmette: Dereification of Self-Model
item.description:
What happens when thoughts, preferences, and even the sense of “me” stop feeling like solid reality and start appearing as objects in awareness? The talk begins with a childhood thought experiment about everything falling away—family, time, universe—then turns that experience into a careful explanation of emptiness as the progressive dereification of the self-model. From there it moves through ordinary examples like lunch, craving, aversion, ownership, and autobiography, showing how mindfulness and equanimity make what was transparent begin to stand out. It closes by pointing toward quiet as the condition for hearing the subtler noise of selfing and becoming more comfortable with not knowing.
Chapters: 00:18 A Childhood Thought Experiment 04:18 What Dereification Means 07:14 Thoughts Become Objects 09:33 Layers of Selfing 12:17 Ownership and Self 14:11 Quiet and Not Knowing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21870

item.title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (2 of 5) Metta
item.description:
What if love is not a stronger form of wanting, but something that steps outside wanting and fear altogether? The talk moves from the Buddha’s framing of clinging to a clear distinction between kindness and niceness, then into the messy reality of attachment in family and romantic life, where letting go is never tidy. From there it turns to metta as a kind of purification practice: approaching what has been avoided, recognizing goodness, and letting the heart rest in a warmth that seeks no control.
Chapters: 00:35 Love beyond liking 01:49 Wanting and fear 03:36 Metta as approach 05:38 What love is not 06:59 Attachment and family 10:19 Goodness as source 11:39 Moral elevation 13:23 Abiding in love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21857

item.title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (1 of 5) Orienting to Love
item.description:
What if love, not aversion, became the place the mind falls back on when life goes wrong? The talk moves from that question into a practical account of non-hatred: how hatred depends on holding on, why love can coexist with letting go, and how ethical conduct and nervous-system reality shape what is actually possible. From there, the speaker turns to the Brahma-viharas—loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity—treating them less as ideals than as trainable resting places for attention. The talk ends by inviting a flexible, improvisational relationship to aversion, so the week becomes a chance to notice where love can be strengthened.
Chapters: 00:50 Choosing Love First 03:00 Why Hatred Arises 04:48 Non-hatred and Letting Go 07:13 Four Species of Love 10:33 Where the Mind Rests 11:54 Training Good Habits 14:00 Depth and Breadth 15:29 Closing Invitation
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21863

item.title: Dharmette: Believing that Special People Exist
item.description:
What happens when charisma is not inside the so-called charismatic person, but in the need to imagine that special people exist? The talk starts with a line from Rachel Kushner and moves into projection, idealization, and the childhood longing for powerful caretakers, then asks how those habits persist in adult life. From there it shifts toward a quieter recognition of goodness: not possession, comparison, or pedestal-making, but reverence, sympathetic joy, and the ordinary feel of real practice. It closes by suggesting that practice is done because it is right and necessary, while applause is really for life and goodness, not for any special self.
Chapters: 00:17 The question of specialness 02:30 Projection and childhood 04:46 Growing up unresolved 06:57 Reverence without ownership 09:49 Sympathetic joy 11:06 Practice without applause
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21823

item.title: Dharmette: Fear and Love and Dana
item.description:
Fear is treated first as a useful survival system, then as something that quietly narrows the heart. The talk moves from a short neuroscience framing of fear into the story of a Brazilian man who became “pathologically generous” after a stroke, using that example to ask what love might look like with less worry, control, and social self-consciousness. It ends by bringing in Thich Nhat Hanh’s reminder that fear eases when it is acknowledged and looked at directly, opening the possibility of a more radical generosity.
Chapters: 00:09 Fear Keeps Us Safe 02:44 Fear Of Suffering 03:38 Joao’s Sudden Generosity 05:54 Brains And Freedom 07:55 What Love Might Be 09:37 Social Rules And Self-Consciousness 11:55 Meeting Fear Directly 13:24 Courage And Radical Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21804

item.title: Dharmette: Aperture of Attention in Meditation and Life
item.description:
How wide should attention be, in meditation and in the rest of life? The talk uses the image of an aperture or spotlight to trace a practical rhythm: begin narrow with the breath when experience feels too much, open wider when there is more stability, and notice that neither contraction nor expansion is automatically better. It also turns to daily life, asking when it is wise to pull inward for rest and simplicity, and when care, service, or compassion call for a much wider field of attention.
Chapters: 00:19 Aperture of Attention 01:33 Settling In 02:57 Opening With Stability 04:00 Life Beyond Meditation 05:51 Contraction and Compassion 08:10 When Narrowing Helps 10:45 When Wide Attention Heals 11:49 Wise Attention Now
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21764

item.title: Dharmette: Sensitivity to Suffering vs. Fear of Suffering
item.description:
What is the middle path when the danger is not indulgence or harshness, but either melodrama or a numbness that shuts down care? The talk moves from a candid account of grief, post-election unsettlement, and not rushing toward hope into a sharper distinction between being sensitive to suffering and being afraid of it. From there it turns toward courage as something woven through compassion, generosity, renunciation, and steadiness, asking what love looks like when it is less afraid.
Chapters: 00:18 Everyday impatience 02:05 Letting grief ripen 03:48 Middle path reframed 06:01 Disenchantment and space 07:12 Not a social cure 09:05 Fear and sensitivity 10:51 Courage in practice 13:04 What love looks like
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21719

item.title: Brief Post-Election Reflections
item.description:
A post-election talk that begins with shock, grief, and the need to find practice in the middle of uncertainty. The speaker leans on kalyanamitta, anicca, and the line between being all right and being very bad to make room for a response that is clear-eyed without becoming cynical or fake-hopeful. Along the way, references to Mother Teresa, Leonard Cohen, the Buddha on dukkha, and Shinzen Young help shape a question about how heartbreak can deepen care rather than collapse it. The talk ends by asking what form that care might take in a life that becomes, in some sense, a nonviolent protest.
Location: Various
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving Together 01:19 What This Moment Means 02:56 Thinness and Uncertainty 05:15 Grief and Naivete 07:09 Heartbreak That Opens 09:16 Sorting Pain 11:39 Comprehending Dukkha 13:52 Beyond Equanimity 15:56 A Nonviolent Protest
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/87036/

item.title: Dharmette: Post-Election Reflections
item.description:
The talk begins with shock and uncertainty after the election, and moves quickly into the question of what spiritual friendship can hold when nothing feels settled. From there, it traces a sober path through grief, impermanence, and the thin line between care and despair, using images of a broken heart, shattered love, and the need to distinguish love from righteousness or aversion. Rather than reaching for easy reassurance, the speaker asks what it means to comprehend dukkha, how to keep the heart open without collapsing, and what a deeper form of care might look like now.
Chapters: 00:17 Gathering in practice 01:23 Holding the shock 03:09 Anicca and the thin line 05:22 Grief that teaches 08:09 Love after heartbreak 12:07 Comprehending dukkha 14:45 No complacent resting 16:09 Closing offer
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21671

item.title: Dharmette: Giving Love the Last Word
item.description:
Anger and fear are treated here as a kind of fever: painful, but also a sign that something of value is under threat and trying to return to balance. From there, the talk keeps returning to a simple refrain—angry or frightened states may be the first word, but they cannot be the last; if suffering is taken seriously, love has to be given the final word. The middle of the talk turns toward the limits of control, fixed positions, and ownership, and toward the relief that comes when self-view loosens enough for life to be shared rather than dominated.
Chapters: 00:19 Fever and Anger 02:25 Love the Last Word 05:23 The Koan of Love 08:12 Not Knowing 09:16 Releasing Ownership 10:24 Relief and Openness 11:43 Every Contact Matters 12:39 Closing Blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21624

item.title: Love & Rest Daylong - Introduction to theme
item.description:
How do we care deeply without being pulled into frenzy, numbness, or hate? The talk begins in the pressure of election season and the wider moral strain of living with greed, hatred, delusion, then turns toward the surprising steadiness that comes from sangha, good friends, and a path that can hold both distress and love. It asks whether some of our suffering is necessary and whether practice can help us keep the heart open without being overexposed.
Chapters: 00:00 Naming the strain 03:35 Sangha and moral clarity 08:57 The heart under pressure 10:46 Disentangling distress 14:38 Love without overexposure 18:56 Suffering effectively 20:27 Practice instructions 40:43 Freedom in restraint
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21698

item.title: Love & Rest Daylong - Between Nihilism and Frenzy
item.description:
How do you love without clinging, and still not turn away from grief? The talk moves through that tension by asking what makes pain worthwhile, what makes it wasted, and how compassion and equanimity can balance one another without becoming either collapse or coldness. Along the way, it lingers with the idea that disenchantment is not nihilism, and that a wide view can hold heartbreak, helplessness, and care at once.
Chapters: 00:00 Wholesome Pain 02:54 Suffering Efficiently 04:49 Love Without Control 07:03 Care And Grief 12:21 Compassion And Equanimity 17:37 Vastness And Dukkha 25:17 Refining Compassion 29:22 Living With Change
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21700

The talk moves through that tension by asking what makes pain worthwhile, what makes it wasted, and how compassion and equanimity can balance one another without becoming either collapse or coldness. Along the way, it lingers with the idea that disenchantment is not nihilism, and that a wide view can hold heartbreak, helplessness, and care at once.
Chapters: 00:00 Wholesome Pain 02:54 Suffering Efficiently 04:49 Love Without Control 07:03 Care And Grief 12:21 Compassion And Equanimity 17:37 Vastness And Dukkha 25:17 Refining Compassion 29:22 Living With Change
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21700

item.title: Dharmette: Personality Disorder at the Individual and National Level
item.description:
What happens when the rigid, defensive patterns we call personality disorder are seen not just as individual pathology, but as an extreme form of egoic life? The talk moves from a clinical reflection on borderline and narcissistic dynamics into Dharma language about self-view, comparison, cruelty, and the way greed, hate, and delusion can pull both clinicians and ordinary people off balance. It then widens further to politics and social media, suggesting that staying sane requires non-clinging, wholesome action, spiritual friendship, and a long view of justice and love.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening With Candid Presence 00:36 Seeing Thick Suffering 01:38 Ego Taken To Extremes 03:08 Rigidity And Self-View 05:19 Comparison And Cruelty 07:10 Clinician In The Current 09:03 From Patients To Politics 12:12 How To Stay Sane
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21565

item.title: Attention and Anicca, Awareness and Anatta (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when attention is treated as love, then tested against impermanence and the self’s habit of taking over? The talk begins with listening and the wish to care better for others, then moves through Simone Weil, Buddhist and scientific accounts of attention, and a practical distinction between attention and awareness. From there it turns toward how practice works beneath conscious report, how effort can quietly strengthen selfing, and why letting go of control may open into a quieter kind of knowing.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening into listening 06:07 Attention as prayer 09:37 Conscious experience 12:23 Below awareness 20:00 How attention works 25:55 Aiming and sustaining 29:02 Impermanence and selfing 35:17 Resting in awareness 39:52 Non-identification and love
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86636/

item.title: Craving, Compulsion, Non-Freedom (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if freedom is not the removal of pain, but the easing of compulsive grasping that keeps turning pain into a whole world? The talk moves from the retreat setting and the usual hindrances into a close look at craving, the second noble truth, and the way suffering and wanting can feed each other in a loop. Along the way, it distinguishes craving from desire, brings in images of the flood, the donkey of habit, and the mind’s endless reach for “next,” then points toward a less braced, less self-made way of practice.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Context 04:45 Seeing Dukkha Clearly 10:48 Freedom and Compulsivity 19:24 Craving and the Unlived Life 26:10 Training a Different View 35:00 Language and Selfing 41:23 Before Clinging 44:19 Non-Striving Closure
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86614/

item.title: Dharmette: Crowdsourced Themes - Death, Grief, Anxiety, Love
item.description:
What happens when death is not treated as a distant idea but as a practice of looking squarely at finitude, grief, and fear? The talk moves from a vivid memory of standing on a roof cleaning skylights and not feeling ready to die, to a close look at how mortality can strip away what feels urgent and reveal what is actually consoling. From there it turns toward anxiety, pre-grief, and the way the three characteristics may first be felt as loss before they become liberating, ending with a wider view of suffering and the need to act with compassion now.
Chapters: 00:09 Listener themes 00:58 Facing mortality 02:53 Ram Dass and clarity 05:00 Pre-grieving loss 06:16 Not being surprised 08:15 Grief as Dharma 10:38 Too much suffering 12:40 Compassion and seeds
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21465

item.title: Dharmette: What is the Function of a Dharma Talk?
item.description:
What is a Dharma talk actually for: to give information, to steady faith, to unsettle the mind, or to create a shared field of attention? The talk moves through a canonical teaching on how Dharma should be offered, then widens into a quieter argument that a talk can function as co-meditation, a space where teacher and listeners meet through sincerity, resonance, and mutual listening. Along the way, it asks the listener to notice that not understanding everything may be part of the learning, and that the words only matter insofar as they open experience rather than close it down.
Chapters: 00:00 Starting the Question 01:42 The Classical Counsel 02:59 More Than Information 04:55 Faith and Uncertainty 07:05 Gladden the Mind 09:39 Attention as Care 12:00 Clearing Space 14:02 Transmission and Sangha
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21413

item.title: Plasticity, Call off the search, Unification and Love
item.description:
What if suffering is all of one piece, but freedom appears in many forms? The talk moves from Tolstoy and Buddhist maps of clinging into a quieter argument that practice makes us more plastic: less rigid in self-story, less trapped in emotional replay, and more able to meet dependent arising as it is. From there it turns toward “calling off the search,” unification across the different roles of life, and a love that is less about comparison than about a vast, measureless inclusion.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 01:05 Suffering and freedom 05:18 Four neighborhoods 07:11 Plasticity and flexibility 11:36 Loose self-story 16:23 Mind before the world 20:23 Calling off the search 27:37 Unification and breadth 34:16 Love without measure 39:42 Prophetic vastness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86542/

item.title: Emptiness of Self
item.description:
What if self is something we keep mistaking for a solid thing, when the talk keeps returning to it as empty, open, and responsive? The first half works through listening as the basic practice movement: listening to the body, to emotion, to pain, and finally to everything without freezing experience into an identity. In the Q&A, the talk turns to innocence, spiritual friendship, and how equanimity can keep compassion from becoming controlling or over-involved, then closes with practical advice about staying with practice through transition instead of dropping out of the container.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:55 Listening as practice 05:12 Empty of self 09:47 Body and energy 15:58 Evenly hovering attention 19:42 Deep listening 28:38 Anchor everywhere 36:52 Innocence and not-self 40:30 Friendship with the moment 45:07 Equanimity and care
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86540/

item.title: Balancing Equanimity and Compassion
item.description:
The talk begins with steadiness: settling the body, and noticing how the steadiness of love depends on attention to the world around us. From there it moves into a guided compassion practice—bringing to mind someone who is suffering, sensing their pain without defense, and offering simple blessings such as, “May your suffering be eased.” The second half widens the frame to equanimity, asking how love can stay open without becoming frantic or collapsing in the face of helplessness, gravity, and the limits of what can be changed. It closes by bringing that balance into the everyday gesture of walking and looking down at the ground, letting love flow into the gaze.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and Intention 01:30 Preparing the Heart 05:32 Meeting Suffering 12:25 Offering Love 18:17 Shared Human Ground 20:28 Compassion and Equanimity 27:21 Refining Compulsion 30:39 Love in Daily Life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86538/

item.title: Grace, Gravity, Surrender and Silence
item.description:
What if practice is less about mastering experience than learning how to fall without being destroyed? Using Leonard Cohen’s image of hanging between gravity and grace, the talk moves from suffering and helplessness into surrender, faith, and the kind of receptivity that lets silence become less threatening. It then widens into a careful look at how fear drives control, story-making, and selfing, and how trust, sangha, and non-knowing can make the heart more at ease inside impermanence.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with Cohen 01:33 Gravity and grace 03:47 What we do not own 06:54 Falling and surrender 10:37 Augustine and receptivity 14:57 Effort, helplessness, faith 21:10 Letting go gradually 28:10 Silence and safety 37:17 Falling into freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86536/

item.title: Relaxing the Body and Mind
item.description:
What does it mean to truly relax the body without trying to make experience pleasant? The talk begins with simple instructions to soften the face, jaw, belly, shoulders, and attention itself, then turns to the subtler strain of the mind’s habit of acquiring, defending, and managing experience. From there, it opens into a fuller way of working with breath, body, and walking practice, including how to meet despair or restlessness with more space rather than more effort.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:01 Begin with release 01:01 Spine, shoulders, belly 03:21 Relaxing is not pleasant 05:36 The mind at ease 10:08 Breath received 14:54 Whole-body breathing 24:49 Notice the meditating mind 26:11 Walking practice and despair 37:51 Space, sangha, awareness
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Metta for Self
item.description:
What if self-love is not a soft sentiment but a steady way of meeting suffering, goodness, joy, and helplessness? The talk moves from the four Brahma-viharas as four species of love into a practical account of loving-kindness: how it can cultivate kindness, narrow and settle the mind, or bring grief, rage, numbness, and fear to the surface. It then turns into a guided offering toward the self, using phrases like “may I be happy” and the image of a merciful gaze that sees innocence without fixing on flaws.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:34 Love and human life 01:51 Four kinds of love 04:00 How metta works 06:29 What metta reveals 09:25 Finding your own path 12:28 A merciful gaze 18:10 Blessing the self 24:52 Problems dissolving 29:14 What has not been loved
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86532/

item.title: Dharmette: Between Gravity and Grace
item.description:
How do you keep practicing when life feels like falling? Using Leonard Cohen’s language of gravity and grace, the talk moves from the inevitability of dukkha to the quieter question of why anyone would stay on the path at all. It doesn’t offer practice as comfort or self-improvement, but as a way of meeting suffering honestly and discovering that wisdom and love may be the only things that matter in the end.
Chapters: 00:14 Cohen’s Gravity and Grace 03:07 Why Practice at All 05:36 Not Understanding Yet 06:37 Intrinsic Motivation 09:22 The Broken Cup 10:10 Young Life, Old Suffering 12:22 Enough Wisdom and Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21296

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (5 of 5): A Training in Loving Well
item.description:
Did I love well? The talk begins with that question and then shifts away from a Dharma framed mainly as moment-by-moment choice toward something slower: training the heart over time. From there it moves through ethical sensitivity, attention, poise, and wisdom as quiet habits that make loving well more natural and less forced. Along the way, the speaker argues that practice is less about sudden breakthrough than the gradual melting of self-deception, defensiveness, and harm in ordinary interpersonal life.
Chapters: 00:26 Did I love well? 01:22 Beyond simple choice 03:50 Small gestures of the heart 05:45 Training for relationship 08:05 Ethics and remorse 09:59 Love as attention 12:20 Impermanence makes love urgent 14:02 Stepping out of self-deception 15:45 Slow change, continued
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21259

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (4 of 5): Honoring the Neurosis/Pain of the Other
item.description:
What does it mean to meet another person’s suffering without trying to fix, control, or outrun it? The talk begins with the First Noble Truth and turns that question toward relationship: how to “comprehend dukkha” when the pain belongs to someone you love, and your care starts to tangle with fear, grief, and the urge to intervene. It moves through images of resonance, two people under one robe, and the need for both compassion and equanimity so love does not collapse into compulsive change-making. The closing note is a quieter one: tending one’s own heart may be what keeps care clear.
Chapters: 00:16 Interpersonal theme 00:51 Comprehending dukkha 02:25 Another's suffering 03:21 Love and destabilization 05:57 Resonance and relationship 07:50 Fixing versus caring 09:46 Melting and freezing 12:01 Equanimity and acceptance 15:02 A treaty between loves
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21243

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on Sexuality
item.description:
How do desire, tenderness, shame, and harm all belong to the same human terrain? The talk takes up sexuality as a neglected Dharma topic and moves through its complications without pretending it is simple: the risks of longing, the pain built into intimacy, and the possibility that attention can bring more freedom, less self-consciousness, and more honesty into sexual life. Along the way, the speaker draws on Freud, Jung, Leonard Cohen, a story by Lydia Davis, and Stephen Mitchell to ask whether erotic connection can be something other than compulsion. By the end, the question is less whether sex is peaceful than whether wisdom and love can still be found inside its disorienting pull.
Chapters: 00:13 Opening the topic 01:40 Desire and instability 03:13 Lay life and pleasure 04:59 Cost of intimacy 07:46 Attention in intimacy 09:50 Seeing and being seen 11:53 Mutuality and disorientation 14:48 Precepts and romance
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21241

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (3 of 5): Assertion and Surrender
item.description:
How do you stay open to another person without disappearing into them? The talk treats relationship as a dance of assertion and surrender, using the images of water and ice to explore when to melt toward connection and when to freeze into healthy boundaries. It moves from a retreat practice of walking toward a partner and signaling stop, slow down, or move forward into a careful reflection on seeing and being seen, the pressure of self-image, and the ethics of yes and no. Along the way, a brief reference to Charlotte Joko Beck and a retreat with Ajahn Sucitto helps ground the point that interpersonal practice is as necessary as meditation for communal life.
Chapters: 00:21 Assertion and surrender 02:27 Ice and melting 02:54 Why community practices matter 03:55 Meeting in the room 05:38 Shy, loving, and frozen 08:05 Seeing and being seen 09:51 Self dissolving 12:39 When freezing appears 15:13 Yes, no, and practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21234

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (2 of 5): Not-Knowing and the Wildness of Loving Another Person
item.description:
What happens when love is built on too much certainty? The talk moves through the tension between knowing and not knowing: how mindfulness can sharpen empathy, yet real intimacy also asks us to let others remain partly mysterious, even to ourselves. With brief turns through Simone Weil, Stephen Mitchell, and a passage from Philip Roth’s The Human Stain, the speaker returns to the same pressure point: predictability can soothe, but it can also flatten the wildness of loving another person.
Chapters: 00:18 Who Am I? 02:23 Empathy and Not-Knowing 04:53 Forgiveness Opens Space 06:16 Seeing Loved Ones Anew 08:20 Safety and Deadness 10:01 The Limits of Knowing 12:10 Wildness in Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21225
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
What happens when love is built on too much certainty? The talk moves through the tension between knowing and not knowing: how mindfulness can sharpen empathy, yet real intimacy also asks us to let others remain partly mysterious, even to ourselves. With brief turns through Simone Weil, Stephen Mitchell, and a passage from Philip Roth’s The Human Stain, the speaker returns to the same pressure point: predictability can soothe, but it can also flatten the wildness of loving another person.
Chapters: 00:18 Who Am I? 02:23 Empathy and Not-Knowing 04:53 Forgiveness Opens Space 06:16 Seeing Loved Ones Anew 08:20 Safety and Deadness 10:01 The Limits of Knowing 12:10 Wildness in Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21225

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Life (1 of 5): Introduction
item.description:
What changes when practice leaves the cushion and enters the room with other people? The talk begins with the shift from a narrow, self-referential life toward one animated by Dharma, then turns to the ways relationship reveals what we cannot see alone: our habits, our tenderness, our shame, and our need for others in healing and understanding. Through brief stories and reflections on belonging, the speaker points to interpersonal life as a place where insight is tested, softened, and made real. The invitation is simple and concrete: notice what happens to the mind when alone, and what is evoked when you are with others.
Chapters: 00:16 From Self to Dharma 01:17 Why Relationship Matters 02:30 Belonging and Wisdom 04:08 Others as Mirrors 06:07 Growing Up With Others 09:37 Practice Becomes Relational 11:41 What Generalizes 13:16 Inquiry for the Week
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21220

item.title: Dharmette: Reflection vs. Rumination
item.description:
When does reflection become the kind of thinking that keeps us stuck? The talk moves from that question into a close, practical distinction: contemplation can help us remember the teachings and extract wisdom, while rumination tries to use words to solve what only action, feeling, or letting go can resolve. Along the way, the speaker describes how repetitive thought can masquerade as insight, how language itself can become over-assertive, and why mindfulness often means approaching experience gently rather than spinning inside it. The talk closes with concrete ways to break the loop: feel the body, put something down, move, act, or lean on spiritual friendship.
Chapters: 00:09 Thoughts and Their Limits 01:54 Reflection and Rumination 03:12 What Rumination Is 04:59 Avoiding Dukkha 07:13 Language and Self-Story 10:11 Seeing Thought Fabricate 12:18 Approach and Support 14:22 Putting It Down
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21199

item.title: Sunday Dharma Talk
item.description:
Is the Eightfold Path something to be followed, something to become, or something that only makes sense after the view shifts? The talk moves from the image of an ancient road to a steady unfolding of practice, pausing over Krishnamurti’s warning about rigid paths, a friend’s simple obedience to a teacher, and the reminder that an experience is not yet a path. From there it weaves wisdom, intention, ethics, and meditation into a single training of body and mind, asking how delusion is recognized, how ambivalence is softened, and how love and non-harming grow out of understanding suffering more clearly.
Chapters: 00:30 Ancient path 01:51 Life’s hidden currents 06:46 Path and freedom 09:19 Trusting the teaching 11:25 Experience is not path 16:25 Wisdom and illusion 23:15 Refining view 29:14 Ambivalence and aspiration 34:18 Training the body
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21182

item.title: Friday Dharma Talk
item.description:
What if the main problem is not pain itself, but the compulsive reaching that keeps trying to outmaneuver it? The talk moves from a close distinction between desire and craving into the way craving divides a life: through partial relief, repeated grasping, and the sense of living beside the life one wants. Along the way it draws on neuroscience, a Buddhist translation of the Second Noble Truth, and quotations from Adam Phillips to show how wanting, thought, and selfing keep reinforcing one another. The practice arc is simple and patient: notice the loop, see what craving cannot do, and make room for steadier forms of pleasure, equanimity, and letting go.
Chapters: 00:01 Normalizing Dukkha 02:16 Seeing Suffering Clearly 05:05 Compulsivity And Cause 09:43 Craving And Desire 16:32 Wanting Versus Liking 20:34 Unlived Lives 27:04 Patience And Insight 32:26 Equanimity And Subtle Clinging 44:43 Relaxing The Self
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21171

item.title: Formal Opening Part 2 (welcome and theme)
item.description:
What if the point of retreat is not to manage experience, but to meet whatever arises without turning it into a problem? The talk moves from that basic shift into a clear frame for practice: slowing down, keeping close to the body and breath, and letting mindfulness carry through ordinary movements as well as sitting. From there, it opens into a larger view of the retreat as a Four Noble Truths practice, then into refuge and the five precepts as ways of finding shelter, trust, and ease in a changing world.
Chapters: 00:00 Meeting What Arises 02:01 Mindfulness Sets the Pace 04:55 From Problem to Lesson 08:37 Uncertainty and Clinging 16:58 Four Noble Truths Retreat 21:09 What Refuge Means 24:24 Open Door Refuge 30:59 Refuges and Precepts 44:00 Sealing the Practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21141

item.title: Dharmette: Meaning and Purpose
item.description:
If you do not know what port you are sailing toward, no wind is favorable: the talk begins with that image and turns it into a question about meaning and purpose in Dharma practice. From there, it moves through intrinsic and extrinsic motivation, arguing that meaning cannot simply be prescribed from outside and often shows up as what is already felt as inherently worth doing. The talk then grounds that idea in three basic needs—autonomy, competence, and relatedness—showing how practice can deepen choicefulness, self-understanding, and connection, and how love and relationship make life feel significant.
Chapters: 00:09 Seneca’s question 00:44 Why meaning matters 02:57 Intrinsic motivation 05:42 Autonomy and freedom 08:42 Competence and understanding 11:25 Relatedness and love 13:44 A life less centered
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21105

item.title: Moral Elevation, Moral Disgust and the Vastness Required to Open to Dukkha
item.description:
How can a heart stay open to dukkha without turning that pain into hatred? The talk moves from moral elevation — the ordinary courage of Wesley Autrey on the subway tracks — into the harder terrain of moral disgust, public brutality, and the danger of letting ethical care collapse into aversion or cynicism. From there it keeps widening the frame: self-distancing, mortality, the cessation of becoming, and Ajahn Chah’s broken cup all point toward a more spacious way of holding suffering, gratitude, and love.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Heart 01:02 Wesley Autry's Courage 04:05 Sensitivity to Goodness 06:53 Moral Disgust 14:13 Prophetic Speech 19:22 Distance from Rumination 24:25 Widening the Horizon 30:18 Already Broken
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21112

item.title: Introduction to Theme - Abiding in Care
item.description:
What if practice is less about fixing the mind than learning how to abide with care? The talk moves from the ordinary swing between feeling good and feeling bad into a broader view of the path: love, anger, purification, and the way the heart rests when it stops trying to control experience. It then unfolds the Brahma-viharas as different forms of love, from kindness and compassion to sympathetic joy and equanimity, before landing on a quieter instruction to bow to this moment and let the species of love it calls forth reveal itself.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the day 04:39 Secret philosophy 10:34 Anger and its limits 14:23 The Brahma-viharas 24:33 Understanding as love 30:30 Many paths to care 37:27 Non-violence toward experience 46:28 Closing reminders
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21111

item.title: Dharmette: Measurement and the Immeasurable
item.description:
What happens when the mind tries to make everything into a comparison, a measurement, or a fixed identity? The talk moves from the pain of comparing self with others—envy, pride, shame, and the habit of turning people into things—to a subtler critique of measurement itself as a source of suffering. From there, it opens into the immeasurable: loving-kindness, compassion, joy, and equanimity as a way of being that does not divide the world into here and there, better and worse.
Chapters: 00:12 Comparing mind 01:18 Pride and shame 02:50 Envy and illusion 04:58 Beyond comparison 06:23 Defilements as measurement 08:28 The immeasurables 10:05 Pervading the cosmos 12:04 Relief in letting go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21064

item.title: Dharmette: Craving>Suffering, Suffering>Craving
item.description:
What if craving is not just drawn toward objects, but is itself a condition that turns up whenever suffering feels unbearable? The talk follows that loop carefully: craving and clinging lead to suffering, but suffering also feeds craving, making the cycle feel intimate, self-reinforcing, and hard to see from inside. Along the way, the speaker brings in Buddhist causality and a brief scientific parallel from addiction research to show how the mind can keep reaching for relief while only deepening its own dissatisfaction. The practical turn is quiet and direct: notice the disappointment, the withdrawal, the partial heartbreak, and learn to stay without moving immediately into grasping.
Chapters: 00:30 Opening question 01:31 Dharma as causality 03:06 Suffering as cause 03:50 The reverse loop 05:26 Craving from within 07:14 What we really want 08:36 Addiction parallel 11:47 Working with craving 14:09 Enough already
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20988

item.title: Dharmette: The Wholesome Disorientation of Feedback
item.description:
What if feedback is less a verdict than a mirror for self-clinging? The talk begins with the strange, useful sting of criticism: even when feedback is off-base, it can expose defensiveness, and defensiveness, in this frame, points to clinging that can harm self and other. From there the speaker moves through a disarming story about being seen as a "middle manager of Kinko's," then broadens into a map of practice through head, heart, and belly, using the image of growth as something that can be startling, even when it opens what we thought we already knew about ourselves.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening with feedback 01:10 Defensiveness and anatta 02:37 The Kinko's story 05:19 Head, heart, belly 07:31 Teachers and students 08:46 Growth we miss 10:28 Startled by change 12:21 Love beyond discovery
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20922

item.title: Dharmette: Between Everything Means Everything and Nothing Means Anything
item.description:
How do you stay open to heartbreak without collapsing into either melodrama or cynicism? The talk moves from adolescent disillusionment with institutions and a felt pull toward “get me out” thinking, into a careful distinction between disenchantment and nihilism. From there it widens into a “point of view of the universe” that can hold suffering, uncertainty, and not-self with a more spacious love. The result is not a call to detach from life, but to see how equanimity can become love in the face of helplessness.
Chapters: 00:12 Early disappointment 02:01 Dukkha at the group level 03:59 Mesmerized by samsara 05:57 Beyond nihilism 07:26 The universe's view 09:01 Emptiness and poignancy 10:40 Love without control 12:22 Equanimity as love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20865

item.title: Not-self
item.description:
What if the real problem is not a wounded self that needs endless repair, but the way selfing keeps tightening around experience? The talk moves from psychotherapy’s limits, through present-moment experience, comparison, shame, and truthfulness, toward a quieter view of practice: not trying to engineer a better self, but loosening ownership and seeing how awareness is already there. Along the way, the speaker uses images like narrative gravity, egoic balance, and deep silence to show how not-self can feel less like disappearance than a release from strain.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 01:17 Healing has limits 04:03 What experience is like 08:35 Narrative gravity 13:28 Why self is tiring 17:26 Shame and comparison 21:53 Humor and fluidity 25:20 Truthfulness and shame 30:20 Practice without a doer
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86530/

item.title: Healing the Self
item.description:
Is healing the self something that comes before self-forgetting, or can the two unfold together? The talk moves through that question by returning again and again to acceptance, self-love, and the ways old pain makes letting go feel unsafe. From there it broadens into a practical view of Dharma as a healing context: a place where the heart can soften, the past can be digested, and a more flexible, less brittle sense of self can emerge. Along the way, the speaker draws on Suzuki Roshi, Marsha Linehan, Freud, and others to show how change depends on deep acceptance rather than self-harshness.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Three modes of healing 04:21 Why letting go feels risky 10:46 Insight and backlash 17:14 Healing as acceptance 24:37 What we really want 32:22 Story, presence, and the past 39:48 Too much to digest 43:25 A stable, flexible self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86528/

item.title: Throughlines of Self and Not-Self
item.description:
How do self and not-self become a lived flexibility rather than a doctrine to defend? The talk begins by reframing practice as a path between pleasantness and necessary discomfort, then moves into a practical argument that the fruit of practice is less suffering, more fulfillment, and a heart that can respond differently to changing conditions. From there, it turns to the balance between self and not-self, using the themes of safety and power to warn against rigid identity, spiritual bypassing, and communities that lose their footing. The latter half focuses on two supports for this balance—renunciation and patience—showing how saying no can make yes trustworthy, and how gradual practice can harden into traits of ease, openness, and care.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 01:17 Pleasure and suffering 03:08 The fruit of practice 05:42 Necessary suffering 10:20 Stress reduction and self 13:33 Flexibility of awareness 20:14 Power and safety 24:26 Renunciation and enough 30:43 Patience through the flood
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86526/

item.title: Love and Space
item.description:
The talk begins by warning not to look for redemption in words, then turns toward a quieter task: returning attention to what lies within. From there it moves through embodiment, the habit of measuring and sorting experience, and the pressure to keep building a story of “me,” offering instead a wider heart that can welcome the whole of experience. A repeated refrain—lay it down—keeps bringing the listener back from self-making toward a more open, less claustrophobic way of being.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening orientation 03:51 Body and earth 07:48 Dropping the categories 10:16 Measuring falls away 16:55 Lay it down 27:47 Autobiography grows remote 35:13 Meaning and no meaning 39:11 Closing reflections
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86529/

item.title: Deep Embodiment
item.description:
What if the body’s uneasy signal to “fix something” is only a false alarm? The talk begins with gravity, breath, and the simple felt sense of being supported, then keeps returning to how sensation carries pleasantness, risk, and the urge to manage experience. From there it moves toward a harder relinquishment: letting go of measurement, story, and the need to improve life, and instead resting in aliveness, uncertainty, and the generosity of the present moment.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling Into Gravity 01:16 Breath In The Body 03:53 Front And Back 08:33 Aliveness Without Story 12:56 Leaving Measurement Behind 18:47 The Urge To Fix 25:15 Receiving This Moment 34:16 Rewrite The Autobiography 38:18 Announcements
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86527/
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
What if the body’s uneasy signal to “fix something” is only a false alarm? The talk begins with gravity, breath, and the simple felt sense of being supported, then keeps returning to how sensation carries pleasantness, risk, and the urge to manage experience. From there it moves toward a harder relinquishment: letting go of measurement, story, and the need to improve life, and instead resting in aliveness, uncertainty, and the generosity of the present moment.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling Into Gravity 01:16 Breath In The Body 03:53 Front And Back 08:33 Aliveness Without Story 12:56 Leaving Measurement Behind 18:47 The Urge To Fix 25:15 Receiving This Moment 34:16 Rewrite The Autobiography 38:18 Announcements
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86527/

item.title: Dharmette: Non-clinging
item.description:
What does it actually mean to stop clinging when clinging is wired into desire, habit, and even love? The talk begins with a simple question—how to overcome clinging—and then resists any neat technique, arguing that letting go is less an algorithm than a knot of conditions: renunciation, energy, resolve, patience, and equanimity working together. Along the way, the speaker uses Joseph Goldstein, Shantideva, the Buddha’s image of anger’s honeyed tip and poisoned root, and even a brief Freud reference to show why release can require both giving something up and reinvesting life elsewhere.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions 01:32 What Clinging Means 02:53 Why No Simple Method 04:18 Conditions and Consent 05:56 Reinforcement and Habit 08:29 The Paramis of Release 12:10 Reinvesting Energy 14:01 Living With Loss
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20759

item.title: Mindfulness, Mindlessness, Rumination and the Species of Unconsciousness
item.description:
What if mindfulness is less about forcing the mind to stay still, and more about noticing when thought has become sticky, urgent, or self-sealing? The talk moves from a quiet guided practice into a careful map of mindlessness, rumination, and the ways attention can either narrow distress or open space around it. Along the way, the speaker distinguishes harmless drifting from the more corrosive loops that feed suffering, and offers a practical response: soothe the body, weaken identification with thought, and trust awareness more than the story it tells. The final third turns to questions about intrusive thoughts and breaking rumination, with a strong emphasis on remorse, compassion, and not making thought into character.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the session 01:08 Settling into practice 06:14 Breath and tranquility 12:04 Letting words loosen 21:53 Silence over explanation 39:01 Mindfulness and its facets 50:09 Less mediated by language 01:01:21 Good uses of mindlessness 01:19:56 Rumination and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/84883/

item.title: Dharmette: Crying
item.description:
What are tears doing in Dharma practice: are they private leakage, a burden, a kind of plea, or something more honest than words? The talk moves from a personal story about crying while teaching into a quiet inquiry into the different kinds of tears—grief, relief, helplessness, joy—and what they reveal about embodied practice. Drawing on research, Beckett, James Baldwin, and a remark from Ajahn Sucitto, the speaker keeps returning to the question of how tears can point back to suffering, goodness, and the need for change without becoming the last word.
Chapters: 00:12 A talk on crying 01:32 What tears might mean 03:13 Words and research 05:02 Need and helplessness 06:15 Practice opens grief 08:03 Teaching from the body 09:56 Grief, solidarity, goodness 12:00 Moral elevation 13:51 Tears are not enough
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20694

item.title: Dharmette: Love and Time
item.description:
What makes love meaningful if everything we love is finite? The talk begins with a blessing at a family rite of passage and opens into a quiet meditation on how time, loss, and grief give love its depth rather than taking meaning away. From there, it moves through the claim that finitude is what makes responsibility possible, then widens into Buddhist images of love as the glue that holds the world together and as many different forms in relational life.
Chapters: 00:09 A blessing request 00:48 Love and time 02:14 Finitude and care 03:25 Grief in love 04:43 Love across years 06:01 Time needs love 07:26 Love holds together 08:47 Many faces of love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20628

item.title: Dharmette: Functions of dharma pleasure
item.description:
What if Dharma pleasure is not a sideline, but one of the things that keeps the path alive? The talk begins with an anecdote about a longtime practitioner who says the usual emphasis on impermanence and suffering can feel like too much, and uses that opening to ask what wholesome pleasure actually does in practice. From there, it moves through several forms of Dharma pleasure—path, sangha, ethical alignment, non-shame, love, concentration, reverence—and returns again and again to the claim that this kind of happiness broadens the mind, steadies motivation, and makes room for purification. A quiet argument runs through the whole talk: pleasure need not feed craving; it can support insight, trust, and the capacity to keep going.
Chapters: 00:09 Why teach pleasure 02:07 Kinds of Dharma pleasure 03:22 Non-shame and love 05:46 Concentration and reverence 07:06 What pleasure does 09:09 Pleasure as support 12:06 Broadening awareness 13:53 Making room for pain
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20586

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on words from Ajahn Sucitto
item.description:
What if generosity is less about giving things than about recognizing how little separates one life from another? Starting from a quote by Ajahn Sucitto, the talk moves through interdependence, ethics, renunciation, discernment, persistence, and patience as practical qualities of the path. It lingers on small, concrete examples—like sharing Dharma by example, or a simple act of giving—to show how practice becomes steadier, wiser, and less driven by grasping.
Chapters: 00:20 Reading the quote 02:39 Generosity as interdependence 04:01 Sharing Dharma wisely 06:58 Ethics and kindness 09:15 Morality and renunciation 12:01 Steady persistence 14:06 Patience and timing 15:55 Closing reflections
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20531

item.title: Dharmette: Cruising Altitude and Upheaval
item.description:
The talk moves from noticing that practice unfolds in phases to a larger question: what do we do when the old story of our path can no longer hold what is happening? The speaker begins with the quieter, often invisible rhythms of practice, then lingers on grief, disillusionment, and the temptation to reduce difficult feeling too quickly into insight. From there, the talk opens into a comparison with Thomas Kuhn’s “normal science” and scientific revolution, using that image to describe how a practice life can shift from steady understanding into upheaval, disorientation, and a new way of seeing.
Chapters: 00:10 Practice in phases 02:34 Grief and spaciousness 04:22 Coming home, leaving home 06:54 Kuhn’s normal science 09:01 When the story breaks 11:27 Self-view under pressure 13:26 Revolution and rest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20469

item.title: Overconfidence As A Defilement
item.description:
What happens when certainty starts to harden into a defilement? The talk moves from anicca—change, uncertainty, and the mind’s drive to manage risk—into a close look at attachment to views, overconfidence, and the ways we quietly rationalize greed, hatred, and self-deception. Along the way, it folds in Russell, Wilde, and a recent essay on Israel and Palestine to show how language can become an accomplice to our fixed positions, and how humility and embodied awareness can loosen that grip.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with certainty 01:42 Anicca and uncertainty 07:14 Doubt over certainty 11:33 Loving our mistakes 17:05 The mind's blind spots 20:28 Confabulation and humility 27:35 Return to the body 33:10 Care without clinging 39:03 Closing reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20449

item.title: Brian Lesage, Cara Lai, Matthew Brensilver: Closing Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
The talk begins with the retreat ending, and a simple invitation to notice the body before moving into conversation, transition, and departure. From there it moves through a guided speaking exercise, reflections on how mindfulness can stay present in relationship, and a closing turn toward integration: how practice continues after retreat, not by force but by continuity, patience, and small repeated intentions. It then widens into teachings on morality, the precepts, and the way depth and healing can develop together, before ending with Sangha, forgiveness, and a final communal blessing. The closing images are understated but vivid: practice as seeds, as morning mist, as love and rest, and as something carried back into ordinary life.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Brian Lesage, Cara Lai
Chapters: 00:00 Landing in the body 17:00 Mindful speaking triads 25:25 Returning to silence 38:19 Bringing practice home 47:57 Seeds and continuity 56:58 Ethics as offering 01:04:56 Six senses and healing 01:15:08 Sangha and forgiveness 01:27:08 Closing blessing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/83917/

item.title: Language is the hammer and every problem, a nail (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
The talk starts with the raw fact that we want to be happy, and then follows how that wish turns almost every moment into a problem to manage. From there, the speaker traces how feeling tone, language, and self-story work together: restlessness or pain quickly becomes evidence about “my life,” and thinking rushes in as a way to control, interpret, and protect. The practice movement is toward simple awareness and acceptance instead of constant narration, so that some experiences can be solved while others are allowed to dissolve in openness.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the problem 00:53 Valence and feeling tone 06:03 Restlessness as data 09:56 Stories from feeling 14:14 Language as hammer 21:16 Mind-wandering and self 26:46 Awareness over solving 36:25 Acceptance and softening 43:07 Closing with discernment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/83885/
