item.title: Dharmette: Coming Home and Leaving Home
item.description:
Dharma practice is framed here as a double movement: coming home to what the heart already knows, and leaving home to learn how to love what is new. The talk moves from recollection and refuge to the harder question of transformation—why change usually cannot be forced by will alone, but grows through small gestures, repeated practice, and revised priorities. A brief story from Alfred Adler and a quotation from Agnes Callard help sharpen that argument, while the closing image is quiet and practical: we rejoice in one home in order to leave it.
Chapters: 00:18 Coming Home 01:13 Recognition and Refuge 03:23 Loving New Things 04:18 More Than Willpower 06:46 How Values Shift 09:01 Small Gestures 10:24 A New Home
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20354

item.title: Dharmette: Fear and Language
item.description:
How does fear recruit language to make uncertainty feel manageable? The talk moves from a fondness for words and a skepticism about treating silence as inherently purer, into a close look at how thought, labeling, and strategizing can arise as self-soothing responses to anicca. From there it turns toward a quieter possibility: letting go of the need to solve, measure, and narrate experience, and learning to trust moments when awareness is enough.
Chapters: 00:09 Words and nourishment 01:23 Silence and language 03:06 Fear beneath thought 04:50 Anicca and uncertainty 07:14 Clinging and self 09:37 No problems 11:31 Trusting release 13:36 Closing blessings
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20282

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on a RJ Davidson Quote
item.description:
What if the real work of mindfulness is not to improve thought, but to become less captured by it? Drawing on a condensed account from R. J. Davidson and colleagues, the talk moves through sustained attention, meta-awareness, de-reification, non-reactivity, and the softening of self-story. Along the way, it treats impermanence as something the mind feels as risk, and shows how practice can loosen both the pull of thought and the grip of “me.”
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the frame 01:03 Davidson's summary 03:08 Less mediated by thought 04:19 Attention and distraction 06:40 Risk and re-entry 08:14 Seeing thought as thought 10:05 Equanimity and permission 11:51 Self-story loosens 14:08 Less self, more life
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20240

item.title: Dharmette: The Clinging Before Clinging
item.description:
What if clinging begins long before obvious craving or aversion? The talk traces that quieter layer, where mind is already shaping experience through sankharas, attention, and the habit of making the world more solid than it is. From there, practice becomes a matter of noticing how self, comparison, and narration are built — and how they can begin to loosen.
Chapters: 00:12 Returning to old loves 00:48 Mind and idealism 02:12 Experience is shaped 03:25 Clinging before clinging 05:00 Models that never idle 06:39 Formative energies 08:03 Making the world solid 10:44 Relinquishment and simplicity 13:03 Wonder without selfing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20194

item.title: Dharmette: Becoming Unintimidated by Suffering
item.description:
What changes when suffering is met without fear, fixing, or shame? The talk moves from the sacred-and-ordinary fact of shared pain to the ways shame teaches us to hide, then toward a more steady kind of witnessing: being able to say, “I’m unafraid of your pain,” without diminishing it. Drawing on Norman Fischer and the Buddha’s instruction to comprehend dukkha, the speaker argues that real empathy begins with knowing our own pain vividly in awareness, until it becomes less intimidating and more human.
Chapters: 00:09 Meeting pain 02:12 Pain and shame 05:16 Witnessing without fixing 06:46 Norman Fischer on compassion 08:47 Not missing dukkha 10:30 Awareness and space 12:17 Innocence and confidence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20160

item.title: Dharmette: New Questions
item.description:
The talk begins with the question of how the Dharma is actually learned, not just explained: how it gets woven into a life, and how our minds change in the presence of others. From there it moves between homecoming and unsettlement, using the image of being both settled and "thrown out of the nest" to show why practice needs a balance of comfort and disequilibrium. It closes by asking whether Dharma can give us words for new questions about self, love, death, and what kind of peace is possible.
Chapters: 00:10 Opening and return 01:09 Who speaks, who listens 03:02 Sangha and the mind 04:46 How learning happens 05:59 Impasses and balance 07:57 Home and exile 10:06 Cracks in the frame 12:23 Words for new questions
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20106

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Letting Go (5 of 5)
item.description:
How do you let go when letting go feels like risk, vulnerability, even neglect? The talk moves through that tension by arguing that Dharma practice asks for courage: to stop over-reading experience, to release familiar reference points, and to trust a descent into not-knowing. Drawing on William James, Nisargadatta Maharaj, and Ajahn Sucitto, it frames awakening as a gradual relinquishment of the personal world in favor of a quieter kind of power and dignity.
Chapters: 00:09 James and tragic depth 02:13 What the path offers 03:31 Trying something new 04:54 Risk of letting go 06:47 Power in helplessness 07:55 Effective suffering 09:08 The rope of release 11:31 Fewer signposts 13:09 Faith and gratitude
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20006
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
How do you let go when letting go feels like risk, vulnerability, even neglect? The talk moves through that tension by arguing that Dharma practice asks for courage: to stop over-reading experience, to release familiar reference points, and to trust a descent into not-knowing. Drawing on William James, Nisargadatta Maharaj, and Ajahn Sucitto, it frames awakening as a gradual relinquishment of the personal world in favor of a quieter kind of power and dignity.
Chapters: 00:09 James and tragic depth 02:13 What the path offers 03:31 Trying something new 04:54 Risk of letting go 06:47 Power in helplessness 07:55 Effective suffering 09:08 The rope of release 11:31 Fewer signposts 13:09 Faith and gratitude
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20006

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Ourselves (4 of 5)
item.description:
What does it really mean to have faith in oneself if it is not a polished story of who we are? The talk argues that confidence grows when the static of self-making loosens: when we stop leaning on grand self-images, and instead learn to trust direct knowing, bodily cues, and the small yes-or-no signals that show what is wholesome. It moves from the limits of self-esteem to a quieter kind of accountability to what we already know.
Chapters: 00:12 Self-esteem revisited 01:41 Faith beyond story 03:19 Not-self and confidence 05:23 Trusting body and mind 06:45 Intuitive knowing 08:15 Less self-deception 09:40 Subtle cues and data 11:14 Accountable to knowing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19994

Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19994

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Love (3 of 5)
item.description:
The talk begins with a tender poem about parting and the raw, familiar feeling of love, then turns toward a larger question: how do we trust love when aversion and hatred can feel so forceful? From there, the speaker argues that faith is not blind certainty but a willingness to keep looking past anger, following the causes and conditions of feeling instead of stopping at resentment. The movement of the talk is practical and patient: examine emotion closely, notice its ending, and see whether it leads not to hate but to kindness, compassion, joy, and equanimity.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Poem 01:07 Faith and Doubt 02:02 Love Against Aversion 04:17 Open-Hearted Happiness 06:20 Anger’s Dead End 07:59 Dependent Arising 10:27 Emotion and Need 11:46 Following to Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19985

item.title: Dharmette: Emerging From Doubt
item.description:
What happens when the mind keeps asking for more certainty than life can give? The talk traces doubt as a kind of disorientation: the search for data, the swing between stories, the pressure to make practice guarantee a dukkha-free outcome, and the way that craving for certainty can deepen confusion. It then turns toward faith as something quieter and more practical—trusting direct experience, loosening the need to perform or to get it exactly right, and finding a steadier way to keep going.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening and Theme 00:57 Doubt as Disorientation 03:01 Narratives and Rumination 05:01 What Practice Cannot Do 07:38 Precision and Letting Go 10:04 Trusting Experience 10:59 Faith as a Hinge 12:15 Closing Encouragement
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19971

item.title: Faith in Our Dharma Path (1 of 5)
item.description:
Faith here is not presented as belief without evidence, but as a way of loosening defensiveness and letting the path touch the heart. The talk traces a personal movement from suspicion of the word to the sense that surrender, openness to all experience, and trust in awareness are what make deeper practice possible. It also asks what it means to remain honest about suffering without pretending, while still believing that truth and love can catch us in the falling.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the Theme 00:46 From Suspicion to Surrender 02:37 What Faith Is Not 04:35 Dropping Defensiveness 06:19 The Path in the Heart 08:09 Truth Without Harm 10:10 Falling into Trust 12:16 Closing Blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19961

item.title: Not-self and self expression
item.description:
What happens when the mask we wear to be someone starts to harden into a prison? The talk follows that tension from social performance and shame into a gentler reading of not-self: not as becoming nobody, but as loosening clinging so personality can be lived more freely. Along the way, it links inner rigidity to defensiveness and harm, then turns toward a quieter kind of self-acceptance that feels more real, more embodied, and more available for relationship.
Chapters: 01:02 Masks and vulnerability 02:52 Self-knowledge, not fixation 07:04 When masks harden 12:51 Self-view and relationship 17:34 Not being nobody 22:18 Shame and innocence 26:21 Interpersonal selfing 34:51 Freedom feels real 42:59 Seeing through and resting
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19674

item.title: Mindfulness, Mind-wandering, and Equanimity
item.description:
Mindfulness here begins not as calm, but as learning how the mind actually moves: how it wanders, why it seeks relief, and how quickly it gets identified with thought. The talk traces that movement from attention and distraction into de-reification, non-reactivity, and finally equanimity—the quality that lets pain crest and soften without becoming more story. Along the way, the speaker uses research, early Buddhist text, and vivid everyday images to show why practice is less about forcing a special state than about making room for experience without being enclosed by it.
Chapters: 00:27 Mindfulness as learning 01:53 What we actually want 04:50 The allure of unconsciousness 08:18 Mind wandering as baseline 14:11 Continuity without strain 22:27 Anchoring and noticing 30:16 Thought, space, and reactivity 38:20 Equanimity and release 44:29 Less story, more life
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19654

item.title: Dharmette: Compassion & Equanimity
item.description:
What happens when compassion opens all the way to suffering, and then begins to fatigue? The talk moves through that question by pairing compassion with equanimity: first staying present with dukkha without blaming or turning away, then recognizing that the heart also needs rest, steadiness, and a quieter kind of love. Along the way, the speaker uses images of flowers opening and closing, and argues that equanimity is not indifference but a way of meeting helplessness without closing the heart.
Chapters: 00:55 Teaching with nuance 02:53 Compassion and suffering 05:26 Why we help 06:32 From grief to love 08:18 When compassion tires 09:06 Equanimity as resting 12:36 Quiet love and rest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19440

item.title: Dharmette: Passivity & Action
item.description:
Is equanimity really a kind of passivity, or can receptivity make action wiser? The talk argues that retreat-like experiments in letting go are not an end in themselves, but a way to see what our habits cost, what they buy us, and how to act with more clarity. From there it moves into impermanence, craving, self-knowledge, and a practical "theory of love" that helps us understand our own limits and other people’s fragility.
Chapters: 00:12 Equanimity Is Not Apathy 01:25 Passivity in Service 02:25 Retreat as Experiment 04:30 Action Becomes More Effective 06:22 Seeing Impermanence Clearly 08:06 Forecasting and Love 10:11 Knowing Our Limits 12:17 Respecting Others Carefully 14:24 A Virtuous Cycle
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19426

item.title: Dharmette: Disenchantment & Engagement
item.description:
How can disenchantment with samsara and care for the world belong together? The talk moves from the vulnerability of aging, sickness, and death into a case for seeing through the ordinary promises of acquiring and becoming, using the Bahhiya Sutta to point to a freedom found in non-clinging rather than escape. From there it turns to samvega, the urgency that comes from recognizing how much we expect the world to supply, and then to the question of how that turning away can open more space for compassion and engagement. It ends with the image of one foot out of the world and one foot deeply in it, shaped by Ryokan’s wish to gather up suffering without pretending it can all be contained.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the Theme 01:10 Human Vulnerability 02:22 Sensory Experience 02:53 Bahhiya Sutta 04:03 Lay Path Questions 06:52 Samvega and Urgency 09:01 What Is Enough 10:29 Disenchantment Into Care 13:28 Ryokan’s Wish
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19423

item.title: Dharmette: Effort & Effortlessness
item.description:
How much effort does practice need, and when does effort start to become self-defeating? The talk moves from the necessity of renouncing easy habits and seeing the “honey” in painful mind states, to the risk of turning practice into sheer willpower and self-striving. It then turns toward a quieter kind of training: forming habits that make the nervous system an ally, so wholesome action and awareness become more natural, less forced, and eventually more effortless.
Chapters: 00:17 Opening the pair 00:55 Why effort matters 02:44 Renunciation and honey 04:32 When effort becomes selfing 07:28 Limits of willfulness 10:09 William James on habit 11:16 Effortless awareness 13:31 Practice as conditioning
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19405

item.title: Dharmette: Confidence & Humility
item.description:
What happens when confidence and humility are not opposites, but two qualities that have to be held together? The talk moves from the idea that wisdom changes with context, to the ways practice can be misused by picking the wrong teaching, and then into a careful unfolding of how confidence grows through experience rather than certainty. By the end, humility is framed not as self-doubt, but as openness to what is actually here—and as a safeguard against violence when we forget how often we can be wrong.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and framing 01:05 Wisdom needs context 02:38 Learning the right tilt 03:49 Confidence from practice 04:58 Practice confirmed 06:44 Enoughness and calibration 08:25 Humbled by realization 10:10 Openness without certainty 11:53 Tentative knowing, nonviolence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19398

item.title: Welcome - Abiding in Care
item.description:
What happens when practice stops feeling like a project and starts feeling like a way of abiding? The talk moves from a reflection on ambition and purpose into a quieter sense of Dharma as a network of care, where the future loosens, the past settles, and attention can rest more closely in the present. After opening the room to shared intention and a period of sitting, it turns more explicitly embodied, emphasizing becoming available to the Dharma through the body, tenderness, and the ordinary textures of love and care. Later, it brings wisdom and love together as inseparable, arguing that understanding deepens through love rather than moving away from it.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening Welcome 00:47 Life Less Managed 03:03 Ambition and Vision 04:44 A Record of Care 07:23 Living Into Practice 10:52 Entering Sangha 17:23 The Form of Love 22:07 Becoming Available 31:44 Wisdom and Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19366

item.title: Dharmette 2 - Love, Helplessness, Equanimity, and Affect
item.description:
What if compassion begins not with certainty, but with helplessness? The talk moves from an early Buddhist image of fear, the world’s lack of essence, and the thorn in the heart into a reading of love as something costly, partial, and inseparable from grief. From there it asks how equanimity can steady the heart at the end of our power, how much of our caring is actually being spent wisely, and how finitude can sharpen presence instead of only producing dread.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Setup 01:11 Fear and the Thorn 04:27 Love and Helplessness 08:20 Widening Empathy 11:36 Compassion Costs 14:25 Progress and Rest 18:58 Grief and Finitude 25:29 Honest Ethical Seeing 31:45 Spending Love Wisely
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19372

item.title: Dharmette 1 - Aspects of Love
item.description:
What changes when love is treated as more than a feeling, as something that alters attention, motivation, and the way the world is seen? The talk moves from a simple image of a sign about love into a close look at love as a bodily felt state, a shift in valence and arousal, and a force that can soften self-absorption and widen perception. Along the way, it brings in emotion science, the Buddha’s language of feeling tones, and a quiet emphasis on practicing metta by noticing how love reverberates in the body. It closes by naming the Brahma-viharas as a complete set of responses for whatever is met.
Chapters: 00:01 Love Beyond Feeling 01:05 Feeling and Mind State 02:36 Where Love Lives 04:59 Intensity and Quietness 07:04 Feeling Shapes Consciousness 09:32 Love Infuses the View 14:00 Space, Bias, and Fear 17:57 Practice and Return
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19367

item.title: Sila and Our Debt to Others
item.description:
What does ethical practice ask of us once we realize that avoiding overt harm is not the end of our debt to others? The talk moves from the noble eightfold path and sila into a sustained inquiry about helplessness, grief, and the limits of what one person can carry, widening at times into Parfit, Dōgen, Toby Ord, and practical examples of giving. It keeps returning to the same balancing act: how to stay open to suffering without losing rest, and how to let love become more discerning rather than more compulsive.
Chapters: 00:00 Ancient Path 02:04 Sila and Obligation 05:35 Continuity and Wakefulness 08:26 Backward Step 12:18 Too Much To Hold 18:51 World at Risk 24:57 Love and Rest 32:50 Being of Use 40:43 Already Broken
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19243

item.title: Intensity, Feeling, Thinking as Coping, Insight
item.description:
When intensity rises in retreat, what if the impulse to explain it is already part of the coping? The talk starts from the feeling that dukkha is uniquely isolating, then moves through silence, attention, and the ways thinking and narrative try to manage arousal. From there it turns toward practices of narrowing and widening attention, a “do nothing” approach, and equanimity as not fighting with experience, before landing on the role of story and insight in freeing the heart.
Chapters: 00:03 Living dukkha 01:48 Retreat as intensification 03:46 Energy and samadhi 06:57 Naming the intensity 12:43 Feeling at the center 18:08 Attention and soothing 24:17 Do nothing practice 31:04 Equanimity and faith 38:37 Stories and insight
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19236

item.title: Duality, Merging, Not-Self and Differentiation in the Interpersonal World (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do we stay ourselves in relationship without turning inward and closing off, or dissolving into the other? The talk moves from self-acceptance and not-self into a long, intimate reflection on face-to-face contact, shame, empathy, and the subtle ways ego shows up in love and conflict. Through a retreat exercise of walking toward another person, plus references to Bahiya, Winnicott, Carl Rogers, and Octavio Paz, the speaker explores how freedom can include both merging and differentiation. It ends by asking what it means to love with space, equanimity, and enough distance to let the other remain free.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:35 Sitting and acceptance 01:48 Not-self and lucidity 03:22 Lay life and relationship 07:55 Approaching another 11:38 The face and ethics 17:29 Self in the gaze 21:46 Seen as merely seen 25:23 Ego, safety, and refuge 31:10 Play and feeling real
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/80583/

item.title: Knowing and Not-Knowing
item.description:
What if the Dharma is not something you can fully explain, but something you keep learning to approach with fewer fixed answers? Beginning from a retreat-planning confession of “I don’t know,” the talk moves into a careful exploration of knowing and not-knowing: the difference between helpful clarity and defensive certainty, between insight as understanding and practice as a deeper training of mind and habit. It weaves together stories, Buddhist texts, and modern ideas about prediction to show how wanting shapes what we think we know, and how relinquishment can open a different kind of seeing. By the end, the emphasis is on a knowing that is informed by humility, patience, and love.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Cliffside Opening 01:53 Not Knowing as Theme 06:33 How Practice Works 08:17 Balancing the Teachings 12:53 Intimate Not-Knowing 16:26 Ignorance and Self-Knowledge 23:42 Study Yourself 29:24 Everything Teaches 35:43 Relinquishment and Surrender
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/80528/

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Randy Fernando: Navigating A.I. from a Buddhist Perspective
item.description:
What if the main question about AI is not what it can do, but what kinds of attention, craving, fear, and confusion it trains in us? The talk moves from a short grounding sit into a wide-ranging examination of generative AI, synthetic media, job disruption, climate pressure, and the way digital systems can scale greed, hatred, and delusion. It keeps returning to Buddhist frames—attention, dependent co-arising, the First Noble Truth, and the Eightfold Path—to ask what is actually being conditioned and what kind of response is still possible.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Randy Fernando
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and welcome 03:34 Attention and practice 09:44 Why AI feels urgent 15:44 Existential risk context 21:19 What AI can do 29:34 Truth, power, and misuse 42:47 Accelerating conditions 51:37 A short sit and conditions 01:04:21 Questions on capitalism
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/80127/?access_key=E1JUe6JNxh

item.title: The anchor is everywhere
item.description:
What if the anchor were not a narrow point of attention, but something so wide it included everything? The talk begins by showing two uses of an anchor: it steadies the mind, and it also makes its movements—hope, fear, obsession—more visible. From there it opens into a more spacious practice, where awareness is less about selecting one object and more about resting in an unpartitioned field of knowing, even as the mind keeps getting snagged and identified.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 What an anchor does 02:03 Balance and transparency 04:07 What if it's everywhere 11:19 From circle to field 12:29 Unpartitioned knowing 20:57 Non-identification 23:23 Forgiveness and release 29:31 Rest where awareness stays
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/77833/

item.title: Surrender & Faith (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when the instinct to manage everything is set aside and the mind stays with helplessness, imperfection, and not-knowing? The talk moves from a vivid image of “all these hands” adjusting the knobs of samsara into a close examination of how practice asks for surrender rather than willfulness. It then broadens into a quiet argument that faith makes surrender feel safe, and that mindfulness, samadhi, and wisdom all depend on some willingness to let experience teach us.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 The urge to control 02:37 A Christian contrast 04:06 Surrender in Dharma 09:45 Conditioning and effort 17:35 Helplessness and faith 24:50 Trusting the descent 31:58 Mindfulness as release 38:28 Opening to change
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/77831/

item.title: Cycling through All Four Divine Abodes
item.description:
The talk moves through the four divine abodes as a single practice of love, asking who or what most needs metta, compassion, sympathetic joy, or equanimity at each moment. It starts with the dead ends of hatred and apathy, then turns toward the heart’s capacity to take in suffering, rejoice in goodness, and meet what cannot be changed without clinging. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to the question of how love can be steady enough to include grief, helplessness, and ordinary gratitude without closing down.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the cycle 02:16 Metta and goodwill 05:04 Receiving goodness 10:18 Compassion for suffering 14:56 When grief softens 19:09 Rejoicing in delight 23:45 Equanimity and limits 28:37 Refining the heart 33:03 Closing reflection
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/77819/

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality - Fear, Helplessness & Self (4/5)
item.description:
What happens when mortality exposes the ego’s need to manage, defend, and stay in control? The talk moves from death as a humbling force to helplessness as a practice area, arguing that meditation can become a way of willingly meeting powerlessness instead of constantly trying to fix or steer experience. Along the way, the speaker links fear to the making of self, then turns to not-self as a real but limited consolation in the face of loss, ending with Derek Parfit’s image of life loosening from a glass tunnel into open air.
Chapters: 00:09 Mortality Clarifies Value 00:48 Helplessness and Selfing 01:51 Death as Humbling 03:14 The Mirage of Control 05:08 Training With Helplessness 07:29 Loss and Vastness 08:50 Self as Effect 10:49 Not-Self and Death 12:04 Parfit and Open Air
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18168

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality and the Clarification of What Matters (3/5)
item.description:
What if mortality is not a dark thought to avoid, but the thing that strips away what is false? The talk begins with the way modern life floods us with information and makes it easy to lose the plot, then turns to retreat, illness, and death as reminders that clarify what really matters. Through brief references to Jennifer Egan, David Whyte, and Roland Griffiths, the speaker keeps returning to the same pressure: when life feels limited, what still counts, and what can we finally let go?
Chapters: 00:17 Noise and Information 01:54 Losing the Plot 04:03 A Life in a Moment 05:09 Remembering Death 07:48 Wakefulness in Illness 09:25 Honey and Poison 11:38 Practicing Letting Go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18178

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality - Futurelessness & Samadhi (2/5)
item.description:
What changes when death is not only something to fear or think about, but a lens for seeing how much of the mind is organized around the next moment? The talk follows that tension from mortality and impermanence to futurelessness: the future-thirst of planning, worry, and strategy, and the quiet possibility that practice can loosen all that. Along the way, the speaker brings in William James, the Buddha’s line that “the world trembles in all directions,” and a contemporary scientific account of meditation as reducing future-thinking and even the sense of time. The result is a compact reflection on samadhi as the felt simplicity of “nothing to do but be awake.”
Chapters: 00:12 Death and practice 01:20 The pull of the future 02:52 A trembling world 04:22 Futurelessness 06:02 Only this moment 07:48 Science and samadhi 10:09 Sit like dying 12:15 How to live
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18179

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on Mortality (1/5)
item.description:
How do we learn from mortality without making it mean too much or too little? The talk begins with the idea that everything teaches us, then uses that frame to explore the tension between overlearning and underlearning from death, grief, and finitude. Along the way, the speaker reflects on a recent family death, a memory of Stephen Levine’s "Year to Live" practice, and a challenge from a Holocaust survivor neighbor that keeps the discussion grounded in lived reality. The talk ends by treating death not as a slogan or a solved problem, but as sacred ground and an open mystery that can inform practice, love, and wisdom.
Chapters: 00:12 Learning from life 00:45 Overlearning and underlearning 02:36 Mortality as teacher 04:53 Finitude and meaning 06:13 A personal confrontation 07:47 Death’s ordinary nearness 09:33 Mystery and humility 11:43 How death informs practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18165

item.title: The Three Characteristics and Love
item.description:
How do the three characteristics become more than ideas, and why does that tenderness matter? The talk moves from retreat as a strangely intimate atmosphere into a sustained reflection on dukkha, impermanence, and not-self as forces that soften the heart rather than just explain experience. Along the way, it brings in stories, neuroscience, and quoted teachings to show how suffering can be metabolized into grief, care, and a quieter kind of love.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Retreat Mind 01:46 Retreat as Recollection 05:07 Love and Receptivity 11:48 Wisdom With Love 15:07 Three Characteristics 18:14 Difficulties as Food 24:34 Grief and Change 29:59 Non-Self and Ownership 39:05 Letting Go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/77031/

item.title: Dharma Talk: Delusion on the Path
item.description:
What happens when delusion is not treated as a failure, but as the very place practice becomes visible? The talk moves from Oscar Wilde and Camus into a close look at retreat, mind-wandering, confabulation, and the ways thought quietly launders greed and aversion into something that seems true. From there, it keeps backing up: from self-view to embodiment, from willful striving to surrender, and from private certainty toward a more porous, receptive way of knowing. Throughout, the speaker returns to the pressure of not knowing and the possibility that waking up means staying teachable, even when the mind keeps making its case.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening on Deception 03:03 Retreat as Travel 06:07 What Delusion Is 13:12 Ignorance and the Plot 17:41 Unknown Unknowns 22:07 Learning from Everything 27:22 Effort and Willfulness 34:24 The Meditator as Program 39:24 Body, Feeling, and Laundering
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17994

item.title: Dharmette: Acclimatizing to Anicca
item.description:
How do you live without treating the present moment as if it were permanent? The talk moves from a plain definition of anicca as constant process into the ways the mind tries to predict, stabilize, and narrow experience so it will not be surprised. From there it turns toward a gentler response: grieving what cannot last, enjoying stability without clinging to it, and building enough inner steadiness to meet change with openness rather than brittleness.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the theme 01:19 What anicca means 02:17 Admitting resistance 03:31 The permanence illusion 05:00 Prediction and surprise 07:11 Three characteristics 09:30 Gratitude for conditions 11:35 Stability and refuge 14:08 Equanimity as refuge
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17881

item.title: Reflections on Ajahn Sucitto Talk
item.description:
What does it mean to keep the heart open when words fail, bodies break down, and there is nothing you can do to fix what is happening? The talk moves through a story of visiting an ill brother, then widens into reflections on family, mortality, and the way simple touch, chanting, and presence can still carry love when explanation cannot. From there it turns toward the strain of caring for others without becoming overwhelmed, distinguishing compassion from the impulse to control, and landing in equanimity as another kind of love. External voices from Ajahn Chah, Cormac McCarthy, Nick Cave, and Carl Rogers are used sparingly, each helping sharpen the same quiet question: how do we meet suffering without losing the as-if space that lets us respond wisely?
Chapters: 00:59 Ajahn Sucitto's visit 04:01 What remains after loss 08:09 Mortality as messenger 11:49 Body over abstraction 18:12 Kindness without words 23:11 Love made vast 28:08 Limits of compassion 33:12 Equanimity and release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17856

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma Practice & Beginning Again
item.description:
What if the path is not a straight line but a continual practice of beginning again? The talk moves through the ways practice slips: getting comfortable and then being unsettled, losing motivation, forgetting the Dharma, making messes, and finding oneself embarrassed or discouraged. From meditation on the breath to family history, relationships, and changing intentions over time, the speaker keeps returning to the same quiet move: remember, forgive, start again, and continue.
Chapters: 00:51 An Iterative Path 02:32 Beginning Again 05:21 Remembering the Dharma 06:57 Messes and Forgiveness 08:15 Relationships and Loose Ends 09:20 No Perfect Finish 10:32 Changing Intentions 12:17 Each Moment Anew
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17756

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma & Grief
item.description:
What if grief is not a problem to get beyond, but part of the way the heart learns to see? The talk moves from the Buddha’s confrontation with aging, sickness, and death into the claim that grief can accompany ethical growth, widening empathy, and the painful process of letting go. It then turns to the comfort of self-deception, using brief references to Shelley Taylor, Metzinger, Nietzsche, James Baldwin, and Pema Chodron to trace a line from mortality awareness to a grief that becomes love and wakefulness.
Chapters: 00:17 Buddha and mortality 01:54 What grief means 04:01 Grief in change 05:56 Suffering and empathy 07:26 Illusions and mortality 09:35 Grief into practice 11:27 Fluid grief 12:52 Pregnant with bodhicitta
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17754

item.title: Dharmette: Humor and Playfulness in the Dharma
item.description:
What happens when seriousness loosens its grip and the path opens into play? The talk begins with a comment about teachers and humor, then moves through a self-deprecating story about being called “Captain Buzzkill” to explore how ego makes everything feel tight, defensive, and overly solemn. From there, the speaker asks where we can still play — even with our foibles, clinging, and shame — and uses Winnicott’s ideas about play to show why humor can belong to healing and Dharma practice. The talk closes with an image of tears and deep laughter coexisting together, with a quiet sense that grief, earnestness, pleasure, and lightness can all be part of the same life.
Chapters: 00:12 Retreat update 00:46 Humor and depth 01:40 Captain Buzzkill 02:49 Seriousness and ego 04:01 What play looks like 06:34 Winnicott and therapy 08:03 Self-humor as relief 09:36 Relating to defilements 11:03 Tears and laughter
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17737

item.title: Dharmette: Surrender in the Dharma
item.description:
What happens when practice stops trying to manage everything? The talk moves from a Leonard Cohen interview, where the search itself dissolves, into a close look at surrender as a Dharma attitude: not submission, but relaxing willfulness, trusting the moment, and letting insight arise from not knowing. It holds that tension carefully against the Buddhist emphasis on verifying teachings for oneself, then unfolds surrender into mindfulness, silence, stillness, and the way effort can become just more agitation.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening with Cohen 01:05 What Surrender Means 03:08 Inquiry and Independence 04:28 The Limits of Will 05:57 Ego Out of Moves 07:50 Present-Moment Receptivity 10:11 Insight from Not Knowing 11:48 Groundlessness and Stillness 12:58 Power from Surrender
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17742

The talk moves from a Leonard Cohen interview, where the search itself dissolves, into a close look at surrender as a Dharma attitude: not submission, but relaxing willfulness, trusting the moment, and letting insight arise from not knowing. It holds that tension carefully against the Buddhist emphasis on verifying teachings for oneself, then unfolds surrender into mindfulness, silence, stillness, and the way effort can become just more agitation.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening with Cohen 01:05 What Surrender Means 03:08 Inquiry and Independence 04:28 The Limits of Will 05:57 Ego Out of Moves 07:50 Present-Moment Receptivity 10:11 Insight from Not Knowing 11:48 Groundlessness and Stillness 12:58 Power from Surrender
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17742

item.title: Dharmette: Awe in the Dharma
item.description:
What if awe is not a distraction from practice, but one of the ways the path opens? The talk begins with a definition of awe as standing before something vast, then moves through a retreat memory, the rarity of human birth, and the emptiness of convention to show how wonder can loosen clinging. From there, it turns to the practical effect: when the self feels smaller, the world feels larger, and love and wisdom have more room to grow.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the Theme 01:42 What Awe Means 02:51 Falling in Love 04:49 Human Life’s Rarity 06:39 Convention and Emptiness 08:44 Wonder Without Clinging 10:14 Small Self, Bigger World 13:39 Closing Blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17745

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Wisdom (5 of 5)
item.description:
What does it mean for wisdom to rule the other faculties, and how does it actually grow in ordinary life? The talk moves from a Buddhist framing of wisdom as the governing faculty, to a practical distinction between facts and “knowledge how,” then into the slow work of loosening delusion, becoming more porous, and letting insight generalize across life. It closes by stressing that wisdom needs quiet, supportive conditions, and repeated practice to hold and deepen.
Chapters: 00:19 Wisdom Ruling the Faculties 00:59 What Wisdom Means 02:01 Knowing How to Live 03:17 Knowledge How, Not Theory 05:37 From Insight to Generalization 07:37 Thawing Delusion 10:25 Happiness Opens the View 12:21 Reinforcing Insight 14:36 Practice It Again
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17631

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties: Samadhi (4 of 5)
item.description:
What is samadhi, and how is it different from a narrow, forceful concentration? The talk moves from the relation between mindfulness and samadhi into a gentler image of attention: not aiming and gripping, but opening, aligning, and letting the mind gather. From there it shows how settledness steadies the heart, supports faith, and makes insight more durable, so understanding can leave a deeper mark. Along the way, references to Thanissaro Bhikkhu, Ajahn Sucitto, and Shinzen Young help frame samadhi as part of an integrated practice rather than a separate technique.
Chapters: 00:09 What Samadhi Is 01:29 Not Separate Practices 03:18 Attention and Receptivity 04:53 Aligned Inner Energies 07:43 Faith Through Stillness 09:32 Letting Go, Not Forcing 11:38 Ground for Insight 14:16 Closing Encouragement
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17633

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Mindfulness (3 of 5)
item.description:
What if mindfulness is less a slogan than the opposite of being trapped inside distress? The talk begins by noting how loaded the word has become, then turns to a classical definition from the Satipatthana Sutta and reads it against the shared features of suffering: rumination, narrowed experience, fragmented attention, avoidance, and rigid self-story. From there, it sketches mindfulness as reduced fusion with thought, greater space around experience, more stable attention, less moralizing about what arises, and a lighter grip on self.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the topic 00:46 A loaded word 02:16 Satipatthana baseline 03:33 How distress feels 05:19 What suffering does 08:38 Less fusion, more space 10:07 Stability and tolerance 12:29 Less self, more wakefulness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17636

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Energy (2 of 5)
item.description:
What if effort in practice is real, but the source of that effort matters more than the force behind it? This talk moves from the Buddha’s relentless striving, through the speaker’s own history with willful practice, into a quieter question: how can energy be sourced from wisdom, relaxation, and sensitivity to goodness rather than from self-contraction? Along the way, the talk uses examples like "underdo it," habits, and the way craving keeps getting reinforced to show why sustained practice often requires less strain and more clear, steady perseverance.
Chapters: 00:13 From Faith to Energy 00:32 Doing and Non-Doing 01:30 The Buddha's Relentless Effort 02:38 When Effort Becomes Self 05:21 Witnessing the Limits 07:14 Relaxed Perseverance 08:42 Why Effort Matters 09:58 Habit and Craving 12:58 Loving the Causes Less
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17640

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Faith (1 of 5)
item.description:
Faith begins, here, not as a belief you force yourself to hold, but as a response to seeing that the old way of trying to manage life is not going to work. The talk moves from doubt and suffering into a more grounded confidence: trust in practice, trust in experience, and trust that the heart can return to its own ground. Along the way, the speaker uses images of being lost in a jungle, falling in love, and taking refuge to show faith as something alive, renewing, and less rigid than doctrine.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and Theme 00:45 Faith Begins in Doubt 01:25 Suffering as Support 02:40 Not Dogmatic Belief 04:05 Trusting Experience 04:50 Faith Like Falling In Love 06:32 Its Own Evidence 07:38 Stable Yet Living 09:17 Ease and Consolation
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17639

item.title: Non-Doing & Liberative Dependent Orignation
item.description:
What if practice is less about making something happen and more about getting out of the way? The talk moves from effort, courage, and surrender into a careful tracing of dependent origination in reverse: faith, joy, rapture, tranquility, happiness, and concentration arising when willfulness softens and the mind stops trying to control its experience. Along the way, the speaker uses a Therigatha poem, Augustine, and short remarks from later teachers to show how non-doing can still be active without becoming forced or vague.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with humility 00:51 Friendship and trust 01:59 Effort and surrender 05:03 Hands off the wheel 08:11 A map of unfolding 12:02 Faith to joy 18:39 Rapture to tranquility 25:44 Stillness and samadhi 33:22 Disenchantment deepens
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17588

item.title: Morning Instruction: Emotions
item.description:
Feeling is not treated here as a forecast, but as something to meet directly. The talk begins with sound and breath as a surrounding, soothing field, then turns to how emotions register risk, reward, and the question, “Am I okay?” Rather than extrapolating from intensity into the future, the instruction is to open to the knots, surges, longing, resistance, and dead zones as they arise, with awareness that can hold all of it without discrimination.
Chapters: 00:00 RAIN and being held 02:22 Breath in the body 07:48 Emotion as signal 10:49 Not a forecast 12:54 Opening to intensity 19:00 Am I okay? 22:27 Soothed and sustained 31:45 An experiment only
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17585

item.title: Non-doing: Receptivity & Purification
item.description:
What happens when the impulse to improve yourself turns into another form of selfing? The talk moves from the familiar project of effort, control, and “fixing” toward a quieter practice of receptivity: letting life teach, softening around craving, and noticing how perception itself changes when the heart stops gripping. From there it turns to the difficult middle of practice—pain, descent, and helplessness—not as detours, but as the place where purification, grief, and a steadier love can emerge.
Chapters: 00:00 Choice and choicelessness 04:11 Effort and willpower 11:20 The meditator program 14:11 Receptivity as practice 23:24 Wanting shapes perception 28:28 Sensitivity and vulnerability 34:13 Suffering and purification 40:27 Grief, love, release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17584

item.title: Conversation about Insight
item.description:
What if insight is not something you figure out, but something that becomes real in the body and changes how the world appears? The talk moves from the claim that insight is insufficient by itself, into an argument that practice is also training, embodied learning, and a loosening of the stories and reference points that shape perception. Through examples like the vase-and-faces illusion, a story about an old monk who knows how not to suffer, and a reflection on samadhi, the speaker explores how freedom can arise when wanting softens and the view widens.
Chapters: 00:00 Insight and Training 03:19 What Insight Really Is 07:58 Knowing and Realizing 12:27 New Feelings Enter 15:20 Enoughness and Not Knowing 18:56 Samadhi and Consolidation 27:37 Accident-Prone Practice 36:40 Craving Shapes Perception
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17242

item.title: Whole Presence Samadhi
item.description:
What does samadhi actually feel like: tightening around a technique, or a softening into continuous presence? The talk moves through three linked dimensions—undistractedness, non-separation, and not being divided—using stories of a calm monk on a Kyoto bus, a Zen drum teaching, and the hard-won spaciousness behind Suzuki Roshi’s gentleness. In dialogue, the speakers keep bringing the question down to earth: how the hindrances, craving, fatigue, and striving shape the practice, and how leaving things alone can let clarity settle on its own.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Samadhi 03:44 Bus Ride Presence 08:33 Undistracted Awareness 15:17 Becoming One 21:38 Ordinary Acts 25:10 Softness Through Suffering 29:50 Striving and Craving 38:02 Hindrances and Wholeness 47:49 Closing Blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17240

item.title: Restlessness, Worry & Compassion
item.description:
Restlessness is treated here as more than a nuisance: it’s a forward-leaning, predictive energy that keeps trying to manage the future and turn the present into a test. The talk moves from ordinary worry and mental “false alarms” into Buddhist themes of anicca, dukkha, and bhava tanha, then slowly shifts toward what actually softens the mind: stopping, settling, and learning to stay. Along the way, it emphasizes that making peace with uncertainty takes compassion, and it closes by returning to a simple image from Ajahn Chah: if the cup is already broken, perhaps it can be enjoyed now.
Chapters: 00:00 Oatmeal and worry 02:29 Restlessness as the preface 06:15 Freud and uncertainty 13:08 Worry as control 22:05 Roots, safety, and false alarms 27:02 Present-moment practice 33:50 Compassion for groundlessness 39:50 Regret and forgiveness 49:23 The broken cup
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17167

item.title: Ill-Will & Metta
item.description:
Ill-will is treated here not as a simple obstacle, but as a kind of shortcut that seems to work just enough to keep repeating itself. The talk moves from the way craving and aversion narrow perception, to the damage anger does to clarity, and then toward a different response: softening the heart, taking responsibility for the mind’s reactions, and learning to see what is present without contention. Along the way, the speaker turns to metta as the gentler, more spacious way of meeting suffering—one that can hold sorrow without hardening into blame.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the problem 00:58 The Dharma as a broad remedy 05:18 Purification and practice 08:07 What and how 12:37 Craving shapes the view 17:40 Mara and the turning inward 23:41 The heat of aversion 31:53 Facing and owning anger 38:30 Metta as antidote
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17159

item.title: Dharmette: The Pleasure of Spiritual Friendship
item.description:
What if friendship is part of the path itself, not just a pleasant support around it? The talk moves from the idea of Dharma pleasures into the particular joy of spiritual friendship: being seen clearly in both strength and imperfection, and learning to direct attention toward goodness. With a brief reflection on Sangha and a line from Kinnell about reteaching a thing its loveliness, it settles into the claim that friends help us remember what is beautiful, urgent, and worth practicing.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 01:02 Seeing and Being Seen 02:58 Known in Imperfection 04:59 Sangha and Goodness 06:17 Shaping the Mind 07:57 Reminders of Dharma 09:48 Connoisseurs of Goodness 11:43 Closing Blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17014

item.title: Dharmette: The Pleasure of Purification
item.description:
The talk starts by pushing back on the idea that practice is mainly about peak experiences, and instead turns toward the ascent, the descent, and the valley as part of the path. From there, it asks what the uncomfortable side of practice tastes like: just suffering, or purification that can slowly become an acquired pleasure. Using an image of physical therapy and brief references to Ajahn Chah and Shinzen Young, the speaker explores how clinging gives a partial reward, while surrender to impermanence can feel painful and freeing at once.
Chapters: 00:09 Beyond the Peaks 01:25 Two Tastes 02:37 Acquired Taste 03:59 Clinging's Reward 06:18 Pain of Healing 07:37 Deep Joy 09:03 Surrender and Exposure 11:19 Freedom Afterward 13:37 Poignant Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17002

item.title: Dharmette: Sourcing our Motivation from Love Rather than Fear
item.description:
What if the force that shapes your practice is not fear, but care? The talk begins with a simple contrast drawn from a residential treatment setting: behavioral change can be pushed by fear, but lasting change is more likely to be sustained by love. From there, it turns inward, asking what kind of “behavioral modification program” we run on ourselves, and whether self-harshness, proving, and shame are quietly driving the work of practice. The talk then softens the whole frame with Suzuki Roshi’s line that we are “perfect just as you are” and there is still room for improvement. It moves toward curiosity, humor, and a lighter relationship to our flaws, suggesting that practice becomes less entangled when growth is sourced from tenderness rather than self-judgment.
Chapters: 00:34 A Different Dharma Pleasure 02:01 Working With Boys 02:56 Fear or Love 04:08 What Drives Us 05:17 Self-Harshness and Proof 07:53 Curiosity and Humor 09:41 Care Over Urgency 12:01 Less Entangled
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17000

item.title: Dharma Pleasure - The Pleasure of Having a Path
item.description:
What if the relief of practice is not mainly in letting go, but in finding a path that feels trustworthy? The talk moves from the pressure to be constantly wise or correctly “doing Dharma” toward a quieter account of pleasure: a path that satiates rather than stokes craving, and a confidence that can hold confusion without demanding instant certainty. Along the way, the speaker uses Nietzsche, Vaclav Havel, and a quoted passage about resting in confusion to show how faith can mean a felt direction, not a guaranteed outcome.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the theme 02:42 Pleasure and craving 04:02 Finding a path 06:48 Having a why 07:47 Faith without certainty 10:44 Direction in confusion 12:48 Closing reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16991

The talk moves from the pressure to be constantly wise or correctly “doing Dharma” toward a quieter account of pleasure: a path that satiates rather than stokes craving, and a confidence that can hold confusion without demanding instant certainty. Along the way, the speaker uses Nietzsche, Vaclav Havel, and a quoted passage about resting in confusion to show how faith can mean a felt direction, not a guaranteed outcome.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the theme 02:42 Pleasure and craving 04:02 Finding a path 06:48 Having a why 07:47 Faith without certainty 10:44 Direction in confusion 12:48 Closing reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16991

item.title: Seven Factors of Awakening
item.description:
The talk asks what changes when practice is no longer only about letting go of suffering, but also about cultivating the qualities that support awakening. From there it moves through the seven factors as a living balance: mindfulness, investigation, energy, joy, tranquility, samadhi, and equanimity, showing how they depend on one another rather than forming a tidy list. The speaker keeps returning to the practical question of what steadies the mind, softens clinging, and makes Dharma feel real rather than merely understood. References to the Buddha, Kabat-Zinn, and Shinzen Young are used to sharpen that movement from concept to lived realization.
Chapters: 00:25 Why This List Matters 02:49 Path and Fruit 05:02 A Teaching in Sickness 08:19 Mindfulness and Balance 11:48 What Mindfulness Does 17:45 Curiosity and Investigation 22:40 Energy and Rapture 29:51 Tranquility and Samadhi 38:53 Equanimity and Release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16986

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma as Existential Therapy
item.description:
The talk begins with mortality and impermanence: if everything we build is destined to pass away, how do we live without denial? From there, it frames Dharma as a kind of existential therapy, moving through death, meaning, freedom, isolation, and the question of what we owe others. Along the way, the speaker leans on Zen reminders, Yalom, a Buddha line about the world trembling in all directions, and a closing appeal to keep learning how to do better.
Chapters: 00:25 Facing Mortality 01:58 Dharma as Therapy 04:18 Learning Groundlessness 06:34 Meaning Is Made 08:20 Building a Life 10:05 Alone, Yet Responsible 11:57 Do Better
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16825

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma as Exposure Therapy for Samsara
item.description:
What if practice works less like escape and more like staying in contact with what we usually rush to avoid? The talk moves from attention therapy and cognitive therapy into a more pointed claim: Dharma as exposure therapy, where purification means gradually meeting the forces of greed, hate, delusion, anxiety, and pain without falling back into the usual strategies of distraction and control. Along the way, the speaker uses clinical exposure treatment, a note from Shinzen Young about developing a taste for purification, and a brief closing story about bitter experience becoming nourishment.
Chapters: 00:15 Three ways of framing Dharma 01:02 Avoidance and suffering 03:10 What exposure means 05:43 Dharma and samsara 07:02 Purification through practice 08:08 Taste for purification 10:15 Meeting primal forces 12:07 Nourishment from bitterness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16822

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma as Cognitive Therapy
item.description:
What if Dharma practice works, in part, by changing the stories that organize experience? The talk moves from mindfulness as more than attention training, into cognitive therapy language—schemas, distorted assumptions, and the way wise view reshapes speech, effort, and action. It then turns inward to the stories people carry about self, happiness, control, and choice, showing how the three characteristics can challenge those habits and make suffering more workable.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the frame 00:59 More than attention 03:00 Beck’s schemas 05:02 Wise view 07:09 A fresh self 08:39 Seeing our stories 11:33 Three characteristics 14:01 Attention and carryover
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16818

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma as an Attention Therapy
item.description:
What happens when attention itself is treated as the main place where Dharma works? The talk moves from the ways modern life fractures attention into a plain case for meditation as training: steadying the mind, releasing stickiness around thoughts, and letting suffering be seen more clearly. From there, it connects gathered attention with insight, arguing that what is understood in practice can sink deeper and last longer. Along the way, it draws on William James and Ajahn Chah to sharpen the point that attention is both central to agency and vulnerable under pressure.
Chapters: 00:11 Opening the framework 01:09 Attention as agency 02:04 Modern life and mind 03:23 Disturbed attention 05:15 Training the attention 07:24 Poise and clarity 09:08 Seeing suffering clearly 10:43 Insights that stick
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16812

item.title: Dharmette: The Mechanisms of Action of the Dharma
item.description:
What does it mean to ask not just whether the Dharma works, but how it works? The talk moves from that question into a simple theory of change: ethical conduct, collected attention, and wisdom as the classical mechanisms that make practice transformative. Along the way, the speaker uses Rilke, Twain, and Dylan to show why people keep going through discouragement, how truthfulness makes life less defended, and how meditation can become a kind of shelter from the overwhelm of ordinary experience. It ends by promising a fresh map of four mechanisms the speaker will unpack over the rest of the week.
Chapters: 00:12 Why Ask How It Works 01:11 Practice as Change 02:57 A Strong Reason to Continue 04:36 Rilke and Perseverance 05:39 Ethics and Non-Regret 07:12 Truth and Shelter 09:16 Collected Attention 11:28 A New Map Ahead
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16814

item.title: Dharma and Concern for the World
item.description:
How do we stay honest about suffering without collapsing into despair, frenzy, or numbness? The talk begins with the sense that many people are worn down by the news, by ecological damage, by political instability, and by the feeling that our attention itself is being mined. From there it moves carefully between grief and correction: a look at what is actually worsening, what is not, why mindfulness can become a kind of multitasking, and how to keep concern bound to action without wasting the heart. Along the way, the speaker brings in Justin Rosenstein, Tom Waits, research by Wheatley and Dan Gilbert, Toby Ord, and a brief Camus line on death, using each to sharpen the question of how to care well in difficult times. The ending turns toward practical restraint, generosity, rest, and a wider empathy that includes people far away and those yet to come.
Chapters: 00:12 Dharma as inquiry 02:54 Extraction and attention 05:18 Collapse and helplessness 10:09 Holding on 12:01 What to do 16:00 Seeing what improves 21:01 A risky century 25:40 Dharma and other medicines 31:11 Give, rest, and let go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16544

item.title: Developing our Dharma Stories
item.description:
The talk begins with a tension between silence and thought: when is thinking helpful, and when does it simply crowd the mind? From there, it turns toward Dharma stories as the narratives that help shape practice, while still remaining provisional, tailored, and open to revision. The speaker then moves into mortality, showing how facing impermanence can loosen defensive self-making and shift practice from self-improvement toward waking up. The ending settles into a quieter invitation: let whatever arises become part of learning, not getting.
Chapters: 00:00 Thought and silence 03:59 The ladder of thought 06:06 Stories that shape change 09:21 Mother tongue Dharma 14:35 Pep talks and awakening 23:10 Sitting with mortality 32:41 Fear, being, and release 38:56 Learning instead of getting
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16508

item.title: Monday Dharma Talk: Relationship between the Body and Thinking
item.description:
How do bodily feeling and thought shape each other, and what happens when practice stops treating thinking as the main source of insight? The talk moves from the body as the place where affect is first gathered, through the ways we build meaning, conclusions, and stories around pain, pleasure, and neutrality, to the possibility of seeing those stories loosen. Along the way, it uses examples from cognitive therapy, prediction and threat, and direct contemplative attention to show how practice can translate raw experience into wiser action without overexplaining it.
Chapters: 00:32 Investigating the human realm 04:11 Affect and meaning 07:48 Thought and feeling 13:56 Views and assumptions 20:18 Body as prediction 29:28 Without story 36:40 Making wise meanings 39:52 Training discernment
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16492

item.title: Sunday Evening Reflection
item.description:
The talk turns on a paradox: how can you be totally acceptable as you are and still honestly commit to change? Using a line attributed to Suzuki Roshi, the speaker argues against the usual half-and-half compromise, where acceptance becomes a tactic and growth becomes another project. Instead, the invitation is a full yes to the messiness of life and a clear recognition that it is possible to suffer less, without bargaining with yourself.
Chapters: 00:00 Bohr's Paradox 00:18 Suzuki Roshi's Line 00:55 Acceptance and Growth 01:53 No Spiritual Bargaining 02:35 A Deep Yes 04:00 Change Without Desperation 09:31 End of Retreat Day 10:12 Rest and Regather
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16487

item.title: Dharmette on Retreat Theme: Thought & Freedom
item.description:
Can thought be part of freedom rather than the obstacle to it? The talk begins by insisting that practice belongs in ordinary life, where the home, the day, and even confusion become part of the path instead of something to hide. From there it turns to the stickiness of thought, the habit of pretending, and the need to move flexibly between meaning, spaciousness, and mindfulness without turning any part of experience into an enemy. It closes with a quiet reminder that the Dharma is meant to sink deeply into the mind so confidence and goodness can stay present without display.
Chapters: 00:00 Practice in life 00:58 No need to pretend 02:06 Fidelity to experience 04:25 Thought and freedom 07:08 Stories in motion 10:11 Mindfulness and unconsciousness 13:21 What mindfulness knows 16:58 Three capacities 19:32 Holding meaning lightly
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16482

item.title: Love as the Default Position of the Mind
item.description:
What if the mind’s default position is not self-protection but love? The talk moves through the tension between radical acceptance and change, arguing that practice is not a self-improvement project but a way of loosening clinging, seeing more clearly, and letting care become more natural. Along the way, the speaker threads in Bohr, Suzuki Roshi, Maslow, Levinas, and Thich Nhat Hanh to show how insight, grief, impermanence, and the brahma-viharas all point toward the same opening. By the end, the question is less how to manufacture love than how to let it be what remains when self-occupation falls away.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:07 Opposites can both be true 01:31 Acceptance without bargaining 06:49 Growth points beyond self 11:12 Seeing as love 15:50 From states to traits 20:12 Home base of the heart 24:05 Insight frees love 29:12 Grief and non-clinging 38:55 The cost of selfing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/70766/

item.title: No escape other than into the heart (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if retreat is not an escape from real life, but the place where life’s pressures show how mind is shaping everything? The talk begins with the five hindrances, then moves into a larger argument: suffering is not solved by rearranging conditions, but by turning toward the heart, where pain, shame, craving, and aversion can be known more clearly. From the Dhammapada to Ajahn Chah, and through stories of envy, a bitter journal, and the line "no mud, no lotus," the talk keeps returning to a single practice: stay close, feel fully, and let experience be transformed rather than defended against.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:11 The hindrances in retreat 02:14 Retreat as real life 04:47 Mind shapes experience 07:16 Conditioning and shame 12:33 Nourishing what arises 18:51 The heart begins the path 21:48 Pain is not enough 31:42 Greed, hatred, delusion 39:16 Stay with the body
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/70711/

item.title: Reflections on Equanimity
item.description:
How do you care deeply without being consumed by what you care about? The talk moves through that tension by comparing equanimity to rest: not indifference, but the heart’s need for moments of being unstimulated, like sleep after constant pressure. From there, it weighs the danger of two distortions — that nothing matters, or that everything matters so much that there is no room for rest — and traces a middle path between them. Quotations about death and loss then deepen the point, showing how equanimity can steady compassion rather than weaken it.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the theme 00:46 The need for rest 02:04 Heart and sleep 04:05 False ways of resting 05:16 Care and not care 08:14 Equanimity with compassion 10:11 Loss made visible 13:44 A steadier response
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16270

item.title: Sympathetic Joy
item.description:
What if joy did not have to be manufactured, but could be received from someone else? The talk begins by noting how awkward and forced “being joyful” can feel, then turns toward mudita as a more relational kind of joy: something that arises when we can genuinely lean on each other’s happiness, even amid grief. From there, the speaker traces the main obstacles—comparison, possessiveness, envy, and the ego’s need to claim all goodness for itself—and shows how practice can make the heart more fluid, so another person’s joy feels unthreatening rather than diminishing.
Chapters: 00:14 What mudita is 01:30 A guide’s example 02:15 Joy through connection 05:22 Joy and grief 06:42 Removing obstacles 07:17 Envy and ego 10:13 Against the stream 13:09 Nothing is taken
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16264

item.title: Reflections on Compassion
item.description:
What does it mean to really comprehend suffering without hardening, collapsing, or rushing to fix it? The talk moves from the shock of dukkha into a careful distinction between empathy and compassion: compassion is presented as love in the face of suffering, with room, patience, and a willingness to understand our own pain more clearly. Along the way, the speaker brings in a grief poem, the First Noble Truth, a compassion-study example, and a closing emphasis on love that does not cling or control.
Chapters: 00:13 The shock of suffering 02:17 Comprehending dukkha 03:57 Love instead of control 06:21 Diagnosing our pain 09:12 Compassion and empathy 11:30 True love doesn't hurt 13:33 A wholehearted wish
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16259

item.title: Reflections on Lovingkindness
item.description:
Can lovingkindness be real without pretending? The talk begins with that tension and follows it into a practical, unsentimental view of metta as something that can meet fear, wanting, and avoidance directly rather than papering over them. From there it moves through a brief turn to neuroscience, a reflection on attached love in family life, and then back to a quieter sense of warmth, goodness, and the way love can thaw what feels closed off. By the end, metta is presented both as a purification practice and a cultivation practice: a way of lowering resistance and becoming more attuned to goodness in ourselves and others.
Chapters: 00:12 Sincere practice 01:34 Fear and wanting 04:13 Fear’s force 05:18 Approaching anxiety 06:29 Metta for fear 08:12 Attachment and care 09:56 Seeing goodness 11:37 Moved by goodness 13:25 Purification and blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16266

From there it moves through a brief turn to neuroscience, a reflection on attached love in family life, and then back to a quieter sense of warmth, goodness, and the way love can thaw what feels closed off. By the end, metta is presented both as a purification practice and a cultivation practice: a way of lowering resistance and becoming more attuned to goodness in ourselves and others.
Chapters: 00:12 Sincere practice 01:34 Fear and wanting 04:13 Fear’s force 05:18 Approaching anxiety 06:29 Metta for fear 08:12 Attachment and care 09:56 Seeing goodness 11:37 Moved by goodness 13:25 Purification and blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16266

item.title: Orienting to the Divine Abodes
item.description:
What if the point of practice is not to build a better self, but to let self-concern fall away so love can come forward? Opening with Maslow and moving into the Brahma-viharas, the talk follows that turn from self-actualization to a more natural way of being: loving-kindness, compassion, joy, and equanimity as places the heart can rest. It also lingers on the tension between clinging and peace, and ends with a story about a practitioner finally feeling self-kindness in a retreat hall after years of practice.
Chapters: 00:13 Maslow and self-erasure 01:46 Love feels natural 03:01 Beyond state chasing 04:34 Where attention lives 07:00 The divine abodes 09:39 Clinging and peace 11:06 Love in every condition 13:03 A story of felt metta
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16256

item.title: Samvega and Love of the World
item.description:
What if disenchantment with the world and love for the world are not opposites but two parts of the same path? The talk begins with the paradox of crossing over the flood without pushing forward or staying in place, then turns to samvega—spiritual urgency, shock, and dismay at the limits of ordinary life—as a force that can loosen our grip on samsara. From there it asks how renunciation, especially in lay practice, can make room not for indifference but for deeper care, until letting go becomes a space filled with love for suffering beings.
Chapters: 01:28 Crossing the flood 02:30 Disenchantment and care 04:23 Lay and monastic paths 06:19 What samvega means 09:00 Not building on quicksand 10:53 Love after letting go 13:12 A robe wide enough
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16101

item.title: Meaning And Nonconceptualization
item.description:
What happens when the mind’s need to make meaning becomes so strong that it starts to shape the whole world? The talk moves from the usefulness of Buddhist categories and beautiful thoughts like metta into a deeper question about fear, interpretation, and the compulsion to know. It then turns to a canonical exchange on how appearances disappear through not conceiving, and ends with a simple instruction: come to no conclusions, and let intimacy with experience generate stories that are more spacious and less entangled.
Chapters: 00:37 Stories and struggle 01:37 Useful Dharma thought 03:14 Meaning and fear 05:19 When thoughts feel real 08:30 Non-conceiving 10:35 No conclusions 12:08 Dharma autobiography
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16092

item.title: Grief and Love
item.description:
What if grief is not a detour from the path, but one of the ways love grows up? The talk begins with loss as something ordinary and pervasive—felt in a parent’s death, in history, and in the fragility of the world—and then turns to grief as a companion to compassion, letting go, and widening moral concern. It moves between personal reflection, short quotations from Katherine Schultz and Maya Angelou, and a challenge to the usual idea that awakening means no longer grieving at all.
Chapters: 00:12 Loss Reveals the World 02:56 Why Grief Belongs 04:21 Growing Up Spiritually 05:48 Learning Better 07:11 Love Meets Suffering 08:34 Permission to Grieve 10:24 Beyond Yet Not Without 12:13 A Deeper, Truer Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16078

item.title: Renunciation and Enjoying Pleasure
item.description:
The talk asks whether Dharma is something to tack onto a comfortable life, or something that asks for real relinquishment. Starting from a William James quote about habit and training the nervous system, the speaker argues that renunciation is not grim self-denial but a way of loosening craving so pleasure can be felt without compulsivity. The middle of the talk turns on a careful distinction between craving and desire, and on the idea that equanimity with pleasure may look like uncomplicated delight rather than defensive caution.
Chapters: 00:12 Habit and training 01:11 Renunciation and resistance 03:12 A central practice 04:40 Letting go eases fear 05:31 Pleasure without defense 07:32 Craving and desire 09:11 Equanimity with pleasure 11:00 De-shaming and freedom
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16075

item.title: Helplessness and Power
item.description:
What if helplessness is not a problem to solve, but the ground from which clearer power becomes possible? The talk moves from a set of paired teachings—suffering and well-being, impermanence and stability, selflessness and self-confidence—into a close look at how much of ordinary life is organized around resisting vulnerability and trying to stay in control. From there, it turns to a quieter kind of strength: opening to helplessness, stopping the fight against the First Noble Truth, and learning to use whatever power we do have more wisely. Along the way, the speaker connects helplessness with tears, self-blame, surrender, and the responsibility that comes with deeper practice.
Chapters: 00:16 Nothing Dominates Everything 00:54 Pairing the Teachings 02:16 The Reality of Helplessness 05:01 Control and Self-Blame 06:38 Opening to Surrender 09:12 Finding Wise Power 11:12 Practice Increases Impact 13:18 Closing Reflections
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16077

item.title: Reflections on Thich Nhat Hanh's Teachings
item.description:
What does it mean to trust love, and to let a teacher’s words keep working after the teacher is gone? The talk begins as a memorial to Thich Nhat Hanh, then moves through the speaker’s early encounters with his sangha, the force of his presence, and the feeling that his teaching was never really about authority but about love, understanding, and how to become available to the present moment. From there it turns to core themes in his work: "no mud, no lotus," the non-duality of wisdom and compassion, care for anger, interbeing, and finally the fearlessness of "no birth, no death."
Chapters: 00:31 Remembering Thay 04:10 Love as credibility 08:14 Available to the kingdom 12:38 Mud and lotus 14:28 Wisdom and love 19:29 Caring for anger 24:53 Interbeing and paper 27:02 No birth, no death 30:36 A love story
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16050
