item.title: Mindfulness, Dharma, Mental Health & Science Part 2 of 2
item.description:
How does mindfulness work when suffering is tangled, not singular? The talk moves from a dense account of psychiatric causality, through the idea of Dharma as something like a "dirty drug" with many mechanisms, into a practical description of attention training, cognitive reappraisal, exposure, tranquility, and existential inquiry. It then turns toward shame, selfing, mortality, and the ways practice can soften defensiveness without pretending there is a view-less technique.
Chapters: 00:12 Psychiatric complexity 03:07 Causality as dependence 06:08 Dharma as multiple methods 09:16 Attention and right view 14:39 Thoughts, depression, and shame 19:58 Exposure and approach 29:10 Tranquility and mortality 37:54 Equanimity, self, and shame 59:18 Questions and integration
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16025

item.title: Mindfulness, Dharma, Mental Health & Science Part 1 of 2
item.description:
What happens when mindfulness is carried into science, medicine, and mental health without losing its depth? The talk moves from growth in meditation research and public use, through William James, Maslow, and Stephen Jay Gould, to a careful map of possible relationships between Dharma and science: separation, competition, selective borrowing, and conversation. It then lands in concrete examples like DBT, mindfulness-based programs, and questions from the audience about whether explicit Buddhist language helps or hinders practice, especially in secular settings.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the question 01:00 James and attention 03:27 Meditation in public life 08:52 Well-being and dukkha 11:40 Dharma and science 15:55 Ways of relating 25:10 Linehan as example 30:38 Mindfulness programs 41:00 Questions and tensions
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16024

item.title: Love and its relation with wisdom.
item.description:
How does love remain trustworthy when the talk keeps returning to suffering, ethical strain, and the limits of wisdom alone? The speaker moves from a playful opening into a sustained reflection on love as the real transmission of Dharma, using examples from Thich Nhat Hanh, Martin Luther King Jr., William James, and the salt-and-river image to show how understanding expands the heart. The talk then turns inward, asking how practice changes a suspicious or hardened mind, and outward, toward how compassion, moral responsibility, and even long-term human survival belong to the same field. It ends by holding liberation and care together: freeing the heart is not the end of the question, but the ground from which love can be spent.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and intention 01:31 Love needs credibility 04:02 Practice as art 07:10 Finite life, love's necessity 11:40 From suspicion to trust 15:50 Recognizing goodness 19:40 Dharma love and the wings 26:42 The river of compassion 29:03 Love in every stage
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/69772/

item.title: Memory, prediction, Bharatanha, and the present
item.description:
What happens when the mind keeps trying to live ahead of life, using memory to predict and control what comes next? The talk follows that pressure from boredom, stress, and vigilance into a quieter question: what does it mean to be present without forcing ourselves into the present? From an embodied memory of Thich Nhat Hanh through reflections on memory, prediction, and bhava tanha, the talk gradually turns toward purifying view, blessing the past, and loosening the grip of future-making. It ends by framing retreat as a protected place to surrender the future and discover a present that is less a moment to hold than something bottomless and open.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the room 02:15 Caught in time 05:50 Availability, not force 08:00 Memory and the past 13:11 Rewriting the story 20:13 Digesting experience 26:32 Prediction and survival 31:22 Becoming and fear 37:22 Letting the future go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/69750/

item.title: Mindfulness, Thinking, Mindlessness, Rumination
item.description:
What counts as helpful thinking, and when does thinking become a place we get lost? The talk starts by distinguishing mindfulness from other forms of non-mindfulness, then moves through examples from retreat life, research on mind-wandering, and the felt difference between ordinary drift, rumination, and a slackening that may actually be restorative. It ends by pointing back to the body and to equanimity with unpleasant feeling, rather than trying to solve emotional distress by thinking harder.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening image 00:48 Kinds of non-mindfulness 02:22 Thinking is not the enemy 06:04 Shame and thought patterns 10:11 Evolution and vigilance 15:13 Energy and slack 20:20 Attention economy 27:57 Loss of autonomy 32:50 Rumination and the body
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14662

item.title: Affect and the Interpersonal Realm (5 of 5)
item.description:
What if feeling, not thought, is the linchpin of how we know what matters, decide, and relate? The talk moves from a recent scientific argument for an “affectivism” phase, into a Dharma framing that “all dhammas converge on feeling,” and then into the risk and vulnerability of intimacy, where another person can feel like both threat and opportunity. From there it turns practical: how to hold pleasure without grasping, face shame without doubling down, and use the body—relaxing the hands, jaw, shoulders, and belly—as a place to discharge tension and regain steadiness.
Chapters: 00:12 Feeling shapes mind 02:58 Feeling at the center 04:58 Threat and opportunity 06:50 Love and vulnerability 08:53 Equanimity with pleasure 11:25 Being wrong gently 13:05 Body as stabilizer 15:05 Empathy deepens
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14569

item.title: Strength and the Surrender to Goodness (4 of 5)
item.description:
What happens when reverence, rather than caution, meets another person’s goodness? The talk explores how the heart can surrender without losing discernment: moving from fear of idealization to a more grounded awe, especially in relationship, in practice, and in the face of the path itself. Along the way, the speaker considers how envy and comparison narrow the mind, and how metta can train us to notice goodness without turning it into a contest.
Chapters: 01:00 From pain to goodness 02:19 Why surrender matters 03:36 Idealization and illusion 05:07 Reverence, not projection 06:34 Surrender to Dharma 08:02 Awe for goodness 09:52 Envy and comparison 11:11 Metta and surrender 12:19 Closing reflections
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14571

item.title: The Pain of the Other Spins Our Mind (3 of 5)
item.description:
How does someone stay open when another person’s pain, confusion, or non-enlightenment starts to spin the mind? The talk begins with the challenge of really realizing dukkha, then moves into how suffering in a loved one can evoke compassion while also awakening clinging, control, fear, and old family patterns. It ends by asking what forgiveness and acceptance look like when love and irritation are both present, closing with a Leonard Cohen lyric that gives the tension a quiet, humane shape.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Dukkha 01:05 Personal Suffering Realized 02:02 The Suffering of Others 03:11 Love and Clinging 05:25 One Mind, One Robe 07:18 When Reactivity Takes Over 09:48 Change Needs Love 12:30 Compassion and Forgiveness 14:01 A Treaty of Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14561

item.title: Not-knowing is the Most Intimate (2 of 5)
item.description:
Who am I, if the mind stops trying to answer? This talk moves through that question into the experience of becoming mysterious to oneself, where the familiar self-story can crack open through insight, awe, grief, or love. From there it turns to the people we know best, asking whether we can loosen the concepts that flatten them and meet them again with reverence for what we do not know. The talk closes by returning to a simple practice invitation: put down some of what seems known and look with new eyes.
Chapters: 00:23 The question of self 02:04 When autobiography cracks 03:31 We are more than imagined 04:39 Not knowing is intimate 05:15 Seeing loved ones anew 07:22 The hidden life of others 08:53 Roles and predictability 11:09 Beyond the role 13:00 Looking with new eyes
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14559

item.title: Interpersonal Life as Dharma Practice (1 of 5)
item.description:
What changes when the other person, rather than the isolated self, becomes the basic unit of practice? The talk begins by using an offhand, humanly imperfect story to show how relational life reveals our habits, our dignity, and our blind spots all at once. From there it moves into the tension between what we know on the cushion and what has actually become real in conversation, conflict, care, and vulnerability.
Chapters: 00:21 Relational Beginning 00:52 Humility and Exposure 02:27 Interpersonal Richness 04:04 Nervous System in Contact 05:46 Cushion and Life 07:00 What Becomes Real 09:58 Knowledge to Realization 12:01 Practice this Week 14:20 Questions to Carry
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14557

item.title: Love and Its Relation to Wisdom
item.description:
What if love is not a sentiment added onto practice, but the condition that makes change possible? The talk begins with the stubbornness of human habits and moves through literature, personal memory, and Dharma teaching to argue that wisdom and love belong together: love clarifies, attunes, and helps us face dukkha without hatred. It then widens from the personal path to Sangha, teacher, and the pressures of modern collective life, asking how a liberated heart is to respond without becoming split.
Chapters: 00:01 Love Changes People 01:58 William James On Goodness 03:42 Mortality and Urgency 08:11 From Cynicism To Sangha 12:24 Recognition And Dharma Love 15:57 Metta And The Two Wings 22:14 Understanding As Love 27:24 Path, Sangha, And Mirror 31:52 Practice In A Fractured World
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14393

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Phillip Moffitt, Tuere Sala: Dialogue on aspects of freedom.
item.description:
What if freedom is not a one-time breakthrough, but a slow loosening shaped by sitting, returning, and letting practice work on habit? In this dialogue, the talk moves from the contrast between depth and breadth, into questions of non-self, self-improvement, striving, and the way the Dharma shows up in ordinary life rather than as a project of becoming someone else. It also turns toward the role of suffering in maturing love, with stories and images that keep bringing the conversation back to practice as something gradual, ordinary, and quietly transformative.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Phillip Moffitt, Tuere Sala
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the dialogue 01:07 Depth and breadth 03:56 Practice as conditioning 10:35 Non-self and reactivity 13:52 Against spiritual ambition 19:34 Striving and purification 26:59 Dukkha opens love 38:16 Learning the plateau 44:54 Why keep practicing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/67334/

item.title: Tranquilizing side of the 7 Factors: Agitation and temporal density.
item.description:
What happens when the mind stops treating every moment as a test of what comes next? The talk moves from a vivid retreat story about a "tunnel of kindness" into a slower investigation of agitation, prediction, and the feeling that time is thick and crowded with meaning. From there it traces the tranquilizing side of the seven factors: how letting go, equanimity, and samadhi thin out that density without flattening the poignancy of life. The talk ends by turning toward surrender, refuge, and a simple instruction to just sit.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:30 Kindness Overflowing 04:44 Why Peace Matters 09:43 Road Before the Bend 12:13 Agitation And Time 20:11 Animal Vigilance 27:20 Equanimity Softens 32:47 Samadhi As Letting Go 40:34 Settling Into Refuge
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/67330/

item.title: Equanimity (5 of 5):
item.description:
What does equanimity have to do with indifference, cynicism, or saying that nothing matters? The talk begins by pushing back on those confusions and instead treats equanimity as a way of meeting what has already arisen without interference, so compassion can stay steady rather than collapse into overwhelm or shutdown. From there it moves through the tension between rest and action, showing how a rested heart can love more deeply, act more strongly, and then rest again.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening reflection 01:20 Not cynicism 03:04 What equanimity is 05:32 Heart with force 06:53 Middle path 08:46 Compassion purified 12:04 Rest and renew 14:23 Closing thanks
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14009

item.title: Equanimity (4 of 5): Skillfully Meeting Feelings Associated with Self
item.description:
What happens when the stories that protect a sense of self start to feel thin? Using the image of planting a flag—claiming territory, making a case, keeping a mask in place—the talk traces how identity gets built through performance, defensiveness, shame, and the need to be seen a certain way. It then turns to equanimity as the way to meet those self-defining feelings directly, without making an enemy of them, so their charge can slowly drain and the heart can soften.
Chapters: 00:12 Planting a Flag 01:25 Life as Performance 03:12 The Self as Argument 04:53 Pain Reveals Self-View 07:23 Unconvinced by Story 09:01 Meeting with Equanimity 10:30 Grief and Nonself 12:24 Love Instead of Defense
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14000

item.title: Equanimity (3 of 5): Transmuting Dukkha
item.description:
What if dukkha is not something to get rid of, but material to work with? The talk moves from the Buddha’s middle path and a critique of self-denial into a quieter claim: pain met with equanimity can be transformed into wisdom, love, and a longing for liberation. Along the way, it uses images of mud and lotus, softening to uncontrollable conditions, and a kind of heart-level purification to show how practice can turn difficulty into something nourishing.
Chapters: 00:14 Closeness Across Distance 01:18 The Middle Path 02:46 Grasping and Desire 03:46 The Limits of Austerity 05:02 Not Squandering Dukkha 06:32 Love Through Difficulty 07:34 Softening to Conditions 09:12 Pain Met Wisely 11:54 Catharsis and Aftereffects
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/13995

item.title: Equanimity (2 of 5): Safety and Compulsive Hypothesis-Testing
item.description:
How does equanimity meet the mind’s habit of treating pain as a threat that must be solved? The talk begins by reframing practice through our animal vulnerability: the speaker argues that when we stop condemning experience and instead understand fear, sensation, and prediction as part of embodied life, we can train more skillfully. From there, the talk turns to neuroscience’s idea of the brain as a hypothesis-testing organ, showing how the drive for safety can pull attention toward the future and make even small discomforts feel urgent. The close returns to practice, with breathing, an open chest, and resting in the triple gem as a way to meet pain without being spun by it.
Chapters: 00:34 Opening Equanimity 02:26 Training the Animal 03:03 Hypothesis-Testing Mind 05:26 Safety and Fear 07:07 The Radical Present 09:07 Pain and Vigilance 11:05 Equanimity in Pain 13:07 Refuge and Confidence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/13990

item.title: Equanimity (1 of 5): Introduction
item.description:
Equanimity here is less a calm mood than the practice of not being compelled to fix, grab, or fight the moment. The talk moves from the limits of attention alone, to equanimity as a widening of perspective and a balance point between suppression and identification, then into how it helps us meet greed, hatred, and delusion without laundering them into something more acceptable. By the end, the emphasis is on a tender kind of non-compulsion: staying open to longing, imperfection, and the pressure of experience without having to act them out.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening and context 01:19 Attention and wisdom 02:44 Equanimity and non-clinging 05:00 Widening perspective 06:16 Balance and realism 07:32 Opening to dukkha 09:41 Why it matters 11:47 Working with the defilements
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/13983

item.title: Anatta and its Blessings
item.description:
What happens when the mind keeps turning experience into a story about “me”? The talk moves from the grief of seeing impermanence and unsatisfactoriness to a close, practical inquiry into anatta: how selfing is built through narration, comparison, fear, and clinging, and how that construction can slowly loosen. Along the way, the speaker reframes self-love as release rather than self-cherishing, and shows how lessening fixation on identity opens space for intimacy, equanimity, and compassion.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 03:07 Grief before relief 09:06 What not-self means 14:18 Looking for the self 20:58 Self-love and defensiveness 28:00 Narrative gravity 35:49 Freedom from comparison 42:39 Open air
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/68411/

item.title: Sunday Closing Dharmette
item.description:
The talk asks what awakening looks like when it is measured not only by deep moments, but by the breadth of one’s life that slowly comes under the care of the Dharma. Using Ajahn Sucitto’s line about enlightenment as “death by 1000 cuts,” the speaker shifts attention from dramatic breakthroughs to gradual cultivation, ethical conduct, and the parts of the heart that still remain untouched. From there the talk widens into sila: becoming safe for others, letting goodness ripen through grief and letting go, and seeing how love, responsibility, and moral incompleteness keep unfolding. It closes by inviting listeners to ask what the silence and stillness of practice imply for their conduct in the world.
Chapters: 00:27 Welcome and Sit 02:43 Depth and Breadth 04:47 Gradual Cultivation 07:39 Sila as Refuge 08:21 Radical Heart 10:16 Love, Debt, Dying 12:50 Implications of Stillness 14:01 Closing Gratitude 20:09 Announcements and Break
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12570

item.title: Saturday Dharma Talk: Freedom to Love
item.description:
What if freedom is not only freedom from clinging, but freedom to love more deeply? The talk moves from a careful map of suffering to a quieter claim that letting go does not empty the heart; it makes room for non-hatred, generosity, and a love that can hold grief without collapsing into denial. Along the way, the speaker uses Tolstoy, Thich Nhat Hanh, Gandhi, and a story about dana to show how wisdom, ethics, and love keep folding into one another.
Chapters: 00:00 Suffering and freedom 02:39 Many forms of liberation 07:24 Freedom to love 11:19 Love after letting go 17:05 Compassion in public life 22:52 Wisdom as understanding 32:00 Defensiveness and generosity 43:57 A surplus to spend
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12567

item.title: Thursday Dharma Talk: Not-Self
item.description:
Not-self here is approached less as a doctrine to believe than as a way of looking: the talk keeps returning to how clinging, fear, and comparison make experience feel dense and centered on a solid “me.” It moves from the urge to simplify spiritual life into one idea, through the trap of turning not-self into a philosophical position, and into the more practical work of noticing self-view, conceit, and the emotional charge around identity. Along the way, the speaker distinguishes not-self from self-hatred, makes room for warmth and self-acceptance, and ends by describing a quieter, less fixed sense of awareness that is still fully present and ethically alive.
Chapters: 00:40 Why simplicity misleads 02:54 Not-self as practice 06:22 Beyond views and fantasies 10:10 Misreading the teaching 16:59 Self-love and release 21:53 Self-view and conceit 32:27 Narrative gravity 39:59 Fear and safety 45:58 Empty center, open heart
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12555

item.title: Wednesday Evening Reflection: Rohini's Enlightenment Poem
item.description:
The talk turns on a line from Rohini’s poem: the path is not a route to fit yourself into, but “a great shining world” you can enter wherever you are. From there, the speaker gently undoes the pressure to mold the heart to Dharma culture, instead pointing to belonging that grows over time and includes all the pieces of life. The reflection closes by encouraging practice, rest, and the simple recognition that what can feel like a wall may be a Dharma door.
Chapters: 03:26 A shining world 03:54 Not fitting in 04:36 Enter anywhere 05:19 Belonging and home 06:05 Nothing left behind 06:47 From every direction 07:25 Affirming effort 08:05 Rest and continue
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12548

item.title: Tuesday Dharma Talk: Practicing Freely
item.description:
What does it mean to practice freedom without trying to cling your way there? The talk begins with the ordinary grip of preference and self-evaluation, then keeps returning to a quieter question: how practice can be guided by awareness, humility, awe, and a willingness to let go of control without abandoning responsibility. Along the way it moves through vivid images—a retreat hall, Wittgenstein’s ladder, the “wholesome unpleasant,” and the sense that the Dharma may feel less chosen than encountered. By the end, the emphasis is not on getting somewhere, but on following the thread of what is already being nourished here and now.
Chapters: 00:24 Retreat Hall Clinging 02:15 How Practice Reveals 09:12 Background Mind 11:54 Ladders and Imperfection 15:01 Intrinsic Motivation 23:54 Humility and Awe 28:01 Curiosity Over Discipline 31:51 Self-Review and Purification 41:03 For Others and the Dharma
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12537

item.title: Monday Evening Reflection: A little bit freer
item.description:
The talk starts by asking what it means to meditate on the mind as it is, especially when practice does not feel free at all. From there, the speaker normalizes the roughness of retreat life and turns to a quieter distinction: the idea of freedom can create distance, while the practice of freedom looks for any small way the present moment is a little less sealed in suffering. The movement stays close to ordinary experience—fatigue, doubt, and the sense of being trapped—before ending with Suzuki Roshi’s image of mist versus rainstorm to suggest that real change may be nearly imperceptible as it happens.
Chapters: 00:01 Begin with the mind 00:31 First-day difficulty 01:18 Hot hall, heavy sleep 02:55 Idea versus practice 04:10 Looking for seams 05:38 Doubt and selfing 06:58 A little more free 08:09 Subtle changes 09:15 Mist, not storm
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12529

item.title: Improvisation and the Messiness of Experience
item.description:
How do you practice when the moment is messy, the instructions only partly fit, and the mind wants to turn everything into a tidy story? The talk moves from a comic opening with The Big Lebowski into a careful look at improvisation, pretending, and the ways Dharma practice can feel more like reading a novel than following Cliff Notes. From there it settles into the harder work of not knowing, resting in confusion, and letting faith, kindness, and humility arise where control runs out.
Chapters: 00:00 A Complicated Case 01:47 Cliff Notes and Novels 04:42 Improvising in Practice 10:20 Listening Without Distortion 13:32 Trusting Guidance 19:56 Not Knowing, Resting 26:16 Monastic Roots, Lay Questions 30:46 Faith at the Stuck Place 36:48 Helplessness and Compassion
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12261

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Tuere Sala: Tuere Sala and Matthew Brensilver in dialogue about wisdom
item.description:
What does wisdom look like when practice stops trying to smooth life back into place? The talk begins with a poem about trees as quiet listeners, then turns into a dialogue on wisdom as something that emerges from retreat, attention, and the willingness to stay close to suffering without turning away. From Toni Morrison’s distinction between data, knowledge, and wisdom to Ajahn Chah’s teaching on two kinds of suffering, the conversation keeps returning to the same question: how do we meet reality clearly enough to let the heart soften?
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Tuere Sala
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with listening 02:58 Dharma as generosity 07:59 From anecdote to inquiry 11:38 Morrison on wisdom 14:01 What wisdom means 24:33 Learning through suffering 34:04 The redemptive path 43:35 Three layers of suffering 50:33 Closing with clear signals
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/63043/

What does wisdom look like when practice stops trying to smooth life back into place? The talk begins with a poem about trees as quiet listeners, then turns into a dialogue on wisdom as something that emerges from retreat, attention, and the willingness to stay close to suffering without turning away. From Toni Morrison’s distinction between data, knowledge, and wisdom to Ajahn Chah’s teaching on two kinds of suffering, the conversation keeps returning to the same question: how do we meet reality clearly enough to let the heart soften?
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Tuere Sala
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with listening 02:58 Dharma as generosity 07:59 From anecdote to inquiry 11:38 Morrison on wisdom 14:01 What wisdom means 24:33 Learning through suffering 34:04 The redemptive path 43:35 Three layers of suffering 50:33 Closing with clear signals
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/63043/

item.title: The Parami of Truthfulness
item.description:
How does truthfulness become freeing if we can’t claim a final, capital-T truth? The talk starts with the promise of “the truth will set you free” and then moves into a more modest but practical claim: that honesty, fidelity, and a willingness to look closely are enough to loosen suffering. From there it turns to the ways the mind rationalizes, hides, and self-justifies, using references to ignorance, the three characteristics, and research on bias and secrecy to show why practice has to move against our default habits. The talk ends by returning to truthful speech and inner transparency as forms of alignment with reality, even while allowing for the possibility that we may still be wrong.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 01:32 Truth and humility 06:12 Fidelity to reality 09:36 Courage to see 15:57 Against the current 25:25 Refining the view 31:18 Secrets and shame 38:19 Truthful speech 42:18 Humility and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/63035/

item.title: Reflections on Truth and Honesty
item.description:
Truth is treated less as a final doctrine than as an honest way of looking. The talk moves from grief and the uneasy question of whether truth can free us, into a quieter claim: even if capital-T Truth is hard to pin down, we can still tell the difference between honesty and self-deception, and that matters for practice. From there it broadens into curiosity, the three marks, secrecy, shame, and lying, showing how much effort it takes to keep a false picture of ourselves in place. The talk closes by turning truthfulness into an ethical vow: to look more carefully, speak more transparently, and be less willing to defend what is merely conditioning.
Chapters: 00:14 Beginning with grief 02:37 Truth and liberation 05:56 From truth to honesty 14:04 Curiosity as practice 16:19 Delusion as default 22:59 Refining the view 27:44 Secrets and shame 34:15 Transparency and freedom
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12293

item.title: Preparing Citta not to Burn - IRC mini-retreat
item.description:
How do you practice when suffering is already layered on top of suffering, and the world itself feels fragile? The talk moves from fire season, climate grief, and William James’s image of people skating on a melting frozen lake into a quieter question: what does it mean to face reality without spinning out or hardening over? From there, it turns to gratitude, adaptation, and the limits of technique, arguing that practice is less about putting out every fire than about cultivating a heart that burns less easily.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with Dukkha 02:18 Frozen Lake Image 04:35 Ordinary Gratitude 06:28 Balancing the Dharma 07:51 Tradition and Adaptation 11:07 Teaching with Caution 15:10 Not Putting Out Every Fire 19:03 Preparing Citta
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12019

item.title: Taking the Backwards Ethical Step
item.description:
What if ethical growth begins not by feeling better, but by becoming willing to feel what is uncomfortable, unresolved, and true? The talk starts with the idea of a “backward ethical step,” then moves from traditional Buddhist teachings on sila, hiri, and ottappa into moral psychology, asking how empathy, discomfort, and even grief widen the circle of what we can no longer ignore. It stays close to lived questions: how people rationalize harm, how sensitivity deepens with practice, and how a response to public suffering can become a personal responsibility.
Chapters: 00:10 Teaching Style and Theme 02:08 Wholesome Unpleasant 06:15 Ethics as One Fruit 09:08 From Avoiding Harm 13:42 The Backward Step 16:33 Moralization and Resistance 24:24 Sensitivity and Vulnerability 29:45 Feeling, Renunciation, Courage 36:09 In Place of the Friend
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/11572

item.title: Your Face: The Gaze, Self-view, Intimacy and Letting Go (Online Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when we really meet another face, and how quickly does that meeting reveal self-view, projection, intimacy, and fear? The talk begins with the charge of eye contact and moves through ethics, boundaries, and the way a gaze can either harden the self or soften it. From a Buddha line on refuge to a long detour through Levinas and the film Her, it keeps returning to the same question: how do we love without turning people into fixed objects or roles?
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:46 A small thought 01:25 Seeing and being seen 03:36 Ethics in the gaze 08:57 Presence and mirror 11:49 Self-view in contact 19:44 Relational practice 26:55 The face as command 35:10 Less self, more refuge 39:37 Love without possession
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61869/

item.title: Vulnerability, Porousness, Equanimity & Love (Online Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if vulnerability is not a problem to solve, but the condition that makes refuge, love, and practice necessary? The talk opens with the image of the human condition as wilderness, then moves through dukkha, the feeling of being always a step behind our real life, and the tension between modern ways of honoring fragility and the classical Buddhist emphasis on relinquishment. From there it turns to equanimity as a lived response: not numbing or denial, but a way of meeting pain without fighting it, widening perspective, and letting care become less sticky and more spacious.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Wilderness and refuge 02:18 The unlived life 06:04 Pain as the teacher 08:10 Vulnerability in practice 12:43 Fragility and animality 21:36 Classical release 26:00 Clinging and release 32:00 Equanimity and love 36:39 Wide perspective, quiet heart
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61761/

item.title: Dialectics of Practice - Opening and Protection (Online Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you practice when both the world and the heart feel exposed? The talk moves through a series of balances: effort and relaxation, compassion and equanimity, seriousness and play, opening and protection. It draws on grief, pandemic-era uncertainty, and a few outside voices to suggest that insight is not forced; it is prepared for through rest, refuge, and a steadier relationship with experience.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the retreat 02:04 Middle path, wholesome balance 05:47 Love and wisdom 08:33 Relaxation and caring 11:34 Seriousness and grief 18:02 Grief and lightness 19:51 Awareness as space 22:48 Opening with protection 29:16 Insight needs support
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61759/

item.title: Addressing Coronavirus with a Dharma View- Monday Night Dharma Talk
item.description:
When coronavirus starts making the mind feel wobbly, what actually belongs to Dharma, and what belongs to science, public health, and ordinary care? The talk moves from naming fear and compulsive news-checking to grounding the listener in refuge, present-moment attention, and the difference between productive caution and anxious spiraling. It then widens into interdependence, metta, and the claim that in moments like this the heart can be trained to offer steadiness, not just for oneself but for others.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:09 Opening the frame 01:28 Dharma and medicine 04:27 Practice in the moment 09:32 Fear and worry 16:22 Caring for now 20:13 Resilience and friendship 28:01 Interdependence and letting go 34:39 Radical hearts 46:24 Closing blessings
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61133/

item.title: Death and the Poignancy of Life
item.description:
What happens when death is not pushed away but brought close enough to change how we live, love, and practice? The talk moves from a wry Onion joke and a contrast between “writing” and “dancing” into a steady inquiry into finitude, grief, and the ways meditation can become a kind of grasping. It then widens through Buddhist remembrances, hospice stories, psychology, and poets to ask whether a life shaped by mortality can become softer, more responsible, and somehow complete.
Location: New York Insight Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:23 Opening the topic 03:07 Writing and dancing 10:03 Birth and mortality 16:55 Practicing with death 25:30 Resisting and meaning 29:40 Finitude and grief 36:54 Loss makes life vivid 41:27 Fear, identity, and softness 50:53 Becoming and letting go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60274/

item.title: How Goodness Accrues on the Path
item.description:
What if practice is less about becoming a better self and more about letting goodness accumulate through repeated, ordinary contact with experience? The talk moves from the Buddha’s instruction to name suffering and investigate it, into a longer reflection on how Dharma talks can steady, unsettle, and reorient the mind without always offering neat answers. From there it widens into a gradual picture of transformation: less self-obsession, more honesty about desire, more transparency, and a growing capacity to live without constantly measuring progress.
Chapters: 00:00 Why Dharma Talks Matter 01:35 Investigate, Don't Collapse 05:14 Transmission and Unsettling 09:38 What Freedom Looks Like 14:06 Gradual Change 18:12 Wanting and Not-Searching 23:44 Desire, Goals, and Intuition 30:17 Transparency and the Past 36:02 Ethical Alignment
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10619

item.title: Attention, Wisdom, Grief and Love (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do attention and wisdom become love, and why does that turning often feel like grief? The talk moves from a reading on finitude and responsibility into stories from retreat, dance, and ordinary life to show how paying attention softens the mind, loosens clinging, and makes room for care. From there it traces the overlap of wisdom and compassion, arguing that letting go is not indifference but a more open way of meeting others and oneself.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Finitude and responsibility 02:47 Silent retreat question 06:14 Love as attention 08:31 Dancing into presence 10:49 Wisdom and love 18:00 Grief as letting go 26:32 Ownership and refuge 38:39 Experience loosens 44:03 Insight becomes love
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/57939/

item.title: Loving the Self to Death
item.description:
The talk begins with a small, ordinary fall in the rain and turns that moment into a question about how we meet difficulty without turning inward into self-attack. From there, the speaker moves through envy, the comparing mind, and the habit of making practice a battleground, then offers a different instruction: to “love it to death,” meaning to stay close enough to experience that it can’t harden into an object or enemy. The last part widens into memory, autobiography, and not-self, asking how practice can soften our relationship to the past and to the sense of “me” itself.
Chapters: 00:30 Opening Story 04:38 Envy as Teacher 08:58 Love It to Death 12:58 Peaks and Valleys 18:18 Acceptance and Love 25:01 Working With the Past 30:53 Blessing Old Stories 36:08 Self as Process
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10281

item.title: Brian Lesage, Matthew Brensilver: The Interpersonal Dimension of the Dharma (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when Dharma is tested not in solitude, but in the pressure of other people, speech, and the need to be seen? The talk moves from the vulnerability that makes clinging and control arise, through the ways relationship reveals greed, hatred, and delusion, into concrete practice: pausing, listening, and speaking more skillfully. It also widens into a harder question about ethics and a good life, using Shantideva and Peter Singer to explore generosity without turning practice into perfectionism. By the end, the emphasis is less on being right or polished than on becoming more free, more responsive, and less locked inside self-view.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Brian Lesage
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the dialogue 04:06 Relationships as practice 07:49 Need and belonging 12:24 Freedom, ethics, and views 19:29 Listening and speech 24:14 Pausing before speaking 29:42 Praise and difficult speech 38:28 Bodhisattva questions 47:15 Ethics without perfection
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/57124/

item.title: The Story of Jumping Mouse (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
A quiet bedtime-story reading becomes a map of practice: a busy mouse hears something faint, follows it into the unknown, and keeps discovering that the path asks for more than she planned. As the story unfolds, the talk moves through doubt, curiosity, disorientation, humility, renunciation, and the way trust deepens when the old ground falls away. The speaker keeps returning to the mouse, buffalo, wolf, and eagle as ways to ask what it means to give oneself to a path that is bigger than self-improvement.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 01:51 Why stories matter 06:28 Hearing the river 11:40 Walking with raccoon 18:10 River and mountain 30:19 Choosing the wider path 40:49 Views and surrender 54:39 Refuge and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/56979/

item.title: Equanimity and Samadhi
item.description:
What if the mind’s urge to land, explain, and improve everything is exactly what keeps it restless? The talk moves from ordinary clinging—pleasure, views, conventions, self-view—into the quieter ground of samadhi as a fruit of letting go, using the image of a ray of light that cannot land anywhere when craving is absent. Along the way it brings in worry, technology, self-referential thinking, and a few short citations to show how steadiness of mind is less about control than about softening, settling, and making peace with imperfection.
Location: Vallecitos Mountain Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Resting In Silence 04:11 Clinging And Safety 06:32 When Consciousness Lands 09:45 Samadhi As Fruit 13:44 Restlessness And Worry 21:57 The Self At Rest 27:57 Thought Without Shame 35:07 Equanimity And Pleasure 40:56 Practice In Daily Life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/56706/

item.title: The Role of Sila, Samadhi and Panna in Interpersonal Life
item.description:
How do sila, samadhi, and panna matter when the real test is not the cushion but other people? The talk moves from learning as a whole-life process, through vulnerability, speech, and the traps of venting and self-protection, into the subtler ways mind states harden into story and identity. It keeps returning to one practical question: what changes when we stop fixing ourselves and others into rigid roles, and instead become more trustworthy, more open, and easier to be with?
Chapters: 00:31 Whole-life learning 01:33 Insight that generalizes 08:04 Vulnerability and dependence 11:01 Relationship amplifies practice 19:36 Trust, speech, and feedback 29:08 Samadhi as agility 37:16 Less self-curation 49:59 Not creating others 56:05 Closing blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10080

item.title: Removing the Thorns of Memory and Becoming
item.description:
How do memory and becoming shape the very feeling of a self? The talk moves from present-moment practice into the way memory, planning, worry, and even identity arise together, suggesting that healing comes less from escaping the past than from letting it be fully present and gradually digested. Along the way, the speaker brings in Elie Wiesel, a neuroscience study, Thich Nhat Hanh, and a film by Kore-eda to show how the past lives inside the present, and how fear of the future keeps the mind leaning forward. The final movement turns toward nibbana as the cessation of becoming, with practice described as settling, softening, and relinquishing the urge to become someone new.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and context 03:30 Presence and memory 09:20 Memory in the brain 14:05 Rewriting the past 19:50 What we become 24:16 Undigested harm 30:07 Blessing the past 36:29 Fear and becoming 44:30 Freedom as release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10076
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
How do memory and becoming shape the very feeling of a self? The talk moves from present-moment practice into the way memory, planning, worry, and even identity arise together, suggesting that healing comes less from escaping the past than from letting it be fully present and gradually digested. Along the way, the speaker brings in Elie Wiesel, a neuroscience study, Thich Nhat Hanh, and a film by Kore-eda to show how the past lives inside the present, and how fear of the future keeps the mind leaning forward. The final movement turns toward nibbana as the cessation of becoming, with practice described as settling, softening, and relinquishing the urge to become someone new.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and context 03:30 Presence and memory 09:20 Memory in the brain 14:05 Rewriting the past 19:50 What we become 24:16 Undigested harm 30:07 Blessing the past 36:29 Fear and becoming 44:30 Freedom as release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10076

item.title: Seven Factors of Awakening
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple, bodily example: a foot spasm, and the shift from trying to make it go away to using it as a teachable moment. From there, it moves into the seven factors of awakening as a living process—mindfulness, investigation, energy, delight, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity—showing how they support one another rather than appearing as a fixed sequence. Along the way, the speaker emphasizes that practice is less about manufacturing goodness and more about releasing clinging, softening reactivity, and learning to be changed by experience. Personal stories, a sutta story about the Buddha in sickness, and a few contemporary references ground the teaching in the ordinary mess of pain, ambivalence, and attention.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening into release 06:01 Seven factors overview 08:29 Learning mindfulness 11:32 Equanimity and balance 18:07 Softening into experience 24:06 Tolerating ambivalence 28:00 Curiosity and investigation 34:43 Energy, delight, calm 43:03 Collected mind, not thought
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10075

item.title: Reflecting On An Ajahn Sucitto Quote
item.description:
What if the restlessness of mind is not a personal failure, but the very shape of selfing trying to find ground? Starting from a quote by Ajahn Sucitto, the talk moves through busyness, meditation avoidance, self-comparison, and the subtle agitation of identity, using Pascal, David Foster Wallace, and other references to ask what happens when we stop trying to distract ourselves. It gradually turns toward a quieter practice movement: folding the self into awareness, meeting experience with loving acceptance, and finding that the real ground is less in self-strategy than in a kind of nonclinging tenderness.
Chapters: 00:34 Ajahn Sucitto's quote 04:09 Busyness as human condition 09:10 Solitude and avoidance 12:26 Self, agitation, and comparison 20:16 Mind and narrative self 25:19 Loving the mess 33:01 Non-dwelling and mortality 40:25 Closing blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9909

item.title: Impressionistic vs Precise Technique
item.description:
Is meditation best approached as a precise technique, or as something more impressionistic and responsive? The talk moves between those poles, first weighing the value of simple commitment and then opening into a detailed noting practice that distinguishes feeling in the body, mental images, and inner talk. From there it turns toward memory, suffering, and the possibility of letting experience be seen clearly enough that the past can be met with more wisdom and less struggle.
Chapters: 00:30 Teaching and technique 05:28 Learning and loving 07:24 Memory shapes the present 10:36 Three channels of experience 20:14 Thought and selfing 25:14 Noting with clarity 28:50 Blessing the past 34:56 Questions and release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9908

item.title: Monday Night Dharma Talk
item.description:
Curiosity is framed here as the engine of practice: not discipline first, but a living interest that makes it possible to look at mind and body without pretending. The talk moves from the difference between genuine investigation and forced self-improvement, into the subtler work of seeing delusion, rumination, secrecy, and the ways we protect an outgrown story of self. It closes by widening the scope of practice outward, asking how honesty, feedback from others, and greater self-knowledge make us more trustworthy and less opaque.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Curiosity Over Discipline 05:49 The Engine of Practice 10:47 Do Not Force Interest 15:13 Investigation as Learning 25:25 Seeing Suffering Clearly 32:58 Inquiry and Distance 38:41 Secrets and Honesty 47:12 Feedback and Disorientation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/55433/

item.title: Practice Note: Note your life away
item.description:
How does a simple mental note change what we take ourselves to be? The talk moves from the practical use of noting—breath, walking, bodily movement—to a subtler claim: that whatever is noted is no longer quite the ground we stand on. By naming body, room, weight, emotion, and thought, the speaker shows how practice can turn experience into something seen as arising and vanishing, rather than something to identify with. In that sense, the talk says, we keep "noting our life away."
Chapters: 00:00 Opening frame 00:30 Noting as awareness 01:14 More than stabilization 01:52 Stepping off identification 02:26 Everything can be noted 03:11 Noting life away 03:42 Closing wish
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9858

item.title: Dharmette: Maturing our autobiography
item.description:
What if mindfulness is not only a way of stepping out of our stories, but also a way of maturing them? The talk moves from the limits of living inside narrative, through the usefulness of seeing thoughts as transient events, into a quieter claim: practice can help us revisit a jagged autobiography, let undigested experience arise, and rewrite the past with more wisdom and love. By the end, the emphasis is less on abandoning the self-story than on becoming able to put it down because it feels more whole and less gripping.
Chapters: 00:09 Stepping out of narrative 01:43 Why stories limit 03:02 Space around thought 04:39 Maturing the story 06:07 A jagged autobiography 08:16 Memory in practice 09:56 Remembering with Dharma 11:42 Putting the story down
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9859

item.title: Compulsive Thinking, Concentration & Equanimity
item.description:
What happens when the mind keeps reaching for control, even in meditation? This talk moves from the pressure of New York intensity and compulsive problem-solving into a careful look at worry, mind wandering, and the way thoughts claim too much authority. The speaker then turns toward concentration and equanimity as forms of letting go: not shutting experience down, but meeting it without interference so life can feel more settled, more poignant, and less driven by the need to optimize every moment.
Location: New York Insight Meditation Center
Chapters: 06:15 What experience becomes 10:54 Samadhi as letting go 18:51 Worry and modern life 28:27 A sheltered mind 33:52 Thought’s false gravity 40:03 Silence without defense 46:10 Pleasure, attachment, and restraint 52:41 Enough, and waking up 59:10 Closing reflections
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/55103/

item.title: Reflections on Sila
item.description:
How does sila become more than a list of precepts and turn into a lived refusal to harm? The talk moves from a personal reading of “make of yourself a refuge for all beings” into a meditation on safety, humility, grief, and the way letting go softens the heart. It then widens into ethical questions about self-justification, responsibility to others, and whether our habits of care are actually large enough.
Chapters: 00:30 Sila as refuge 06:07 Safety and self-knowledge 09:58 Letting go and grief 13:06 Universal and personal 17:10 Heart, karma, and love 24:19 Emptiness as nonviolence 27:25 What goodness requires 34:11 Shelter and enough 41:02 Completeness and offering
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9776

item.title: Technology as an Existential Threat to Mindfulness - Matthew Brensilver and Randy Fernando
item.description:
Technology can feel like a useful companion, but the talk keeps asking when it starts training the mind toward fragmentation, urgency, and dependence. It moves from a quiet opening meditation into a plain-eyed look at phones, feeds, notifications, and the way they shape attention, habits, and even what feels true. The discussion stays nuanced: mindfulness is not presented as a moral demand to eliminate every distraction, but as a way to see more clearly what is being fed and what is being weakened. Later, the talk turns practical, with concrete suggestions for changing phone use, reducing stimulation, and noticing the pull toward mindlessness without piling on guilt.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 02:06 Arriving and settling 09:34 Attention and mind wandering 20:23 Time slipping away 24:45 Phones as training 35:49 What tech rewards 48:37 Division and deepfakes 58:58 Safer products and band-aids 01:10:59 Mindlessness and guilt 01:25:31 Practical habits
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/54975/

item.title: Monday Night Dharma Talk
item.description:
What if the hard edge of practice is not breakthrough, but grieving what we can no longer hold? The talk traces how mindfulness, compassion, and letting go can bring up loss, through examples ranging from an anger retreat that felt grief-soaked to images of the clenched fist, the foggy road, and the “valley of lost things.” Along the way, the speaker argues that grief is not a detour from Dharma but one of the ways the heart opens, deepens, and becomes more intimate with impermanence. The talk ends by sitting quietly with the question of how to honor what is lost without trying to possess it.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:54 Three Questions 02:21 Grief on Retreat 05:57 Why Grief Matters 10:36 Poetry of Loss 16:55 Letting Go 22:24 Ownership and Gift 28:26 No Steps For Grief 34:35 The Valley of Lost Things 40:25 Questions and Closing Sitting
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/53866/

item.title: Sila as Our Practice in the World (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
The talk begins with a plain question: how do we live ethically when we are vulnerable, dependent, and often ashamed of that dependence? From there, it moves from mindfulness to sila, arguing that the real way practice enters the world is through attention to how our choices affect others, and through the feedback that exposes clinging, self-protection, and fear. Along the way, the speaker uses brief references to Mother Teresa, Ajahn Sumedho, Ajahn Chah, and a closing Leonard Cohen lyric to keep returning to the same point: wisdom and love are not separate, and letting go can make us more responsive rather than less.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Vulnerability and dependence 02:23 Belonging and mindfulness 06:36 Ethics as the practice 10:30 Love, clinging, and letting go 16:00 Wisdom and humility 24:25 Feedback and self-view 29:07 Freefall and courage 36:19 Moral incoherence 41:28 Radical hearts
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/53265/

item.title: Untitled (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
The talk begins with human vulnerability: we start dependent, remain incomplete, and carry emotions that map our needs and fears. From there it turns to a quiet argument that ethics, not self-improvement slogans, is the real place where practice becomes visible—through how we respond to others, to feedback, and to our own clinging. The speaker keeps returning to the tension between wanting to be seen as good and the harder work of letting that self-image loosen, so that wisdom, love, and non-harm can actually converge. By the end, the question is not whether one is saintly, but how to live in a way that can bear the truth of interdependence and still act with courage.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Dependence and Shame 02:52 Belonging and Mindfulness 06:47 Ethics as Practice 15:53 Wisdom and Humility 24:39 Feedback and Self-View 32:05 Freefall and Courage 36:39 One Fruit of Practice 41:51 Radical Hearts
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62481/

item.title: Sīla, Samādhi, Pañña
item.description:
What if sīla, samādhi, and paññā are not separate goals but one movement: becoming less harmful, more settled, and more clear? The talk begins with a practical response to the hindrances, then widens into a meditation on suffering, self-judgment, and the way ethical conduct, collectedness, and wisdom support each other like different parts of one fruit. Along the way, the speaker uses images of a refuge-bearing heart, a closed heart being gently worked with, and the problem of ownership to show how practice loosens fear and makes room for love.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Hindrances and response 03:51 Four Noble Truths 08:12 One fruit, three trainings 11:49 Ethics as refuge 19:14 Self-judgment loosens 29:31 Collecting the mind 35:52 Thought and narrative 42:08 Wisdom and non-ownership 47:05 Gift, identity, release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/52718/

item.title: Practice Notes
item.description:
What does the mind need right now: effort, gentleness, reassurance, or a simple question that cuts through self-improvement? The talk moves through that practical territory by treating practice as a series of small, well-timed “pep talks” that can be tailored to the moment. It also opens briefly into the value of one-on-one practice discussions and the idea that Dharma is worked out in a very idiosyncratic way, person by person.
Chapters: 00:30 Opening Practice Notes 01:15 Personal Practice Discussions 02:44 Why Guidance Must Fit 03:51 Learning Inner Pep Talks 04:49 Energy, Gentleness, Inquiry 05:56 Returning To Present Goodness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9287

item.title: Dharmette: The Human Condition
item.description:
How do joy, grief, fear, compassion, and even laughter fit into one human life? The talk begins with the felt confusion of being alive in a time of suffering and climate anxiety, then turns to a lived example from a breathing practice where hearing someone cry brought tears, deep belly laughter, and a surprising sense of peace at the same time. From that experience, the speaker points toward a fuller acknowledgment of the human condition—one that does not neatly separate pain from joy, but lets both be present.
Chapters: 00:11 Being Human 01:18 One Moment at a Time 02:27 What Happens in the News 02:43 A Practice Setting 03:35 Tears and Compassion 04:57 Laughter in Grief 06:45 Peace Underneath 08:03 The Human Condition
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9288

item.title: Ethical Implications of Mindfulness
item.description:
How does a meditation practice become an ethical life, especially when its effects are slow, hidden, and hard to measure? The talk moves from the temptation to check progress breath by breath to a wider view of cultivation, then turns toward the ways insight spills into speech, relationships, giving, and responsibility. Along the way, references to Suzuki Roshi, Vaclav Havel, Ram Dass, Bhikkhu Bodhi, and effective altruism press the question of what it means to let compassion become concrete action.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Measuring Progress 03:21 Little by Little 05:44 Conscience and Responsibility 08:49 Compassion Needs Action 10:33 Insight Must Be Lived 17:15 Practice in Relationship 28:56 Compassion and Giving 36:23 Doing the Most Good 42:36 Without Expecting Applause
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50747/

item.title: Brian Lesage, Matthew Brensilver: Dharma Debate: Samvega versus Softening and Receiving (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you keep practice from becoming either a hard push forward or a passive staying in place? The talk moves through that tension by setting samvega, urgency and resolve, against softening, receiving, and the willingness to let experience be exactly as it is. It draws on the Buddha crossing the flood, an early discourse on dread and the thorn in the heart, Dogen’s “backward step,” and a closing poem by Marie Howe to bring the question back to ordinary life. Rather than resolving the issue neatly, the talk leaves the listener with a live inquiry into how effort, ease, and freedom meet in practice.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Brian Lesage
Chapters: 00:00 The Middle Path 02:00 Urgency and Ease 07:45 Crossing the Flood 10:05 Samvega and Dread 18:44 Burnout and Softening 26:54 The Backward Step 33:14 Curiosity and Renunciation 40:09 Freedom in Ordinary Life 42:16 What the Living Do
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50687/

item.title: Samadhi (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if concentration is not something you force, but what appears as you stop creating friction with experience? The talk moves from the pressure of discursive thought and attention-fragmenting technology into a practical case for samadhi as steadiness, shelter, and the ground for insight. Along the way, the speaker questions the habit of treating thinking as the main way to solve life, and keeps returning to patience, letting go, and learning to be present without strain.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and presence 00:57 Compassion and stability 04:44 What samadhi means 10:06 Attention under pressure 13:14 Shelter from the storm 20:14 Thought and story 29:03 Pleasure and attachment 35:10 Patience and letting go 45:36 Natural stillness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50672/

item.title: Metta Reflections: Opening to Life Requires Love (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What does love have to do with opening to life as it actually is? The talk moves through metta as cultivation, concentration, and purification, then widens into the claim that insight practice itself needs a softening of the heart in order to meet experience moment by moment. A story from teen retreats shows this in concrete form: resistance eases not through force, but through a simple, caring touch. The talk then settles into a gentle guided sequence of wishing well—"May I be happy. May I be safe. May I be at peace."
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 What Metta Does 02:13 Three Functions 04:23 Love and Insight 05:38 Teen Retreat Story 09:06 Connection and Poignancy 11:02 Facing Innocence 16:36 Softening Through Uncertainty 18:47 Guided Metta 23:27 Humility and Care
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50656/

item.title: Delusion
item.description:
Delusion is treated here as the mind’s most camouflaged habit: the one that makes our assumptions feel like reality until they are disturbed. The talk moves from a threefold Buddhist personality map to the quieter work of noticing self-deception, rationalization, and the ways we use thought to avoid uncertainty, pain, or shame. Along the way, it keeps returning to humility, mindfulness, and the willingness to take feedback as ways of loosening the stories we live inside. The practical center of the talk is not analysis for its own sake, but learning to recognize when curiosity is genuine and when it is just another defense.
Chapters: 00:21 Three Roots 02:06 Delusion in Daily Life 06:38 What Delusion Feels Like 11:41 Humility and Self-Deception 16:56 Willingness to Know 21:50 Wise View and Rationalizing 27:11 Ambivalence and Confusion 33:05 Signs and Antidotes 40:56 Lowering the Waterline
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8976

item.title: Practice Note: Noticing Something New
item.description:
The talk asks how attention can become so familiar that it stops noticing what is new. Using the image of a theater of the mind, it points to both the usefulness of steady, routine looking and the possibility of deliberately looking in fresh ways. The practice invitation is simple: pause, question your usual way of seeing, and see whether anything unexpected appears.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Context 00:30 Grooved Attention 01:00 Practice And Familiarity 01:49 Looking In New Ways 02:49 Letting The Frame Go 03:28 Seeing With New Eyes
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8898

item.title: Dharmette: Worrying and Tranquility
item.description:
Worry is treated here as a strategy for managing the insecurity of being human: the mind keeps leaning forward into imagined threats, hoping that vigilance will prevent pain. From there, the talk turns toward tranquility as something shaped by conditions rather than willpower alone, with clinging and letting go presented as the larger frame. It then gets concrete, considering overcommitment, time in nature, and the ways technology can train attention toward restlessness instead of ease.
Chapters: 00:26 Human intensity 02:18 Worry as strategy 05:05 Longing for peace 07:14 Causality and letting go 09:31 Simpler commitments 11:18 Restorative nature 11:35 Technology and compulsion 14:32 Quiet conclusion
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8899

item.title: Samadhi
item.description:
How does the mind settle when it stops reaching for the next moment? The talk moves from the restlessness of attention—shaped by craving, technology, and the habit of narrating experience—toward a quieter possibility of samadhi: less friction with what is arising, and more willingness to stay with it. Along the way, the speaker argues that concentration is not a narrow squeeze of effort but a gathered, steady attention that grows when the gaining mind relaxes, and he warns against turning special states into something to chase.
Chapters: 00:08 Opening reflections 02:06 Less friction, more gathering 05:55 Attention under pressure 10:40 Waiting without seeking 15:03 Samadhi as refuge 20:13 Narration and fear 26:19 Mindfulness and concentration 31:36 Not chasing peak states 36:25 Letting go of will
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8786

item.title: Grief
item.description:
What if grief is not a detour from the path, but part of what letting go actually feels like? The talk moves from the blunt fact that love in a changing world will hurt, into a quieter argument that grief is the slow, sometimes painful release of clinging rather than a problem to solve quickly. Along the way, the speaker brings in a few vivid images—an open hand, a wound that heals slowly, driving at night in fog, a heart that breaks open into groundlessness—to show how practice can make room for loss without shutting down. The talk also widens from personal sorrow to collective grief, asking what it means to face harm honestly rather than stay defended against it.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Topic 01:48 Non-clinging and Grief 04:25 What Grief Feels Like 07:26 Practice and Collective Loss 10:03 Risk, Darkness, Wound 14:00 Buddha, Attachment, Impermanence 21:06 Fog and Titration 25:44 Heartbreak and Groundlessness 33:15 Mortality and Gratitude
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8761

item.title: Concluding talk and Q&A
item.description:
How do impermanence, suffering, and not-self become something more than ideas? The talk moves from a quiet closing reflection into a clear argument that practice is really a long process of maturation: learning stability, knowing oneself, and meeting life without getting weighed down by it. A few stories — about an ordinary Zen teacher, a retreat question about how often to sit, and a closing anecdote about people leaving retreat too abruptly — keep the teaching grounded in lived practice. The Q&A then turns to a practical problem: how to stay connected with others while changes in practice alter your way of being.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening gratitude 01:56 Three characteristics 04:16 Maturation through practice 08:03 Ordinariness and humility 10:11 Stability and confidence 12:09 Serious, not grim 14:55 Retreat rhythm 21:40 Quiet and connection 30:16 Generosity as the field
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8751

item.title: Talk: Insight into Dukkha
item.description:
Can any moment of your life be enough to vanish into? The talk opens with a film image of the recently deceased recreating their happiest memory, then turns that question back toward practice: whether we can meet ordinary experience with enough stability, confidence, and openness to see freedom in it. From there it moves through dukkha as a basic fact of being human, not a personal failure, and asks what happens when suffering is studied carefully instead of resisted. The talk gradually shifts that recognition toward equanimity, grief, and a more condition-independent happiness.
Chapters: 00:00 After Life as image 02:47 Stability and confidence 07:01 What dukkha means 11:09 Study suffering carefully 18:13 Retreat and realism 22:33 Not your fault 28:24 Grief as doorway 34:12 Equanimity and love 40:35 Condition-free happiness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8746

item.title: Talk: Cultivating Well-being for Insight
item.description:
What if insight depends first on building enough steadiness to meet experience without flinching? The talk moves from the limits of therapy and the sting of contingency into a gradual case for safety, ethics, concentration, and loving-kindness as the conditions that let the heart open without being overwhelmed. Along the way, the speaker uses a child’s fear of mountain lions, a lake simile for samadhi, and brief readings from Batchelor, Nietzsche, and Rumi to show how well-being and insight support each other.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with the rain 01:05 Practice starts close in 04:51 Insight and contingency 08:36 Titrating truth 13:05 Safety, ethics, refuge 18:07 A field of non-harming 23:07 Blamelessness and transparency 26:29 Gathering the mind 30:47 Rapture and loving-kindness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8742

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Closing Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Beginning with the question of how to meet a hard public moment without turning away, the talk moves from quiet retreat silence into an explicit turn toward love, non-harming, and ethical responsibility. It draws on Martin Luther King Jr., Leonard Cohen, Norman Fischer, Nyanaponika, Thomas Merton, and Shantideva to show how practice is not escape but a way of living with suffering, aspiration, and uncertainty. The talk builds toward the image of the monk on the vine: precarious, held between loss and possibility, still finding the strawberry. It ends by widening that image into a communal dedication of merit, asking how retreat practice can become care for one another and the world.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 00:00 Settling Into the Day 05:05 Love With Strength 13:38 Treaty and Ethical Grief 20:07 Refuge Becomes Practice 30:28 Zen Paradox and Freedom 36:58 Purpose in Daily Life 53:30 Aspiration Without Control 01:01:32 The Tiger and the Vine 01:17:56 Dedication and Departure
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48357/

item.title: Bhavatanha Mortality and Letting Go
item.description:
What happens when the mind’s push to keep going, secure itself, and become something starts to narrow life? The talk moves from a film about the recently dead choosing one moment to live forever into a clear inquiry into mortality, craving for existence, and the ways fear turns life into a field of threat, identity, and future-planning. From there, it turns practical: how meditation can quietly expose that forward-tumbling, and what loosens when the sense of self thins and the moment is allowed to stand on its own.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Framing 01:48 The Film Premise 04:15 Mortality and Tension 09:30 Existence Bias 15:50 Death Anxiety Shapes Culture 20:29 Sitting Like the Dying 26:15 Bhava Tanha in Practice 33:46 Self as Conditioned 36:24 Cessation of Becoming
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8504

item.title: Monday Night Meditation Talk (Drop-in at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if the hardest thing to see is not greed or aversion, but the delusion that feels like common sense? This talk moves from contemporary research on greed, hate, and delusion into a careful examination of how self-story, certainty, and habit can quietly organize a life. Along the way, the speaker brings in images from David Foster Wallace, Catherine Schultz, and a few teachers to show how practice can mean learning to notice confusion without shame, take feedback, and stay close to what is actually happening.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Research and the three poisons 03:14 Delusion as habit 08:09 Water we cannot see 11:52 Willingness to be wrong 17:48 Wise view and self-story 26:41 Meditating with honesty 34:23 Disruption as opportunity 47:48 Humility in the heart 55:47 Keep looking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/47160/

item.title: Practice Note
item.description:
What changes when you really turn toward the moment instead of bracing against it? The talk returns to a small but important movement: paying attention can bring relaxation in the heart, even when the moment itself feels unpleasant or intense. It also notes the tension that mindfulness may first make discomfort clearer before it reveals a deeper sense of inner safety, and it cautions against half-hearted attention that only imitates awakening. By the end, the emphasis is on recognizing the part of experience that is soothed by awareness and letting that surrender do its quiet work.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening note 00:19 Attention relaxes 00:54 Difficulty and safety 01:24 Turning toward the moment 02:08 Not half-hearted 02:34 Surrender and soothing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8228

item.title: Dharmette
item.description:
The talk begins with a familiar Dharma tension: practice can sound full of paradox, as if letting go and striving, self-cultivation and selflessness, all point in opposite directions. From there, the speaker uses a New York Times essay on “living in the present” to sort out a distinction between activities that aim at an endpoint and those that are complete in the doing itself. That thread leads into meditation, where Zen non-striving and insight-language about progress and awakening are set side by side, but the emphasis lands on how practice is actually lived moment by moment.
Chapters: 00:15 Dharma and paradox 02:08 One world, not two 03:48 A present-moment essay 04:45 Telic and atelic 06:54 What present means 08:27 Practice and attention 10:07 Meditation question 11:16 Here, not elsewhere
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8229

item.title: Perils and Promises of the First Day
item.description:
The first full day of retreat is where the mind most easily turns intensity into a story about failure, and the talk keeps returning to that problem: how to stay with difficulty without making it mean that practice is broken. From there it moves through the pressures of wanting, aversion, doubt, and the habit of trying to optimize life, then points toward a steadier response: equanimity, curiosity, and a willingness to meet imperfection directly. Along the way, it uses retreat stories and brief references to Thomas Merton, Kobun Chino, Thich Nhat Hanh, and others to show that practice is not an escape from friction but a way of suffering more skillfully and loosening the grip of future-thinking.
Chapters: 00:00 First Day Reality 01:08 Mood, Not Truth 04:42 Peace and War 07:15 Pleasures of the Path 14:03 Hardship with Purpose 18:28 Wanting, Aversion, Fatigue 28:04 Change and Responsibility 32:30 Equanimity and Karmic Knots 43:09 Completeness and Confidence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8128

The first full day of retreat is where the mind most easily turns intensity into a story about failure, and the talk keeps returning to that problem: how to stay with difficulty without making it mean that practice is broken. From there it moves through the pressures of wanting, aversion, doubt, and the habit of trying to optimize life, then points toward a steadier response: equanimity, curiosity, and a willingness to meet imperfection directly. Along the way, it uses retreat stories and brief references to Thomas Merton, Kobun Chino, Thich Nhat Hanh, and others to show that practice is not an escape from friction but a way of suffering more skillfully and loosening the grip of future-thinking.
Chapters: 00:00 First Day Reality 01:08 Mood, Not Truth 04:42 Peace and War 07:15 Pleasures of the Path 14:03 Hardship with Purpose 18:28 Wanting, Aversion, Fatigue 28:04 Change and Responsibility 32:30 Equanimity and Karmic Knots 43:09 Completeness and Confidence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8128

item.title: Surrender, Power, Sila
item.description:
What does it mean to surrender in Dharma without giving away discernment? The talk begins with the charged atmosphere of retreat practice, then moves into a careful look at how surrender, power, and ethical conduct intertwine in teachers, students, sanghas, and institutions. Along the way, it weighs the pull toward control against the deeper release that can open practice, while staying alert to the real risks of misuse and projection. A question period brings the theme down to lived experience, including tears on retreat, long-term change, and what it means to let go safely.
Chapters: 00:22 Retreat and Surrender 02:33 Surrender and Power 05:35 Augustine and Nietzsche 08:24 Giving Power Away 11:30 Trust and Caution 15:40 Mindfulness as Letting Go 20:25 Teachers and Boundaries 25:39 Sila and Misuse 33:05 Questions and Closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7995

item.title: Bringing Dharma Into Interpersonal and Communal Life (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
The talk begins by questioning the habit of measuring meditation by the surface weather of the mind, then uses images like getting wet in a fog to suggest that real change is often gradual and hard to see. From there it widens into the relationship between retreat and ordinary life: practice is not something added onto living, but something that reshapes speech, attention, and the way we meet other people. The talk keeps returning to the tension between inward clarity and outward responsibility, asking how insight becomes compassion in family, community, and the wider world.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Measuring retreat 06:27 Fog and gradual change 09:00 Practice becomes life 11:19 What silence reveals 12:42 Compassion must act 18:12 Speech and relationship 26:38 Softening self-view 32:31 Widening care 37:05 Justifying a life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/41183/
