item.title: Intentions for Practice and the Jewel of Curiosity (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if curiosity is a stronger guide than discipline, and mindfulness is just its living form? The talk moves from a small mouse-in-a-trap story to a wide-ranging reflection on practice intentions, urgency, renunciation, and the danger of living only inside our stories. It then turns toward the felt difference between simply experiencing something and knowing it as experience, with equanimity framed as a radical permission to feel and a way to let life arrive without so much friction.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Mouse as mirror 02:55 Practice and trust 09:36 Urgency and renunciation 15:38 Love and freedom 20:16 Curiosity becomes mindfulness 25:06 Fresh attention 30:59 Equanimity and knots 38:36 Beyond story
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/41128/

item.title: Day 1: Metta for Self (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when self-kindness is not treated as a special mood, but as the natural response to seeing the full poignancy of being human? The talk moves from a brief orientation to a simple metta practice for self, then keeps widening the frame: mindfulness and heart practice are not fully separate, and the more clearly we see, the more reason there is to love. A Buddha teaching about making of oneself a refuge becomes the center of the talk, leading into questions of safety, steadiness, and what it means for a heart to be trustworthy for others.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Orientation 00:45 Heart Practices 01:45 Seeing More Clearly 03:55 Refuge and Safety 05:23 The Wish To Be Happy 07:19 Settling Into Phrases 13:06 Heart of the Practice 16:57 Listening Inwardly 25:37 Three Ways of Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/41099/

item.title: Clinging, letting go and the path of sila, samadhi and panna
item.description:
What if the heart’s habit of holding on is the very thing that keeps suffering in place? The talk moves from an opening anecdote about summarizing Dharma to a close look at ownership, clinging, and the way letting go can reshape ethics, concentration, wisdom, love, and even one’s sense of self. Along the way, the speaker uses the Satipatthana Sutta, a story about dana, and images of open air and a sunbeam that does not land to show what becomes possible when experience is no longer grasped at.
Chapters: 00:00 A Dharma in one sentence 04:12 Mindfulness as non-clinging 06:21 The habit of ownership 10:48 Dana and the feel of giving 15:22 Love without control 20:25 Security and intimacy 27:15 Open air and mortality 30:56 The threefold path 36:46 Nothing lands
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7547

item.title: Attention, Love, and Service
item.description:
How do attention, love, and service become one practice when the mind keeps sliding toward what is wrong? The talk moves from the mind’s negativity bias, through the feeling that goodness is already present, into a wider view of practice as both self-care and care for others. From there, it turns toward the heart of love as attention, then as letting go—of clinging, control, and even fixed views—so service can become a natural response to what is needed.
Chapters: 00:10 Goodness and Negativity 03:34 It's Already Here 07:43 Shared Suffering 13:15 Attention as Love 18:28 Seeing Human Life Clearly 26:22 From Insight to Service 31:25 Love Without Clinging 36:01 Mortality and Freedom 38:46 Closing Reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7488

item.title: Intensity, Clinging and Anatta in the teen years (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when the effort to hold on starts to feel like the real source of suffering? Opening with a Leonard Cohen lyric, the talk moves from grief and the tenderness it can open, into stories about adolescence, self-consciousness, and the pressure to perform, before arriving at a clear teaching: clinging hurts, and letting go can be cultivated. The speaker keeps returning to the difference between influence and control, and to the possibility of loving others and oneself without making identity into something to defend. Near the end, the talk settles into a quieter, more spacious view of practice as a way to open the heart without getting stuck in it.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Grief Softens 02:01 Trusting Young People 05:28 Being Heard 08:38 Intensity of Being Human 10:51 Performing a Self 15:54 Clinging and Letting Go 20:00 Love Without Possession 25:04 Freedom and Compassion 31:52 The Me Flag Relaxes
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/38545/

item.title: Rhythms and Refinements of Practice
item.description:
What if practice is less a straight line toward arrival than a series of recalibrations, surprises, and quiet reorientations? The talk opens with Leonard Cohen’s wish for a treaty between opposing forces, then moves through early uncertainty, the feeling that practice is larger than one’s plans, and the way progress can show up as subtle changes in perception, behavior, and lessened suffering. Along the way, the speaker uses a few outside references and personal stories to argue for a path that is not measured by dramatic experiences alone, but by growing mindfulness, love, non-harm, and the willingness to keep going without demanding certainty.
Chapters: 00:43 A wish for peace 02:13 First contact with practice 05:38 Early retreat and depth 08:01 Unknowns on the path 11:29 Subtle transformation 14:37 Recalibrating intention 18:31 Practice for others 24:59 Progress without arrival 28:16 A loose Dharma map
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7345

item.title: Spirit Rock-Attention, Love and Service
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple three-part aim: to love what is already here, transform ourselves, and serve others. From there, it moves through the way suffering dominates attention, the danger of turning practice into self-improvement, and the need to balance seeing clearly with not getting trapped in the self. Along the way, the speaker brings in stories from mindfulness teachers, clinical psychology, a museum performance built around sustained gazing, and poetry to show how attention can soften the heart into love, compassion, and service.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Practice and what it serves 02:20 Goodness already here 05:17 Beyond becoming 06:03 Service and mutuality 12:00 Attention as love 22:33 The gaze that reveals 27:11 From empathy to freedom 34:38 Purifying care 42:02 Start and continue
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/38168/

item.title: Spirit Rock-Samadhi in the Context of Letting Go
item.description:
What if samadhi is not something to force, but what appears when grasping relaxes and experience becomes less frictive? The talk moves from the common idea that awakening will solve our problems into a quieter argument: settling the mind, making peace with the human condition, and learning to let go are what allow collectedness to arise. Along the way, it turns to the hindrances, the pull of wanting to be elsewhere, and the subtle ways clinging makes life more complicated than it needs to be. External references to William James, Suzuki Roshi, Shinzen Young, Wittgenstein, and a story about Joseph Goldstein help shape the arc from theory toward direct practice.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Reversing the Question 03:01 Samadhi and Letting Go 07:23 Waiting for Nothing 11:57 Beyond Ordinary Mind 16:55 The Uses of Samadhi 24:01 Attachment and Confidence 30:13 Letting Go of Reference Points 37:34 Stillness Without Will 44:35 What Is Not Yours
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/38143/

item.title: Spirit Rock-Metta in the Post Obama Era
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple but urgent question: what does it mean to be safe for others in a time of political fear and harsh rhetoric? From there, the speaker turns inward to metta practice, linking self-love with non-harming and offering short phrases like “may I be safe,” “may I be happy,” and “may I be at peace.” The practice becomes less about repeating words and more about listening for how they land in the body, then widening that felt care outward to all beings. Along the way, the talk keeps returning to the way pain and joy, grief and hope, can soften the heart together.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and settling 03:42 Safe for others 08:56 Self-love and longing 13:42 Metta phrases 18:08 Listening to the body 22:44 Suffering and companionship 24:39 Widening to all beings
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/38115/

item.title: Insight of Not Self and Relationships
item.description:
How does the insight of not-self change once we bring it into the mess of relationship? The talk moves from the problem of wisdom not “generalizing” into daily life, to the way other people expose our mood, shame, clinging, and self-image more sharply than solitude does. Through examples from retreat practice, a story about the empty boat, and a long reading of the film Her, the speaker keeps returning to the same pressure point: how selfing leaks into love, conflict, and the wish to be safe for others.
Chapters: 00:00 Practice Beyond the Cushion 02:16 Wisdom That Generalizes 04:53 Relationship Reveals Habit 07:58 Silence and Selfing 13:24 Anatta in the Eyes 19:01 Shame and the Empty Boat 23:53 Becoming Safe for Others 31:47 Not-Self and Not-Other 34:54 Love, Possession, Letting Go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7192

item.title: Romance, Relationship, Sexuality & Dharma
item.description:
How do practice and relationship meet when the heart is no longer on retreat? The talk begins with the idea that life is not only practice but also performance: the place where our habits, cravings, tenderness, and defenses show up most clearly is in love, sexuality, and intimacy. From there it moves through impermanence, dependency, projection, monogamy, and conflict, asking how to love without clinging and how to let another person remain free.
Chapters: 00:00 Practice Meets Relationship 02:00 Relational Life as Mirror 07:28 Clinging and Belonging 11:44 Love’s Difficulty 15:37 Desire, Dependency, Risk 24:54 Eros and Freedom 36:37 Aggression and Not-Self 48:27 Commitment and Refuge
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7186

item.title: Welcome and Opening Remarks
item.description:
What if retreat begins not with effort, but with the sense that all of experience belongs? The talk moves from welcome and belonging to the practical conditions that make retreat possible: safety, silence, ethics, and the willingness to listen closely to one’s own mind and heart. It then opens into the refuge and precepts ceremony, framing practice as a shared reorientation toward truth rather than a personal project of achievement.
Chapters: 00:09 A Real Welcome 01:59 Everything Belongs 05:05 Listening With Care 08:47 Shared Sincerity 13:39 Surprised by Practice 16:41 Fresh Seeing 24:42 Refuge and Ethics 34:20 Reciting Refuge 41:17 Five Precepts and Rest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7182

item.title: JoAnna Hardy, Matthew Brensilver, Vinny Ferraro: Where You At, Where You Going
item.description:
Where do you enter practice from, and what part of the path is actually your edge? The talk moves through the Eightfold Path as a set of doorways — ethics, meditation, and wisdom — then keeps testing that framework against personal stories of homelessness, theft, self-aversion, and the relief that comes from precepts and honest reflection. It opens into a practical question for listeners: not just what feels natural, but what needs to be strengthened so the path becomes more balanced and alive.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: JoAnna Hardy, Vinny Ferraro
Chapters: 00:51 Eightfold Path Frame 02:40 Three Doorways 05:36 Ethics and Turning Around 11:43 Precepts As Mirror 18:33 Friendship And Directness 22:28 Taking Refuge In Samadhi 26:46 Dropping Into Experience 33:28 Find Your Edge 37:58 Dyads, Silence, And Closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37207/

item.title: Practice Note
item.description:
A small shift in attention becomes the whole teaching: a noise stops, and the mind suddenly notices the relief that had been hiding in plain sight. The talk uses that everyday moment to point toward a practice of seeing suffering as it fades, and of recognizing contentment, ease, and the absence of aversion when they are present. The emphasis is simple and quiet: notice endings, notice what is no longer there, and let those moments become part of insight.
Chapters: 00:09 Familiar Noise 01:19 Ease Goes Unnoticed 01:58 Noticing Contentment 02:44 The Fading Away 03:07 Knowing Absence 03:38 Simple Practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7001

item.title: Feedback & the Architecture of Self
item.description:
What happens when feedback stops being something to avoid and becomes a way of seeing? The talk begins with two practice maxims—don’t fight with yourself, and take feedback—and then follows that thread into cause and effect, self-clinging, and the ways criticism can reveal the hidden architecture of identity. By the end, the emphasis turns outward too: the less defensive we are, the more safety and refuge we can offer to others.
Chapters: 00:31 Two Maxims 01:35 Learning From Feedback 02:58 Delusion and Self-Image 05:07 Seeing Self-Clinging 07:10 Becoming Unoffendable 09:30 Tolerating Disorientation 10:51 Safer For Others
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7002
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
What happens when feedback stops being something to avoid and becomes a way of seeing? The talk begins with two practice maxims—don’t fight with yourself, and take feedback—and then follows that thread into cause and effect, self-clinging, and the ways criticism can reveal the hidden architecture of identity. By the end, the emphasis turns outward too: the less defensive we are, the more safety and refuge we can offer to others.
Chapters: 00:31 Two Maxims 01:35 Learning From Feedback 02:58 Delusion and Self-Image 05:07 Seeing Self-Clinging 07:10 Becoming Unoffendable 09:30 Tolerating Disorientation 10:51 Safer For Others
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7002

item.title: Practice Note: Waiting for Nothing
item.description:
The talk turns on a simple but unsettling question: what happens if meditation is not waiting for the next thing, but waiting for nothing? It describes how ordinary life keeps us leaning toward a future payoff, then asks the listener to sit and walk at the edge of the present without reaching ahead for redemption. The result is a brief, spare instruction in a more radical kind of mindfulness: surrendering to the moment without treating it as a means to somewhere else.
Chapters: 00:10 Small theme 00:29 Waiting for something 01:04 Waiting for nothing 01:22 Living without a future 01:47 Radical mindfulness 02:04 The invitation 02:30 Practice instruction
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6694

item.title: Dharmette: Reasons and causes
item.description:
What if the story we tell about why we feel a certain way is not the same thing as the real cause? The talk starts with cause and effect as the backbone of practice, then turns to a psychiatric study on depression that complicates the easy link between reasons and causes. From there, it becomes a quieter inquiry into how much confidence we should place in our own explanations, and how that uncertainty can open into humility and wonder. The talk ends by bringing that question back to practice itself: how, exactly, does practice become a cause of less suffering and more well-being?
Chapters: 00:00 Opening frame 00:42 Cause and effect in practice 02:14 Reasons versus causes 04:32 The depression study 05:42 What the study shows 09:44 Humility and wonder 11:38 Practice as inquiry 13:12 Closing thanks
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6695

item.title: Anxiety and Tranquility
item.description:
Restlessness and anxiety are treated here as the ordinary pressure of being human: the mind keeps leaning toward the future, adding stimulation, and calling that relief. The talk turns toward tranquility as a quieter possibility—something that can be tasted in the body, in the exhale, in the visual field, and even in the support of other people. Along the way, the speaker uses a Buddha story, Suzuki Roshi’s phrase “soft readiness,” and a retreat anecdote about suddenly realizing, “I’m calm,” to show how peace can be present before we notice it.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:31 Buddha in illness 02:50 Restlessness as human life 09:06 Waiting for nothing 15:56 Sangha and co-regulation 20:45 Finding steady anchors 26:26 Naming the meltdown 31:34 Letting go of becoming 35:57 Self becomes vague 40:17 Peace that does not fatigue
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/33651/

item.title: Grief, Love and Groundlessness
item.description:
When love is real, why does loss feel so inevitable, and what if grief is not a problem to solve but part of the cost of opening the heart? The talk moves through poems, Buddhist teachings on impermanence, and stories of heartbreak to ask whether practice is meant to erase sorrow or help us meet it without hardening. Rather than offering techniques, it keeps returning to the slow, improvisational work of letting grief be felt, and noticing how it can soften the heart instead of closing it.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Love and Loss 01:45 Poetry of Grief 03:49 Impermanence and Honesty 07:01 What the Buddha Grieves 09:25 Belonging and Attachment 13:52 Loss That Stays 18:10 Making Room for Heartbreak 27:30 Purifying the Grief 34:24 Questions, Practice, and Closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/33320/

item.title: Tranquility
item.description:
How do you practice when the human condition itself feels intense, restless, and hard to rest inside? The talk begins with the image of the sick Buddha asking for the seven factors of enlightenment and then follows tranquility as a cooling, balancing force against agitation, hyper-arousal, and the urge to keep manufacturing the next moment. Along the way, it moves through retreat life, gentle anchors like the breath and sound, and the way quiet can loosen the grip of self and future-oriented striving. The closing tone is practical and modest: peace is not dramatic, but it changes how life is held.
Chapters: 00:00 The Buddha at Night 01:46 Human Intensity 05:14 Restlessness and Leaning 08:30 Balancing Energy 12:31 Soft Readiness 19:27 Settling the System 25:02 Not Making a Self 31:50 Tranquility and Not-Self 40:09 Peace as the End
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6629

item.title: Energy
item.description:
What does it take to practice against the grain of habit without turning effort into strain? The talk moves from the Buddha’s teaching on virya, or energy, through the sweetness and cost of familiar habits, the limits of self-control, and the danger of over-efforting. It gradually turns toward a gentler question: what can be relaxed, what can be stopped, and how can effort become wise, steady, and open to the welfare of others?
Chapters: 00:30 What Energy Means 03:16 Against the Stream 06:01 Knowing Isn’t Doing 10:37 Self-Control and Fatigue 14:09 Effort Without Strain 20:01 Relaxed Perseverance 23:41 Why Practice 28:27 What Sustains Practice 36:55 For the Sake of Others
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6626

item.title: Mindfulness
item.description:
What if mindfulness is not a definition you start with, but a way of knowing that emerges through practice? The talk moves from that opening uncertainty into a careful unpacking of two threads: present-time awareness and equanimity, with images of a telescope, a black Labrador, a dance partner, and even free fall helping show the difference between merely having experience and actually knowing it as experience. Along the way, the speaker argues that mindfulness is less about mastering a technique than about seeing how suffering is generated, softening the heart, and learning to rest in the present without trying to control it.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 02:23 False promises of relief 03:57 Mindfulness in context 07:32 An umbrella term 10:06 Knowing that we know 14:07 Seeing causality clearly 17:42 Equanimity and softness 21:37 Practice as orientation 34:18 Hold it lightly
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6622

item.title: Thinking
item.description:
Thinking is first treated as something obvious and necessary, then as a kind of spell that quietly takes over. The talk moves from the usefulness of thought in daily life and Dharma reflection to the more unsettling discovery that we often mistake inner chatter for reality, self, and even safety. Along the way, it uses Douglas Harding, the Dhammapada, neuroscience, and the idea of a "center of narrative gravity" to show how practice can learn to know thought as thought without turning it into an enemy. It ends by countering that warning with a reminder not to rank experience or shame ordinary states.
Chapters: 00:00 Thinking and its place 03:06 Stopping thought 08:28 Mind shapes experience 13:34 What thought is made of 22:09 Self as narrative 26:23 Seeing self as phenomena 29:01 Settling without forcing 31:04 Not making a hierarchy
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6448

item.title: Insight as the Mechanism of Change
item.description:
What if insight is not a private breakthrough but the mechanism by which suffering actually loosens? The talk starts by comparing meditation to clinical research: first asking whether something works, then asking how it works, and uses that frame to explore insight as something that reduces dukkha rather than delivers a theory. From there it moves through three kinds of insight—personal, universal, and perceptual—ending with a shift in how self, world, and ordinary experience are seen when attention grows more refined.
Chapters: 00:00 Mechanism of change 02:41 What insight means 05:34 Repeated and embodied 10:01 Personal discovery 14:42 Everything is teaching 23:46 Suffering and mortality 32:30 Seeing differently 39:40 Less melodrama
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6350

item.title: Opening Talk - Welcome to Retreat
item.description:
How do you meet a retreat when the mind wants to turn every open moment into a plan, a project, or a way out? The talk begins with the unease of unstructured time and slowly turns toward a different stance: letting the form hold you, staying with continuity of attention, and being willing to be surprised by what appears. From there it broadens into renunciation, silence, the precepts, and the sense that retreat is not a solo self-improvement effort but a shared field of practice. The closing sitting then brings the teaching down to simple instructions: soften, relax, and receive the moment as it is.
Chapters: 00:00 Time and retreat 02:40 Not a self-improvement project 05:49 Curiosity and surprise 07:37 Wonder and renunciation 11:42 Renunciation at the center 14:12 Practice as refuge 25:32 Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha 32:41 Precepts and silence 41:33 Brief closing sitting
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6344

item.title: Going Home
item.description:
How do you leave a retreat without trying to hold onto it? The talk starts with reentry: afterglow, aftershock, a strange return to ordinary life, and the reminder that what has been touched here is not confined to the retreat setting. From there it moves into the practical question of staying connected—through practice, community, and patience—with a later turn to resources for continuing, the blessing cord, the three refuges, and a closing recitation of the precepts as something to carry back into daily life.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Returning Home 03:58 Afterglow and Aftershock 07:17 Staying Connected 11:34 Resources for Continuing 17:32 Volunteer Gratitude 41:09 The Blessing Cord 50:28 Three Refuges 54:29 Carrying It Forward 58:06 Final Goodbyes
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32079/

item.title: Q&A
item.description:
When should practice make room for things as they are, and when should it lead to change? The talk keeps returning to that question by separating acceptance of this moment from acceptance of one’s whole life, then showing how attention to direct bodily experience can interrupt the cycle of constant optimizing. From there it moves into practical questions about rerouting the mind, working with anger without suppressing it or acting it out, and finding a more honest way to relate to a bad mood or a difficult day.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the questions 02:10 Acceptance and change 04:50 What this moment means 10:03 Rerouting attention 14:06 Wholesome attention 18:14 Opposite of habit 23:57 Working with anger 29:34 Naming and relating 32:30 Bad moods and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32084/

item.title: The Intensity of Being a Teenager
item.description:
What if the real problem is not that being alive is too much, but that we keep trying to manage its intensity by adding more intensity? The talk moves from the pressure to live someone else’s life, into the felt overwhelm of being human, and then into mindfulness as a way of meeting experience without flinching or trying to fix it. From there it turns toward love and sexuality, where desire, clinging, vulnerability, and non-harming become central questions rather than side issues.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and orientation 02:40 Missing our own life 06:51 Listening inward 11:54 The intensity of being human 19:22 Digesting experience 22:44 Love and vulnerability 26:54 Eros and metta 30:57 Clinging and harm 34:55 Freedom, uncertainty, aliveness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32081/

item.title: Practice Note: Vision
item.description:
How often do we treat the visual field as if it were just the solid world, rather than an experience that arises and passes? This short practice note points to seeing itself as a mindfulness bell: the breath, body, and emotions may already be familiar reminders, but vision can quietly pull the mind into association and proliferation. The invitation is simple and practical—while walking or going about the morning, notice the visual field as seeing, then let it fall away and begin again.
Chapters: 00:10 Dharma reminders 00:50 The overlooked sense 01:14 Seeing as practice 01:36 Associations in the visual field 02:30 Walking with open eyes 03:04 Seeing arise and pass
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6222

item.title: Dharmette: Practicing for oneself is complicated.
item.description:
Practicing for one’s own benefit, the talk suggests, can quickly turn into a tense self-improvement project. By contrast, when practice is oriented toward the welfare of all beings, it can feel simpler, less self-conscious, and less trapped in the question of how one is doing. The speaker uses Shantideva’s bodhisattva ideal—and a brief reference to Joseph Goldstein’s emphasis on compassion—to show how a vast intention can loosen the tightness of self-focus and make the heart more useful.
Chapters: 00:41 Complicated Self-Benefit 01:44 The Bodhisattva Turn 03:14 Compassion as Mission 03:56 Shantideva's Vast Vow 05:16 The Weight of Grandness 06:26 Beyond Self-Improvement 08:09 A More Useful Heart 09:41 Enough, Then Others
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6223

item.title: Learning from Retreats
item.description:
What do we think retreat is for: escaping life, or seeing it more clearly? The talk moves from a careful defense of silent retreat into a deeper claim that intensive practice reveals the intensity of being human, the limits of clinging, and the surprise of insight when the mind can no longer keep fidgeting away from discomfort. Along the way, it uses images of national parks, Merton on rest, and a few brief quotations to show how silence can uncover both the heart’s fragility and its unexpected capacity for joy, ease, and letting go.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Why Retreat Matters 03:05 Silence and Surprise 08:57 The Courage to Rest 11:55 Insight Changes the Story 18:21 Mind and Happiness 25:26 Intensity and Heart 31:10 Attention as Love 35:43 Lightness and Letting Go 40:20 Closing Good Wishes
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29011/

item.title: Thinking
item.description:
Thinking is treated here as both the engine of practice and the source of confusion. The talk starts with Descartes and the Buddha, then moves into a close look at how thought appears as inner talk and mental images, how it hooks onto selfing, and why meditation is less about banning thought than about knowing it as thought. Along the way, the speaker brings in neuroscience, mind-wandering research, and examples from meditation teaching to argue for a steadier, less judgmental relationship to the mind's narration.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 What thinking is 03:26 Silence and value 08:42 Meditation reveals the mind 16:16 Knowing thought directly 31:58 Mind wandering by default 39:07 Self, story, and urgency 49:56 Unlearning aversion 54:28 Loving relationship
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/28021/

item.title: Sincerity
item.description:
Sincerity is treated here as the lifeblood of practice, not as earnest effort but as a willingness to be surprised. The talk moves from asking what sincerity means to pointing toward curiosity, experimentation, and the capacity to see experience in a new way. Insight, the speaker suggests, often arrives as something unexpected or even startling, and it is nourished by continuous mindfulness paired with openness.
Chapters: 00:20 Sincerity in practice 00:50 Willingness to be surprised 01:18 Learning to investigate 01:49 Insight starts surprising 02:08 Continuity and curiosity 02:26 Seeing in a new way
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5768

item.title: Limits of Technique
item.description:
What happens when meditation becomes too identified with technique? The talk follows that tension from the value of having reliable practices to the limits of turning Dharma into a closed system, arguing that real practice also requires improvisation, curiosity, and an “appropriate response” to the moment. Along the way, the speaker brings in a Zen teaching story, a line from Nietzsche about mistrusting systematizers, and Sayadaw Tejaniya’s reminder that wisdom must be invested to grow wisdom.
Chapters: 00:29 Why teach at all 01:30 The appeal of systems 02:46 Technique has value 03:59 Improvise with the moment 05:20 No fixed template 06:54 Curiosity and surprise 09:08 Technique and view 11:14 What technique misses
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5769

item.title: Not-Self
item.description:
What happens when the urge to improve the self becomes another way of holding onto it? The talk moves from self-love and confidence, through the habit of evaluating and protecting a personal identity, into a careful case for not-self as a practice of letting go rather than a new philosophy to believe. Along the way, it uses psychology, sutta passages, and a few personal stories to show how the sense of “me” is made, how it tightens, and how it can soften into something less defended and more open.
Chapters: 00:00 Love and selflessness 01:58 Evaluating the self 04:51 Ego and insecurity 07:31 Not a new view 12:43 Image and possession 19:36 Narrative gravity 24:30 Grasping makes self 30:41 Quiet and anicca 37:49 Centerless peace
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5730

item.title: Cultivating Confidence in Practice
item.description:
How can confidence in practice deepen not by fighting self, but by relating to it more kindly? The talk moves from the complications of emptiness and not-self into the practical work of rewriting the self-story, easing self-harshness, and finding that a loved self is easier to relinquish than a hated one. Along the way it connects insight to ethics, community, and the steadying effect of trusting the path even when the whole view is not yet clear.
Chapters: 00:00 Selflessness and its limits 02:05 A functional relationship 05:15 Back to the present 08:30 Confidence and release 12:51 Care that protects others 16:47 Ego without battle 19:15 Retelling the self 24:51 Belonging and trust 30:35 Equanimity and enough
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5727

item.title: Psychotherapy and Dharma
item.description:
What happens when psychotherapy and Dharma are asked to answer the same question: how do we reduce suffering without shrinking the whole human life? The talk keeps returning to the tension between insight and psychological growth, arguing that awakening and maturity are best understood as mutually supportive rather than competing goals. From there it moves through mindfulness in clinical settings, exposure and avoidance, ethics, samadhi, and the question of whether practice can be taught without a view. The discussion opens into questions of boundaries, relationships, and charisma, with a steady emphasis on what actually leads to more freedom and less suffering.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 01:25 Insight and maturity 04:30 Mindfulness enters therapy 12:11 Thoughts and suffering 17:19 Exposure as practice 21:21 Different aims of each path 29:36 Secular Dharma questions 40:03 Questions and lived practice 55:05 Compassionate closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26810/

item.title: Practice Questions
item.description:
How do you work with sleepiness, sexual desire, and anger without turning practice into a battle? The talk moves through a series of practice questions: how to meet torpor with small adjustments, why precepts on retreat create safety and reveal craving, and how to tell when a practice is steadying and when it is masking avoidance. It ends by looking beneath anger to the mood that surrounds it, with a vivid image of fish not noticing the water they swim in.
Chapters: 00:14 Opening and context 02:02 Working with sleepiness 04:19 Fatigue and retreat 06:14 Why precepts matter 13:06 Choosing a practice 16:03 Anger and mood 20:16 Soothing the system
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5533

item.title: Love, Relationship, Sexuality and Dharma Part 02
item.description:
How do love and sexuality become practice without being reduced to possession, projection, or fantasy? The talk moves through a series of careful pressures: the union of eros and metta, the limits of expecting another person to end our seeking, and the humbling work of meeting the other as truly separate and free. It then turns to relationship as shared risk—shared nightmares, shared uncertainty, shared aggression—and asks whether the heart can stay open without demanding certainty or permanence. Along the way, images from Lacan, Kittisaro, Rilke, and others sharpen the question of how intimacy can include distance, not-self, and tenderness at once.
Chapters: 00:09 Love and Eros 04:27 Projection and Seeking 07:00 Seeing the Other 11:17 Relationship as Practice 14:38 Risk and Desire 18:20 Not Knowing Together 21:23 Freedom and Not-Self 25:56 Monogamy as Adventure 29:17 Closing Reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5534

item.title: What would it be like without narrative?
item.description:
What would experience feel like without the usual inner commentary? The talk starts with that question and follows it into a quiet inquiry about how story, self-concern, and even the wish to understand can shape and fatigue experience. Rather than rejecting story outright, it points toward a brief, simple dropping of the need to know who we are, what is happening, or what comes next.
Chapters: 00:24 Opening Question 01:00 Story and Fatigue 02:10 Dropping Below Story 03:13 Even Insight Can Grasp 04:11 Relinquishing Orientation 04:52 Simple Exploration
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5531

item.title: "History is Ending Today"
item.description:
How does impermanence move from heartbreak to refuge? The talk begins with the Buddha’s three marks of existence and lingers on anicca as something deeper than things eventually ending: conditioned phenomena are already breaking up as they arise, and the sense of a stable self can obscure that fact. A study of how people misjudge their own future change, along with a brief story about a child and Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles, makes the teaching feel immediate before the talk turns toward the openness that comes when nothing is fixed.
Chapters: 00:09 Three Marks of Existence 01:36 Impermanence Deeper Than Ending 02:53 The Self As Stable 05:28 Research On Blind Spots 06:57 The Heartbreak Of Anicca 08:05 Openness As Refuge 09:20 Born And Dying
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5532

item.title: Practice Questions
item.description:
A Rumi line about “living in the nowhere you come from” opens into a practical discussion of emptiness, mystical experience, and why some teachings land as felt resonance rather than doctrine. From there, the talk moves into the ordinary pressure points of practice: staying mindful while driving, noticing how quickly attention slips, and finding a way to work with frustration without pretending the mind is easier than it is. It then widens to the question of whether Dharma can still take root in daily life, and finally to a brief reflection on artificial intelligence, longevity, and what these technologies might reveal about self and desire.
Chapters: 01:21 Rumi and emptiness 07:13 What the line points to 07:56 Mindfulness while driving 13:39 Practice and frustration 14:21 Daily life and Dharma 20:07 AI, longevity, self
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5526

item.title: Love, Relationship, Sexuality, and Dharma
item.description:
How do love, relationship, and sexuality become part of Dharma practice without being reduced to rules or ideals? The talk begins with a vivid memory of a dance party at a Buddhist gathering, then turns toward the tension between embodied life, longing, clinging, and the wish to live and love with more awareness. Along the way, it draws on Adam Phillips, Rilke, Jack Kornfield, Freud, and Nietzsche to explore how relationship can reveal both our deepest conditioning and our capacity to grow.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Memory 04:28 Love and Dharma 08:30 Listening Gently 12:02 Why Love Is Hard 15:34 Cultural Assumptions 18:18 Desire and Renunciation 24:10 The Past in Love 27:05 Relationship as Practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5527

item.title: Enlightenment, Truth and the Fetishization of Mystical Experience
item.description:
Is awakening best understood as a ticket out of suffering, or as a doorway into special knowledge? The talk moves from a practical exchange about meditation posture and everyday clinging into a sustained argument that mystical experience is real and important, but easily overclaimed, over-explained, and turned into a kind of spiritual possession. Using Shinzen Young’s three human jobs, William James on non-ordinary consciousness, and a Rumi line about the “nowhere” one comes from, the speaker keeps returning to the same caution: don’t confuse a powerful experience with a metaphysical system, and don’t lose sight of ethics, service, and the ordinary reduction of suffering.
Chapters: 00:15 Checking practice 04:43 Wholesome engagement 09:51 Clinging and views 12:01 Enlightenment and allure 18:13 James and non-ordinary mind 24:40 Poetry and description 31:10 Experience into interpretation 37:34 Freedom without inflation 44:59 Closing wishes
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5490

item.title: Self-Love and Selflessness - Part 2
item.description:
What happens when self-love is not a better story about me, but a loosening of the need to keep evaluating and defending the self at all? The talk moves from the felt contrast between self-harshness and warmth, through a reading of the Buddha’s teaching on not-self, into a close look at how grasping manufactures the sense of a center. From there it opens into more experiential territory: the “center of narrative gravity,” the image of having no head and gaining a world, and the way insight into selflessness can also break the heart open into compassion.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Self-Love and Selflessness 05:52 Worth and Success 11:11 The Buddha on Not-Self 18:39 Grasping Makes the Self 22:42 Seeing Through the Ground 31:52 No Head, Gained World 40:54 Face, Heart, and Compassion 46:18 Practice Becomes Life 48:18 Closing Meditation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26520/

item.title: Facets of Mindfulness
item.description:
Mindfulness is often treated as a single skill, but the talk begins by separating present-time awareness from equanimity and asking which part actually supports well-being. Drawing on a study of nearly a thousand people, the speaker shows that being highly observant without non-judgment can coincide with more depression, worry, and instability, and uses that finding to argue for the central role of equanimity. From there the talk turns practical: why staying with discomfort is a deep form of renunciation, how retreat ripens that capacity, and how meeting pain with steadiness can soften the heart rather than harden it.
Chapters: 00:30 Mindfulness and research 01:51 Two facets of mindfulness 02:49 The cluster study 04:59 Awareness without equanimity 06:51 Why equanimity matters 08:43 Ripening through retreat 10:39 Renunciation and courage 12:54 From pain to love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5466

item.title: Self-Love and Selflessness - Part 1
item.description:
Is self something to overcome, or is self-kindness part of the path into selflessness? The talk starts by challenging the usual opposition between insight and goodness, then argues that self-love and self-clinging are not the same thing. From there it moves through the suttas, the silence around Vacchagotta’s question, and some modern psychological framing to show how a softer relationship to self can reduce suffering and make room for service.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Insight and service 02:52 No conflict 05:29 From harshness to kindness 09:01 Experience, not theory 14:48 Humbling the ego 19:29 Healing the fragments 27:55 Love, security, and illusion 36:16 Awareness and not-self 47:57 Questions and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26519/

item.title: Feedback In The Context of Dharma Practice
item.description:
What does it mean to take feedback without turning it into self-defense? The talk begins with a simple Dharma frame—don't fight with yourself, and take feedback—then opens that idea outward until almost everything in life becomes material for learning. From there it moves through certainty, wrongness, greed, hatred, and delusion, and then into the harder territory of praise, criticism, defensiveness, intimacy, and the fragile work of staying open when the ground shifts under self-image. It closes by tying that openness to the safety of Dharma communities, where feedback is not just personal but communal care.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Two guiding principles 02:01 Everything is teaching 03:53 The lure of being right 07:28 Greed, hate, delusion 12:58 Seeing self-view clearly 19:24 Praise and criticism 25:16 Tolerating disorientation 28:39 Giving feedback well 34:52 Feedback and Sangha safety
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26154/

item.title: Things Nobody told Me When I was a Teen
item.description:
What if the hardest thing about being a teenager is not finding your place, but realizing that nobody has a finished answer for what it means to be human? The talk moves from the pressure of adult advice and teenage self-protection into a quieter invitation to meet intensity honestly, without hiding behind "shoulds" or a fixed identity. Through images like "planting flags" in the self and a closing quote from Norman Fischer, it points toward a practice in which attention loosens defensiveness and life itself becomes the path.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the conversation 02:58 Finding your own path 06:24 Mindfulness as honesty 09:59 Being human is intense 15:18 Digesting experience 23:00 Who am I, really? 28:50 Planting flags 34:10 Love, suffering, and the next generation 40:24 Practice becomes life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/25990/

item.title: Mortality and the Poignancy of Life
item.description:
How do we live when the world is slipping through our fingers? The talk begins with mortality as a practical question, moving from a personal moment in a market to Buddhist reflections on mindfulness of death, denial, and the ways fear of dying can quietly organize the rest of life. It then widens through psychology, post-9/11 kindness, hospice work, and poetry toward a quieter claim: remembering impermanence can clarify what matters, soften the heart, and make life feel more complete.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the topic 03:28 Mindfulness of death 05:52 Year to live practice 11:07 Death, dignity, resistance 16:30 Mortality salience 21:58 Kindness and groundlessness 25:50 Hospice and priorities 30:11 Self and surrender 34:51 Closing poem
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/25377/

It then widens through psychology, post-9/11 kindness, hospice work, and poetry toward a quieter claim: remembering impermanence can clarify what matters, soften the heart, and make life feel more complete.

Chapters: 00:00 Opening the topic 03:28 Mindfulness of death 05:52 Year to live practice 11:07 Death, dignity, resistance 16:30 Mortality salience 21:58 Kindness and groundlessness 25:50 Hospice and priorities 30:11 Self and surrender 34:51 Closing poem
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/25377/

item.title: Practice Questions
item.description:
What do you do when meditation exposes the leftover anxiety and confusion of a full day? The talk begins with a direct, practical exchange about restlessness at the end of a sit, then widens into the idea that practice is not about fixing the self so much as learning to digest experience more gently. From there it turns to confusion and not knowing, with a steady emphasis on not forcing answers, not measuring progress breath by breath, and letting clarity emerge without agitation.
Chapters: 00:09 Opening the practice questions 02:54 Anxiety as residue 06:21 Working with anxious energy 10:38 Choosing another object 12:59 What confusion feels like 15:56 Equanimity with not knowing 18:58 Not every breath
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5185

item.title: Metta: Cultivation, Concentration and Purification
item.description:
What if metta is not mainly something to feel, but something that trains attention, purifies the heart, and slowly changes where the mind defaults? The talk moves from balancing wisdom and love to a threefold view of metta as cultivation, concentration, and purification, including the dry stretches where nothing tender appears and the practice still works. It then widens into an account of being seen—through an art performance, the human face, and a quoted teaching on not harming—so that kindness becomes tied to recognizing our own and others’ vulnerability and goodness.
Chapters: 00:31 Wisdom and heart 02:19 Why metta matters 05:33 Defaulting to kindness 09:01 Three functions 11:46 Purification and tears 15:40 Ringing the bell 18:32 Presence and being seen 22:30 The face calls us 25:34 Self-regard and goodness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5186

item.title: Balance on the Path: Awakening and Cultivation
item.description:
What happens when practice is pulled toward sudden awakening on one side and gradual cultivation on the other? The talk moves through that tension carefully, showing how an exclusive focus on enlightenment can turn into spiritual materialism, while a purely gradual approach can miss the radical possibilities of the path. Along the way, the speaker uses Gil Fronsdal’s “from A to A” framing, quotes Trungpa on ego, Leonard Cohen on emptiness not making a burden light, and Nisargadatta on freedom as a way of rethinking what practice actually changes. In the end, the two sides of the path are said to converge in harmlessness, service, and love.
Chapters: 00:14 Practice Leaves Nothing Out 02:31 Two Sides of Change 04:53 The Middle Path 10:34 Spiritual Materialism 16:41 What Insight Does Not Buy 21:38 Emptiness And Longing 27:21 Freedom As Experience 29:28 Harmlessness And Service
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5113

item.title: Practice Note
item.description:
What happens when life stops feeling like a chain of projects? The talk looks at the stress that comes from turning tasks, meditation, walking, even going to the bathroom or making tea into something to finish, and invites a gentler experiment: do what needs doing without fixing each moment into a goal. The emphasis is on relaxing that project-making mind and staying open, moment by moment, to what is here and what is skillful.
Chapters: 00:18 Life as a Project 01:09 Not Doing Nothing 02:13 Meditation as Task 02:51 Relaxing the Schedule 03:51 Ordinary Actions 04:38 Open to What Is Skillful
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5110

item.title: Dharmette
item.description:
What is mindfulness, really: a loose umbrella word, or a set of capacities that can actually be trained? The talk begins by noticing how mindfulness is used in modern Buddhist and secular settings, then turns to a working definition drawn from Shinzen Young: concentration, clarity, and equanimity. From there it moves through each quality in turn, showing how gathered attention, higher-resolution seeing, and not pushing or pulling on experience can change what practice feels like from the inside. The talk closes with a simple self-check: look at where these three qualities are strong or weak and use that to orient practice more skillfully.
Chapters: 00:00 What mindfulness means 02:20 Mindfulness in the mainstream 04:58 A threefold model 08:31 Gathered attention 09:37 Seeing clearly 12:16 Not pushing or pulling 14:21 Checking practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5111

item.title: Mudita
item.description:
What happens when the heart stops treating another person’s good fortune as a threat? The talk moves from the ordinary claim that mindfulness gives us more choice into a close look at mudita, sympathetic joy, as a habit the mind can actually learn over time. Through a Maurice Sendak story, a passage from Sam Harris, and reflections on envy and jealousy, the speaker shows how comparison narrows the heart and how letting go of self-protection makes room for delight in others’ well-being. The talk ends with a quiet return to practice, suggesting that joy becomes steadier as the sense of territory loosens.
Chapters: 00:00 Practice and Momentum 05:01 What Mudita Means 06:24 Sendak’s Eaten Card 09:59 Love Broadens Out 13:36 Joy Can Be Trained 18:36 Envy and Comparison 21:43 Evolution and Letting Go 24:12 Self-View as Obstacle 32:00 Less Territorial, More Free
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/5101

item.title: Some Fruits of Practice
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple question: what is a Dharma talk for? From there it moves through three functions — instruction, gladdening the mind, and support — and uses that frame to explore how practice gradually softens doubt, aligns conduct with intention, and makes life feel less fragmented. The later sections turn toward the fruits of practice: less self-clinging, more openness, a quieter heart, and a growing sense that everything in life can belong on the path.
Chapters: 00:12 Three functions of talk 03:15 Why fruits matter 06:05 Ethics becomes natural 10:04 Wholeness and trust 12:27 Path becomes personal 15:13 Wanting less 18:50 Self-clinging loosens 22:09 The vase and the heart 25:49 Wisdom and love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4906

item.title: Practice Questions
item.description:
How do you practice when desire is pulling in different directions—when goals matter, but the mind keeps turning them into a promise of happiness? The talk moves through a series of questions about wholesome desire and craving, why success can still leave us unsettled, and how taking care of this moment is a more reliable way of caring for the future. It then turns practical: how to keep practice alive in a busy life, how much solitude or contemplation is enough, and whether joy has a place in the path before the hard work feels complete.
Chapters: 00:09 Opening Questions 01:28 Goals and Craving 03:47 Success and Letdown 05:11 Take Care of This Moment 06:52 Busy Life Practice 11:40 Solitude and Contemplation 14:42 Dharma in Daily Life 16:39 Practice and Joy
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4905

item.title: Knowing and Not Knowing
item.description:
How does practice deepen both knowing and not knowing? The talk moves from mindfulness as a way of seeing experience more clearly—breath, body, pleasure, pain, habits, and the ordinary facts of being human—into a quieter argument that fixed views can block insight and intimacy. Along the way, it uses Rilke, Joseph Goldstein, a Zen tea story, and reflections on science and relationships to show why openness, wonder, and the willingness to be wrong matter on the path.
Chapters: 00:12 Knowing the path 01:33 Seeing through experience 03:27 Bare knowledge 06:41 Self-knowledge and habit 11:28 What helps well-being 14:09 False refuges 17:00 The value of not knowing 21:41 Views and practice 28:54 Relationships and learning
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4686

item.title: In The Gap
item.description:
The talk turns to the quiet moments between formal practice: the walk to the bathroom, the shift out of sitting, the places where habit quietly takes over. Rather than pushing for constant vigilance, it asks what the minimum useful effort of mindfulness might be, and suggests that the urge for unconsciousness often comes from trying too hard. With just a little less strain, what feels spacey can sometimes open into real spaciousness.
Chapters: 00:12 Noticing the gaps 00:35 Ordinary transitions 01:03 Habit hides there 01:26 The cost of strain 02:05 Less effort needed 02:34 From spaciness to space
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4652

item.title: Failing Well
item.description:
What if failure is not a detour from the path, but one of the ways the path works? The talk begins with perfectionism as a familiar form of self-harshness and constant checking, then turns toward a different stance: start, continue, and let the practice include not getting it right. A story about a mistaken gift and the response, “It’s such a good mistake,” opens into a gentler view of learning, where metta and repeated beginning again soften the heart.
Chapters: 00:20 Perfectionism and the path 01:18 How it shows up 02:45 Striving and comparison 05:21 Connection instead of separation 06:14 Failing as practice 09:10 The good mistake 12:23 Metta and innocence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4653

item.title: Secrets, Shame and Transparency
item.description:
What happens when secrecy is treated not just as a private problem, but as a place where the practice has not yet reached? The talk begins with a contrast between depth and breadth in practice: the mind can settle more deeply, but the Dharma can also spread into the hidden corners of life until less and less feels protected from awareness. From there, it turns to shame, honesty, and the stress of keeping secrets, using examples from Foucault, Sam Harris, and the Buddha’s teaching on conscience and concern for consequences to ask how transparency becomes a form of safety for oneself and others. A final exchange with the audience brings the question into focus by teasing apart authenticity and privacy, and asking what it means to live with less to hide without losing good boundaries.
Chapters: 00:12 Depth and breadth 02:56 Becoming transparent 05:09 Turning to secrets 07:57 Privacy and shame 12:53 Honesty as a mirror 16:26 Suppressing the hidden 23:06 Wholesome shame 28:12 Kindness and vulnerability 35:25 Questions on authenticity
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4647

item.title: Practice Note
item.description:
The talk begins with a practical question: how should concentration and mindfulness be understood in relation to each other? Rather than treating them as separate, the speaker reframes them as deeply intertwined, arguing that mindfulness without concentration can become scattered, while concentration gives mindfulness its gathered, rich quality. The discussion moves from a sutta-based reference to a more technical definition that folds concentration into mindfulness itself, then circles back to the listener’s experience of “ping-pong” attention and how practice can become more saturated and intimate with each object.
Chapters: 00:13 Opening Question 01:00 Mindfulness or Concentration 01:53 A Continuum Model 03:00 Gathered Attention 04:25 Definitions And Insight 05:48 Concentration And Wisdom 06:53 Saturated Awareness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4631

item.title: Reflection on Mortality: The Poignancy of Being Alive
item.description:
A cemetery by the speaker’s workplace becomes a starting point for asking what it means to face mortality without flinching. The talk moves from personal reflections and the limits of “year to live” exercises into Buddhist teachings on impermanence, not-self, and the possibility that seeing death clearly can soften the heart rather than harden it. Along the way, the speaker weaves in Freud, Larry Rosenberg, Pema Chödrön, Mary Oliver, and a closing poem by Nyogen Senzaki to trace a path from denial toward a quieter, more open way of living.
Chapters: 00:13 A Cemetery Reminder 01:49 Mindfulness of Death 04:49 Practicing the Last Year 09:01 Denial and Humility 14:08 Walking With Death 18:07 Mortality Salience 21:39 Kindness Under Groundlessness 25:22 Safety in Change 31:14 The Phantom Self
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4626

item.title: Practice Questions
item.description:
How do you keep practice steady when life is crowded, and how do you tell the difference between true meditation and the mind simply spinning? The talk moves from a practical question about fitting in practice with two young children to a set of clear suggestions: make a time and place, clarify the intention, and let meditation become as ordinary and non-negotiable as brushing your teeth. It then turns to the structure of practice itself, distinguishing breath- or body-based focus, open awareness, and times when thought is intentionally taken as the object of reflection rather than a distraction.
Chapters: 00:09 Opening the question 00:32 Two parts of the concern 02:35 Make practice automatic 04:15 Intention and mortality 05:36 Clear instructions matter 07:13 Noting and distraction 08:59 Thought as object 10:06 Listener’s response
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4615

item.title: Anxiety
item.description:
Anxiety here is treated less as a private flaw than as a mix of useful alarm, restless thinking, and an uneasy hope that worrying will somehow keep life under control. The talk moves from fear on the edge of a cliff, to the joke of a 3 a.m. new worry, to psychological research on how worry stays trapped in future scenarios and overestimates what it can predict. From there, it turns toward practice: loosening the grip on control, working with uncertainty, untangling thought from body sensations, and learning that safety comes more from relinquishing than from finding the perfect path.
Chapters: 00:09 What Anxiety Feels Like 04:34 Fear and Alarm 10:12 Worry as a Habit 12:23 Future-Making Mind 17:00 Forks in the Road 23:00 Groundlessness and Practice 28:16 Working with Anxiety 35:06 Honesty and Relinquishment 39:54 Closing Dedication
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4616

item.title: Wholesome Doubt and Faith
item.description:
What if doubt is not the obstacle, but the beginning of an honest practice? The talk traces a middle path between paralyzing skepticism and blind belief, using the Kalama Sutta, direct experience, and the difference between “belief” and “faith” to show how confidence can grow without closing down questioning. It then moves into the lived texture of practice: early unease, bright faith, steadier commitment, and the willingness to keep looking rather than settle for certainties. A later question-and-answer section turns to the historicity of the Buddha and the strain of right speech, bringing the teaching back to what can actually be tested in daily life.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the Question 04:09 The Kalama Sutta 07:18 Come See For Yourself 09:59 Practice as Inquiry 17:26 Doubt and First Steps 27:45 Bright Faith 33:33 Letting Faith Mature 40:52 Questions and Right Speech 46:49 Mudita and Closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4605

item.title: How We Relate To Each Other
item.description:
How do the insights of practice actually show up in the way we speak, listen, and meet one another? The talk begins with the holiday season and quickly turns to relationship as the place where meditation is tested, especially through wise speech, careful listening, and the gradual noticing of when speech is out of alignment with deeper intention. It then moves toward a quieter challenge: loosening rigid self-definition so we become less defensive, less easily offended, and more able to receive others fully. A final image of the empty boat brings the teaching into focus, suggesting that when self-clinging thins, reactivity has less to hold onto.
Chapters: 00:14 Holiday Relationship Theme 00:35 Relational Beings 02:00 Practice in Relationship 03:10 Wise Speech 04:26 Trust and Feedback 06:50 Deep Listening 09:04 Letting Go of Ownership 10:35 The Heavy Vase 13:35 The Empty Boat
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4496
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
How do the insights of practice actually show up in the way we speak, listen, and meet one another? The talk begins with the holiday season and quickly turns to relationship as the place where meditation is tested, especially through wise speech, careful listening, and the gradual noticing of when speech is out of alignment with deeper intention. It then moves toward a quieter challenge: loosening rigid self-definition so we become less defensive, less easily offended, and more able to receive others fully. A final image of the empty boat brings the teaching into focus, suggesting that when self-clinging thins, reactivity has less to hold onto.
Chapters: 00:14 Holiday Relationship Theme 00:35 Relational Beings 02:00 Practice in Relationship 03:10 Wise Speech 04:26 Trust and Feedback 06:50 Deep Listening 09:04 Letting Go of Ownership 10:35 The Heavy Vase 13:35 The Empty Boat
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4496

item.title: Clinging & Letting Go
item.description:
What if clinging is not just a problem to defeat, but a habit worth understanding for how much it seems to work? The talk moves from a simple claim about freedom as non-clinging, through poetry, personal examples, and Dharma teachings, into a careful look at the places clinging takes hold: sense pleasure, views, and self-view. It ends by turning toward the body, feeling, and the difficult work of staying open when letting go does not come easily.
Chapters: 00:10 Happiness and clinging 02:58 Freedom as non-clinging 06:00 Respect the habit 08:06 Ajahn Chah's simplicity 12:33 Craving and desire 16:00 Four fields of clinging 23:59 Wrongness and self-view 29:18 Feeling and release 35:44 Compassion and surrender
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4405

item.title: Working with Anger: Part 3
item.description:
How do you work with anger without turning it into self-blame, blame of others, or a rush to premature forgiveness? The talk moves from seeing anger as something conditioned and burdensome, to a careful definition of forgiveness that is not condoning, suppressing, or necessarily reconciling. It then turns toward a deeper practice: looking at how a fixed sense of self and a fixed image of others keep anger in place, and how clearer seeing can soften resentment into goodwill. Questions at the end bring the teaching into practical territory, including self-forgiveness, guilt versus remorse, and anger in response to injustice.
Chapters: 00:21 Reviewing the ground 03:10 Forgiveness and clear seeing 06:28 What forgiveness is not 12:39 Concepts feed anger 16:36 Self-clinging and threat 23:01 Not self, not other 26:37 Suffering and tenderness 33:39 A gift loosens resentment 37:27 Questions, remorse, and closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4046

item.title: Ripples of Happiness
item.description:
How do we practice when the wish for our own well-being starts to feel too small? The talk moves from that ordinary starting point into a steadier intention to practice for others, with anger, generosity, and compassion as examples of how the work does not stay private. A social-network study on how happiness spreads through friends of friends then gives the teaching a concrete shape: whatever softens or frees the heart does not remain contained, but ripples outward.
Chapters: 00:13 Opening the talk 00:31 Intention in practice 01:29 Self and others 02:40 A happiness study 03:52 Three degrees out 05:24 What the study suggests 06:07 Dedicating the effort
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4045

item.title: Working with Anger: Part 2
item.description:
How do you work with anger without turning it into self-blame, repression, or an endless story about other people? The talk moves from radical accountability to the claim that anger is sustained by repetition, then into a set of practices: noticing love, watching body sensations and inner talk, and investigating rather than blaming or despairing. It also brings in Ajahn Chah, Thich Nhat Hanh, Sharon Salzberg, Shantideva, and a few concrete ways of speaking honestly when anger has to be addressed with another person.
Chapters: 00:12 Framing the work 04:07 Ajahn Chah's warning 07:28 Hope and path 12:13 Investigate instead 16:51 Care for anger 19:45 Mindfulness and vulnerability 23:27 Speaking from the heart 32:22 Body and urgency 39:24 Questions and closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4041

item.title: The Non-Paradox of Self and Not Self
item.description:
If self is not something to defend, what is actually being protected? The talk begins by setting self-love and self-forgetting side by side, then moves through the worry that they must be in conflict, before arguing that kindness, cultivation, and insight into not-self can support one another. It stays practical rather than philosophical, using the Buddha’s silence on speculative questions, a psychologist’s caution about ego language, and a series of images about retreat, memory, and the mind’s “home base” to show how practice loosens identification and makes inner life feel safer and less burdened.
Chapters: 00:54 Two Path Tasks 02:41 Self-Clinging and Kindness 07:05 Beyond Paradox 10:31 Buddha and Pragmatism 13:31 Ego and Development 16:51 Mindfulness as Healing 22:58 Suffering in the Self 27:47 Rumi and Sumedho 30:46 Loving It to Death
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4035

item.title: Working with Anger: Part 1
item.description:
What if anger is both natural and deeply misleading: a force that can sometimes signal injustice, yet so easily hardens into a story that feels absolutely true? The talk moves from listeners’ questions about outbursts, suppression, self-anger, and being around other people’s anger into a careful distinction between what happens and how we respond, and into the question of how to meet anger without rehearsing it. Drawing on Thich Nhat Hanh, Ajahn Chah, the Dalai Lama, Shantideva, and a Buddha image of anger’s “honeyed tip and poisoned root,” the speaker keeps returning to one practical point: notice the tangle, see the seduction, and take good care of the heat before it takes over.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening questions 01:43 Not rehearsing anger 03:39 Impact and response 09:00 Responsibility without self-blame 14:36 Not an angry person 18:30 Natural, but costly 27:28 Shantideva and patience 34:53 Seeing anger directly 45:53 Closing reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4033
